불안함이었습니다. 남들보다 뒤처지지 않을까 하는 불안함, 주변의 기대에 부응하지 못할까 하
는 불안함, 내가 선택한 이 길이 맞는지에 대한 불안함을 늘 마음에 지니고 있었습니다. 그 불
안함은 저를 이른 아침부터 밤늦게까지 집중하도록 해주     는 고마운 존재이기도 했고, 어떤 때에

rr

AS 집어삼켜 아무것도 할 수 없도록 만드는 원망스러운 존재이기도 했습니다.

그런데 조금씩 준비가 잘 되어가고 있다는 자신감이 생기면서 불안감이 가득했던 마음은 조금
씩 기대감으로 채워졌습니다. 저에게 맞는 공부 방법을 하나씩 터득해 가고, 임용시험에 대해
조금씩 더 잘 이해하게 되면서 AS 어려운 시험이지만 이렇게 계속 최선을 다하면 합격할 수
WP se 자신감이 생겼습니다. 그때부터 저는 교육학과 전공 영어 SH} 단순히 합격을
위한 공부가 아닌 학교 현장에 BSD 수 있을까 고민하였고, 교사가 된 미래에 대한 기대감에
부풀기 시작했습니다. 그리고 그 기대감은 저를 합격으로 이끌어주

저의 불안함을 기대감으로 바꿔주었던 것이 바로 기출 분석이었                        학
습효율은비교할 수 없을 만큼 좋이겠습니다. 철저한 기출 BAS 동해 agate 2011
생각ㅎ the?  wee 8   엇인지 파악하고, 어떤 부분이 어떤 방식으로 다뤄질지, 어떤 답안이 S

음지 예상하며 공부할 수 있는 헤안을 얻었습니다. 지피지기면 백전백승.
eae 흔한 그 Be BA 체험했던 시간이었습니다.

하지만 임용시험에 함격했다는 이유만으로 기출 분석 교재를 세상에 내놓아도 괜찮음지에 대
해서는 고민이 앞섰습니다. 그래도 결국 결심했습니다. 합격자이기에, 수험생의 불안함이 무엇
인지 너무나도 잘 아는 합격자이기이, 철저히 수험생의 시선으로 공감하며 따라갈 수 있는 '함
격자의 비밀노트' 같은 기출 분석집을 만들어 보자고 말입니다.

본 교재가 수험생분들의 터닝 포인트가 되어주기를 바라는 마음으로 한 장 한 장 정성을 들여
집필했습니다. 이 서문을 읽고 계실 모든 수험생분들 마음속의 불안함이 이 교재와 함께하며
AS 기대감으로 바뀌기를, 그리고 그 기대감이 여러분들의 남은 수험 생활에 날개를 달아 합
격으로 이끌어주기를, 진심으로 응원합니다.

* 배점은 총 80점 중 기입형에 12점, 서술형에 68점을 부여함

진행되던 임용시험의 체계가 대폭 바뀌면서, 2014학년도에는 처음으로 기입형/서술형/논술형이 모두
출제되는 전공 시험이 실시되었다. 그래서인지 지금의 수험생들 입장에서는 비교적 간단하게 느껴질 수 있는
기본 개념들일지라도, 당시로서는 어렵게 느껴질 수밖에 없었을 것이다. A 시행이다 보니 치밀하게
설계되지 못한 문제들이 일부 눈에 떤다. 서술형은 비교적 쉽게 풀 수 있는 편이었으나 논란의 여지 없이
정확한 답이 존재하지 않는 기입형이 일부 있어 혼란을 자아낼 수 있었다.

## p.21

![p.21](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0021.jpg)

.떼가뱀전공 영머 기출곰략서 -

GET) 지론의 내용를 바랑으로 이유나 근거를 설명하기 _

보통 중심 내용과 연결 및 확장되어 전개되는 USS 구체적으로 이해했는지를 묻기 위한 문제 유형이다. ACHE
주제가 있다면, 이에 대한 구체적인 예시를 보여주고 그 예시에서 어떻게 ^가 적용되는지를 설명하라는 식의 문제이

ㆍ 거나 와 상반되거나 더 확장되는 부분에 대해 설명하라는 문제가 많다. 라는 주제에 대한 명확한 이해는 물론, 그
것이 어떤 방식으로 지문 내에서 구체화되고 확장되는지 이해하는 능력이 필요하다. 전반적인 내용 이해는 물론 모든
문단 속 세부적인 문장들의 의미와 논리적 연결고리를 가지고 있어야 한다. 이 역시 보통 Do NOT copy 조건이 함께

영어교육학의 대표 원서들은 중요한 개념을 많이 담고 있지만, 무턱대고 원서만 읽으면 체계적으로 정

리하기가 쉽지 않은 것이 사실입니다. 그래서 기본 개념 강의나 ILS 위해 정리된 교재를 통해 임용시험
에 맞게 요약되고 편집된 ABS 접하는 것으로 시작하는 것이 좋습니다. 이것이 체계성을 확보하는 측
면에서 유리합니다. 어느 정도 체계가 잡혔다면, 이제 대표 원서를 스스로 읽고 이해하는 과정을 반드시

고

쳐야 함니다. 영어교육학은 그 내용 자체가 이해하기 까다롭지는 않고, 학부에서 접해 익숙한 개념들
많기 때문에 속도감 있게 개념 학습을 진행할 수 있습니다. 원서를 읽는 것 자체가 개념을 잡는 데 도
되기 때문에 원서 강의를 수강하는 것보다는 스스로 읽는 것이 좋습니다. 스터디를 구성해 진도를
추고 함께 요약본을 공유하는 방식으로 원서를 읽어나가시는 것도 좋습니다. 영어교육학이란 어떤 것
고, 그중 임용에 다루어지는 개념은 어떤 것인지 감을 잡는 시기라고 할 수 있습니다.

@ 출제자가 의도한 답안은 무엇인가

SASS 창의적인 답안을 원하지 않습니다. 아무리 괜찮은 말이라도 담안에 꼭 포함해야 하는 키워드나 개념,
지문 속 근거가 없으면 좋은 점수를 받을 수 없습니다. 출제자의 의도에 맞게, 채점자의 채점기준에 맞는 담을 고
민하는 것이 기출 분석의 핵심 중 하나입니다. 처음 기출문제를 풀면서 바로바로 군더더기 없이 깔끔한 답안을 쓰
는 것은 매우 어려운 일입니다. 기출 분석을 하며 시간을 오래 들여 고민해서 답안을 새롭게 써보는 연습을 함께
진행합니다

위 세 가지를 반드시 확인하며 기출 분석을 진행하고, 이와 동시에 문제에서 다루고 있는 개념을 원서
에서 찾아보며 직접 정리하고 영어교육학 지식을 더 세세하고 정확하게 기억하도록 해야 합니다.

니다. 기출 BAS 통해 어떤 식으로 답안을 구성해야 하는지 파악했기에, 처음 만나는 문제를
드와 지문 내 근거가 잘 들어가게 담을 구성하는 ASS 해봅니다. 스터디원들과 PS 공유하
하면서도 키워드가 모두 들어가게 담안을 작성하는 방법을 연구해 보는 것이 좋습니다.

고, 어떻게 BAS 풀어나가야 하는지, 다루고 있는 개넘은 무엇인지를 워드로 다시 정리하면서 직접 분
문제는 나만의 시선으로 다시 분석을 해보고, 관련 개념을 눈으로 다시 한번 익협니다.
서 등 가지고 있는 교재를 총망라하여 GAs 작업을 진행합니다. 하나의 워드 파일에 영

## p.29

![p.29](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0029.jpg)

HA AS SH 기출 SE

과정을통한 '임용 영어학' 지식 체계화 + 영역별대표 원서 복습(7~8월)

기출문제에 접근하기 위한 선행작업이 완료되었으므로, 임용시험 대비에 있어 가장 중요한 부분인 기
출 분석 작업을 시작합니다. 이 과정어서 그동안 공부한 개념이 실제 임용시험 문항 속에서 구현되는 방
식에 대한 감을 잡을 수 있습니다. 특히 유형이 크게 변화하여 헌재의 서술형 기조를 따르게 된 2014학년
도 기출문항부터는 반드시 답안을 직접 적어보고 모범답안과 비교하여야 합니다. 이를 통해 그간 학습한
영어학적 지식이 문항 Bolo] 있어 어떤 방향으로 활용되고 적용되어야 하는지를 이해하고, 앞으로의 학
습 방향도 이에 맞춰 조정할 수 있습니다. 또한 최근 들어 점점 길어지고 있는 제시문 Soy 답안 작성
에 활용할 수 있는 부분을 골라내고, 문항에서 요구하고 있는 정보를 네 줄의 답안 속에 깔끔하게 구현하
는 방법에 대한 단서도 얻게 됩니다.

또한 기출문제 속에서 sa   은 분량이라도 다뤄진 적이 있는 개념이거나, FASE Uae 않
았더라도 기출된 개넘과 관련지어 이해할 수 있는 개념은 원서를 통해 꼼꼼히 다시 정리합니다. 이때 기
Bel 개념에 대한 암기가 부족하다는 것을 발견한다면 SE 이를 채워 나가야 합니다. 하지만 영어학
BHO] 있어 암기에만 에너지를 많이 쏟는 것은 효율적이지 않습니다. 어디까지나 해당 영어학적 지식을

활용하여 실제 언어 데이터 분석에 활용할 수 있는 능력에 집중하는 것이 필요합니다.

모의고사와 문제은행 풀이룰통한답안                                                          단권화(9월~시험직전)

이때부터는 시험이 임박하였기 때문에 학습 효율이 상당히 높아지는 것이 일반적입니다. 따라서 수험

글: Principles of Language Assessment

Principles                Explanation

- costs & time to construct and to administer the
test must be appropriate

Practicalil                              ae       .           .
시          - clear administrative details
- ease of scoring and interpreting the results
- consistent and dependable
- given to the same student, the test should have the
.                    same results
Reliability

- clear direction for scoring/evaluation
- clear rubrics
~ unambiguous test items

- the extent to which the test measures what it
intends to measure
Validity           - appropriate, meaningful, and useful in terms of
the purpose of the assessment
- has appropriate theoretical background

- correspondence of the features of a language test
task to the features of a target language task
- “real-world” connection
Authenticity        - natural language
contextualized
- meaningful, relevant topics
- thematic organization

~ the effect of the test on learning
Washback           - promotion and inhibition of learning
- formative test

a Washback effect

1. The effect of a test on the teaching and learning process

- The test results should give a useful diagnosis of strengths
and weaknesses
~ so that students can reflect it on their subsequent studying.

(after a test)

- It also includes the effect of test on prior teaching and
learning. (before a test)

2, How to make beneficial washback effect

- high content validity, reflect the test result, alternative
assessment (self-assessment, conferencing, learning journals,

글: Major Phonetic Classes

1. Noncontinuants and Continuants

Noncontinuants: stops, affricates

- Continuants: all other consonants, vowels

tS

. Obstruents and Sonorants

Obstruents: non-nasal stops, fricatives, affricates

Sonorants: vowels, nasal stops, liquids, glides

wo

. Consonantal Sounds

Consonantal: Consonants, but glides are excluded;

obstruents, nasal stops, liquids

Subclasses of consonantal sounds
~ Labials
fp], 0], br), [f], [v], fw]
~ Coronals
{0], [5], 띠, [a], fn], fs], [2], 다], (31, (tf, [a3], 0, fr]
+ Anteriors
(pl, 0], mJ, (£1, Lv], fw], [6], [6], (t], [0], fn], [5], [2]
> Sibilants
(s], (zl, [J], (31, [tf], [03]
4. Syllabic Sounds
- Vowels, liquids, nasals

- Obstruents and glides are never syllabic.

foot.

nw Aspirated Consonants

- Another dimension that differentiate /p, t, k/ from /b, d,
g/in English is the feature of aspiration.

- The voiceless set of stops is pronounced with aspiration
at the beginning of stressed syllables.
(8) pay [pe], take [tek], cab [k'eeb]

- This characteristic is not restricted to word-initial
position:
8) apart [epaat], attack [atk], occur [aks]
~ The aspirated stops are not word-initial, but in initial

positions of stressed syllables.

PART O1 기출편

- Voiceless stops may be produced with weak aspiration
in the following positions:
(a)in an unstressed syllable: polite [palait], vacuum
[vekjum]
(b) before syllabic consonant: pickle [p"1k1]
(c) if released in final position: sit [srt], sick [stk]

The syntactic function of dependents (i.e., complements

or adjuncts) influences oneweplac      if of nouns, too.
Note, for example, that a noun prince can be replaced
by one in (2a), but not in (2b). The difference lies in
what syntactic function the PP serves in each example.
The noun is modified by an adjunct in (2a), but by a

complement in (2b).

the professor teats the Korean language and the

qv Complement vs. Adjunct

1. Semantic nature

Complement                                     Adjunct

- More optional and more loosely
related to the head
- Additional information

- Complete the meaning of the
head

2. Ordering

- Complements will always be closer to their head Noun than Adjuncts.

3, Stacking

Complement                                     Adjunct

- Cannot be stacked                       - Can be stacked

4. Ordinary Coordination

- We can coordinate two XPs which are both Complements, and
likewise we can coordinate two XPs which are both Adjuncts.
- We cannot coordinate a Complement with an Adjunct.

a Complements and Adjuncts

1. Complements and Adjuncts
(1) (a) a student [of Physics] = Complement
(b) a student [with long hair] =Adjunct

2. Semantic difference
(2) (a) He is a student of Physics.
(0) He is studying Physics.

명시해 준다.

글: Intercultural competence

- SLA is intertwined with sociocultural identity.

- We have to be mindful of intercultural competence
while focusing on communicative competence.

- Raise sociocultural awareness of students

- How to initiate contact, anticipating cultural
misunderstanding

- Process-oriented tasks promote cross-cultural
adaptability.

- Culture capsules / culturgrams

Language, Culture, and Identity

1. Culture

- Culture is a way of life: the context within which we
exist, think, feel, and relate to others.

- Culture is the ideas, customs, skills, arts, and tools that
characterize a given group of people in a given period

of time.

2. Acculturation and Culture Shock

- L2 learning involves developing an identity.

- The creating of a new identity is at the heart of culture
learning (acculturation).

- For an L2 learner, understanding a new culture can
clash with a person’s worldview, self-identity, and
systems of thinking, acting, feeling, and communication
~ A learner may experience culture shock.

- Four successive stages of culture acquisition

(acculturation)

1. an initial period of excitement and euphoria

2. culture stress or culture sock, erosion of self-esteem and security

3. gradual recovery, adjustment to new ways of thinking, feeling,
and activity

4. 8 final stage of adaptation/integration, acceptance of a new
identity

wc VP Ellipsis

- The VP constituent in English has some syntactic properties
which are unique, not shared by any other type of
constituent.

- Ellipsis is a general term referring to the omission (or
deletion) of some element in a sentence which can be
understood from the immediate context.

- The type of ellipsis we are concerned with here, VP-Ellipsis,
is illustrated in (i):

(i) a. Max didn’t [help me with the dishes], but his brother
did        .

b. If Mary [gives buns to the elephant], then John will
too.

c. 1 could never have [formulated the argument] as clearly
as Susan has

d. Bill agreed to [kiss the Russian ambassador on both

cheeks], but Al wouldn’t

- As the name implies, the omitted elements in this

construction must constitute a VP.

features

12

Read the passage and follow the directions.

Nucleus positions in syllables are usually taken
by vowels. In the cases that syllables have no vowel,
consonants stand as the nucleus. It is usual to indicate
that a consonant is syllabic by means of a small vertical
mark (.) beneath or above the symbol. Even though
syllabic consonants are observed word-medially (e.g.,
Hungary [hangai]), most syllabic consonants are found
word-finally as in (1). Note that some words can be
realized in two phonetic forms. - <]의경무>

Major Phonetic Classes

1. Noncontinuants and Continuants
- Noncontinuants: stops, affricates

- Continuants: all other consonants, vowels

2. Obstruents and Sonorants

1

Obstruents: non-nasal stops, fricatives, affricates

Sonorants: vowels, nasal stops, liquids, glides

w

. Consonantal Sounds
- Consonantal: Consonants, but glides are excluded;
obstruents, nasal stops, liquids

Subclasses of consonantal sounds
~ Labials

## p.62

![p.62](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0062.jpg)

62

메가썸 기출 공략서 | 전공 영어

14

Read the passage in <A> and the conversation in
<B>, and follow the directions.

Conversation is co-constructed by two or more people,

unfolding dynamically in real time. For conversational
discourse to be successful, the participants have to know
how to organize the events in it; that is, they need to
achieve cohesion. A cohesive relation is one in which
the interpretation of one element in the discourse
presupposes, and is dependent upon, another. In English,
along with the

as<reference, substitution, ellipsis, and conjunction,
cohesion>in conversation can also be achieved using
xical cohesive devices.

§ by which links are made

The use of lexical
cohesion is an indicator of topic consistency, and hence
contributes significantly to the sense that speakers are
talking to topic, and the talk, therefore, becomes more
coherent.                                ,

(Two friends are having a conversation in the wallpaper
aisle at a hardware store.)
repetition
$1: Isn’t it funny that wallpaper is in fashion again?
$2: Yeah, I thought it was gone forever.
$1: Me, too. So, you are redoing your kitéH@ti? whole
$2: Yup. And I want to use one of these.
511 Good idea. (pointing to a roll of wallpaper)\How about
that?
$2: You mean the one on the top shelf?
$1: Yeah, do you like it?
$2: Uh-huh. It will go with my ditt
$1: Have you been to Lesley’s new office?

$2: I have, actually. It was huge and everything was so

well organized.
$1: Yeah. And she had the same wallpaper.
$2: Oh, that’s right. I remember that.

Note: S = speaker

hould be rigorous, clearly

stated, and historically informed. It should neither
 (as current fashion criticism often does)

nor be unnecessarily gbscitre (as current art criticism
often is). It should look for vitality and boldness, and
distinguish the original from the derivative. It should
track a designer’s development — or point out standstill
or regression — and attempt to figure out what led
the designer to make these specific aesthetic choices,
elaborate on the techniques and materials that have
been used — and finally pass judgment. As already
mentioned, a proper judgment is something more than

mere opinion — it is a reasoned or justified opinion

Lwhich aims for broader validity. [Criticism can never

crificismO| 갖추어야하는 것들

be completely Objective,

al Which is why writing
criticism necessarily means exposing yourself. Writing
criticism is about struggling to come to terms with what
you do not yet know exactly how to deal with, to pass
judgment, to expose yourself, knowing that you expose
yourself, putting your prestige and your very identity at
risk. As Pierre Bourdieu formulated it: “®
an                            f.” This is a truth that holds
for all of us, but it is especially acute in the case of the

critic, whose judgment is at the greatest public scrutiny.

[ What about               between critics? They

. It is never a good sign when too
many people are in agreement about too much. It almost
always means that we are thinking too little. There
should be disagreements between critics. Two critics

can certainly disagree about the relative merits of two
designers, such as for instance Alexandre Herchcovitch

and Phoebe Philo. one should also note that

there will usually be a high degree of convergence

between critics in their judgments. One might prefer

Herchcovitch and the other Philo, and even find a
certain collection plain tasteless, but it would be highly
surprising if one of them argued that Herchcovitch or
Philo is a designer with virtually no aesthetic merit.

Would a serious critic pass such a judgment? There will

What did a presidential administration mean when
they proposed “revenue enhancement” through “user
fees”? Translated, “revenue enhancement” through
“user fees” is taxes paid by citizens. This is an example

speak. Doublespeak is carefully designed and

~ doublespeak9l 정위     :              ;
constructed language used to Take things seem different

from what they are, and it comes in <éveral form®

One form of doublespeak iste

Inflated language is designed to make the ordinary seem

extraordinary. Cheap material used to make a purse
could be described as “genuine imitation leather” or a
glass stone in a piece flweiry as a “real countertet
diamond.” These types of descriptions can also be  |

used to describe situations. A guide telling a group of
French tourists that their visit to the Eifel Tower is a

“once in a lifetime opportunity” is almost certainly
using doublespeak, especially as the attraction is not

far from home. This type of language gives a perception

of importance to situations and things that would not

normally be considered ipod tublesreak 1 inflated language

Another form of doublespeak [의

Jargon as
doublespeak occurs when professional language is used

with people not “in-the-know.” The use of jargon can be

an efficient way of communicating within a specialized
group. But when used with those not in-the-know, the
intention may be to give an air of profundity, authority,

and prestige. If a computer technician tells a novice
computer user with an internet connection problem to
“power-cycle” their router rather than saying “you need
to Unplug the router and plug it back in,” the technician
might be using doublespeak. — doublespeak 'I! 2: jargon
Doublespeak is language used to make the bad seem
good and the basic profound. As shown in the examples
above, it is present in many aspects of our lives. Keeping
awareness of the definition and forms of doublespeak

can help us discriminate between what someone wants

us to believe and the reality.

+ Writing Activities — Controlled to Free Writing

1. Imitative writing: Dictation (beginning level)

- Ss will simply ‘write down’ or ‘imitate’ English letters,
words, and possibly sentences in order to learn the
conventions or the orthographic code.

PART 01 기출편

( 관련기출

- Steps

a. T reads a short paragraph once/twice at normal speed

b. T reads the paraphrase in short phrase units(three or
four words each) with pauses

c. During the pause — Ss write exactly what they hear

d.T than reads the whole paragraph once more at
normal speed — Ss check their writing

e. Scoring (#= punctuation 2S 문법성에 초점)

N

. Controlled Writing

Ss practice writing focusing on certain grammatical

items or vocabulary

.8J If the students learned about ‘past tense,’ they can
involve in a controlled writing activity where they
should change all the present tense verbs into past

tense ones.

3. Guided Writing

Questions and answer, sentence combine, and dicto-

composition can fall into this category.

Dicto-Composition:

a. A paragraph is read at normal speed, usually two or
three times.

b. T asks Ss to rewrite the paragraph to the best of their
recollection of the reading.

c.In one of several variations of the dicto-comp
technique, the teacher, after reading the passage, puts
key words from the paragraph on the chalkboard as
cues for the Ss.

-_

. Free Writing

Ss involve in free writing related with their real life

outside the classroom.

8.) Dialogue journals:

«Enable Ss and T to interact on a one-to-one basis at any
level and in any learning context.

* The writing is not corrected, graded, or evaluated.

«T should keep the writing private, promising not to
show the journal to a third party.

주제
instruction that has been proposed to develop learners’

‘iy is one of the approaches to 12

fluency and accuracy. It occurs when learners briefly
pay attention to linguistic items within a larger meaning-
focused context. Focus on form can he accomplished
in various ways. A basic distinction is drawn between

‘reactive focus on form’ (where attention to form arises

Sut of some problem in a participant’s production as in
A1 and A2 below) and ‘pre-emptive focus on form’ (where

the participants make a particular form the topic of the
conversation even though no actual problem has arisen
as in B1 and B2 below).

responds to a student’s error by
directly indicating that an error
has been made, e.g., by formally
correcting the error or by using

PART 01 기출편

qv Focus on Form

1.

Definition

Leads students to engage in incidental learning by
drawing their attention te target forms while they are
engaged in communicative activities

Emphasizes the learners’ noticing their grammatical
knowledge, which is necessary for successful target
language use

Important in Task-based approach

Focus on Form occurs reactively when it is needed
during or after doing the task

. Noticing and Consciousness-raising

Noticing: “The process of the learner picking out
specific features of the target language input which
she or he hears or reads, and paying conscious
attention to them so that they can be fed into the
learning process”(Cullen, 2012)
Consciousness-raising: helps student notice and pay
attention to the gaps between their utterances and the
correct forms

Gives corrective feedback with further explanation,

exemplification, and follow up practice as requires

. Advantages

More similar to natural language acquisition or
interlanguage development

Students are not overwhelmed by the grammatical
explanations.

Intrigues more intrinsic motivation

Categories of Feedback

- implicit
Recast               - reformulates an ill-formed or incomplete utterance
- in an unobtrusive way
Clarification       - indicates the hearer has not understood it
request            - attention is drawn to an utterance
Metalinguistic “- provides comments, information, or questions
feedback              related to the correctness of students’ utterances
~          - prompts the learner to self-correct
Elicitation                             .        .
- more overt in their request for a response
Explicit            - clear indication that the form is incorrect
correction         - provides a corrected form
te             - repeats the ill-formed part of the student’s utterance
Repetition

usually with a change in intonation

jo

@ 배경지식과 주어진 예시를 통해 <&>의 (1)에서 설명하는 7개의
morphological process 이해한다.

a. blending: 복수의 단어에서 각각 일부
운 단어를 형성하는 과정이다,

b. clipping: 긴 단어에서 일부분을 삭제하여 준말을 만든

c.compounding: 기존에 존재하는 단어들을 합쳐 하나의 합성어를
만드는 과정이다.

d.conversion: 본래 가지고 있던 품사의 의미가 확장되어 다른 품사
로도 쓰이는 경우를 말한다.

6. derivation: 접사를 붙여서 파생어를 만드는 과정이다.

6 inflection: 동사에 문법적 의미를 더하는 접사 -ing, -en, -ed, -s
등을 붙이는 것을 말한다.

g.initialism: 단어들의 머리글자를 떼어 새로운 단어를
이다. 이러한 과정을 통해 민
이라고 한다.

a postposition type (e.g., Korean). With the preposition
type, we find that a preposition head precedes its
complement as in (1a). As for the postposition type, a
postposition follows its complement as in (1b).

## p.81

![p.81](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0081.jpg)

39

글 Cross-Categorical Structural Symmetry

- There is broad structural symmetry across categories
insofar as all major categories (e.g., N, V, P, A, Adv)
permit two types of phrasal expansion.

- More particularly, any head lexical category X can
be expanded into an X-bar by the addition of a set
of Complements; and X-bar can be expanded into
X-double-bar by the addition of a Specifier/Determiner.

- Thus, the ‘basic’ structure of Phrases is of the schematic

form below:
XP
>
Specifier                         x
x                      Complement

PART O1 7/24

Syllables. Feet seem to have many different structures
in English. For example, there are feet composed of a
single stressed syllable (e.g., {‘son}) and feet where a

stressed syllable is followed by one stressless syllable

(eg., {mother}) or by two or more stressless syllables (e.g.,

(c&n8a8p. Sometimes, a strong syllable appears in the

second (e.g., {d&mSna}, {ben8n§}).

Rhyme of a Light Syllable                Rhyme of a Heavy Syllable
short vowel +coda

short vowel                                       long vowel (+coda)
diphthong (+coda)

- Syllable weight is an important factor in stress
assignment in languages. The weight of a syllable is
determined by its rhyme structure.

- Heavy syllables attract stress, and no stressed syllable

may be light.

’ involves the preposing of a
locative phrase before the verb and the postposing of the
subject after the verb, as illustrated below.

(1) a. John taiifinto the housd

b. Into the house ran John.           run > S410
tL ™

(2) a. A woman &t66a[on the corney,.

b. On the corner stood a woman.          stand > SAIO

Note that (1b) can be derived from (1a) by moving the
locative phrase into the house to the front of the sentence
and by inverting the verb ran and the subject John.
Likewise, we can derive (2b) from (2a). Meanwhile, there
is some restriction with ‘locative inversion.’ Consider the
following examples.

(3) a. My friend Mary seated my mother among the

guests of honor.   seat > AIX

. “Among the guests of honor seated my mother my
friend Mary.

. “Among the guests of honor seated my friend

Mary my mother.

. The women di     “d beautiful dances around the

fire.         dance > SAIX

. “Around the fire danced beautiful dances the
women.

. “Around the fire danced the women beautiful

dances.

All the examples above clearly show that the basic verb
types affect the grammaticality of ‘locative inversion.’

## p.85

![p.85](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0085.jpg)

In <B>, (i) is grammatical, while (ii) is ungrammatical.

grammaticalityS state

(i) is grammatical because the intransitive verb
verb typeO! 무엇이며,해당

“come(came)” allows locative inversion. On the other

typeI locative inversionS 허용하는지를 SHOR 설명

hand, (ii) is ungrammatical because the transitive

verb “live(lived)” does not allow locative inversion.

ale        글
Pi Inversions
- Moving element into a position of prominence at the
beginning of a sentence

1. Lexical inversions
- Triggered by the presence of a particular word
- Locative adverbs: here / there
(1) a. Here comes the train!
b. There goes another car!
- Negative words: never, seldom, rarely, only
(2) a. Never have I seen such a nice show.
b. Only with a bank loan will they be able to live.
- Not only, neither, nor, so, as
(3) Not only was I exhausted, but also I frankly just
couldn’t stand the humidity there.

2. Stylistic inversions
- The deletion of if in hypothetical and counterfactual
conditional sentences
(4) a. Were I to live another 20 years, I would be 45.
b. Should I leave for only a second, I would be fired.

3. Information packaging inversions
- Information packaging inversions “package” the
information in the sentence in a way more appropriate

to the discourse context.

PART 01 기출편

2021 학년도

In an attempt to better understand language
development, a three-tiered approach has been
proposed, encompassing the following components for
investigating production changes:           ity,        cy,

and fiieiéy. [Complexity| generally refers to the lexical
variety and syntactic elaborateness of the learner’s

linguistic system. [Accuracy] involves the correct use of

the target language, while [fluency] concerns a focus on
meaning, automatization, and real-time processing.

or spoken language skill (i.e., performance) as well as to

3 can be applied to appraise written

assess the state of the linguistic knowledge that supports

Teacher’s Log

In order to evaluate the progress of their speaking
ability, I usually have my students read a story and then
tell about it in their own words. It’s not easy to measure
all aspects of their speech at once. On the part of the
students, it’s also not easy to focus on more than one
aspect simultaneously. So, I usually give my students
two presentation opportunities and ask them to pay
more attention to one aspect over the others in each
presentation session. In the ff                      5
I focus on hi      i

real-time processi                                        Meaning
delivered. To that end, T evaluate students’ presentations

1, I record and transcribe the students’ oral

performance for a closer look. At this point, the
presentation is evaluated especially by calculating thé

lexical variety

         3 and tallying
syntactic elaborateness

Teaching Speaking Issues

1. Accuracy vs. Fluency

Accuracy                     Fluency

- clear                        - flowing

- articulate                     - natural

- grammatically correct          - automatization

- phonologically correct                    real-time processing

- 1970s, too much focus on meaningful language
involvement & de-emphasis on grammar and phonology
> led to fluent but not comprehensible output

2. Complexity
(*여기서의 complexity 2]Zo44] learner2| 발화 complexity2} 다
= 개념이다. 0681. communication tasks2] complexity 대한 설명이
니 참고할 것)

- Grammatical and lexical complexity

- Task design

~ cognitive, strategic, and interpersonal complexity

3. Affective Factor

- It is important to overcome anxiety generated from
the worries that you might sound ignorant, stupid, or
embarrassing.

- Language identity: “You are what you say.”
~ People avoid speaking when they can be judged by

others.

- Teacher should provide warm and non-judging
environment so that students can try speaking without
anxiety.

4. Intelligibility

- Most interactions in English are among nonnative
speakers.

- The norm that intelligibility should be gauged by
whether nonnative speakers are intelligible to native
speakers are considered ‘arrogant’ now.

- New standards of “correctness” / new attitudes on
“accent”

> meet the global realities

PART O1 기출편

2009학년도 16번

@* use strategies to open and close conversations.

(Written Language Skills]

read a short text about a familiar topic and write a

read a short text about a familiar topic and
organize the content.
view an object or picture and write their thoughts

Teaching Procedure

Mr. Yang designed a reading lesson for his 2nd year
students based on two of the achievement standards that
he set out to accomplish this semester.

Sumi’s Worries

Sumi thought Kate was her best friend, but
now, she feels that Kate has changed and that she
is avoiding her. A few days ago, Sumi met Kate
in the hallway at school, but Kate turned around
and walked away from her. Sumi tried to find the
reason, but she couldn’t think of anything wrong
she had done to Kate. So, Sumi asked for her older
sister’s advice. Sumi’s sister suggested that she
simply ask Kate what’s wrong.

nonproficient second language speakers, native speakers
KAajistiients in the flow
of conversation. These include slow speech rate, loud

tend to show £6

speech, long pauses, simple vocabulary (e.g., few idioms,

high-frequency words), and paucity of slang. They also
tend to make adjustments to their speech [in the area
‘of grammail. They often move topics to the front of the
sentence, put new information at the end of the sentence,

use fewer contractions and pronouns, grammatically

repeat non-native speakers’ incorrect utterances, and fill

in the blank for their incomplete utterances.

‘them’, respectively. He tries not to use a contraction

그것이 대화에서 어떤 7|능을 하는지 채점 ㅋ|워드가 될 만한 단어를 활용하여 설명
utterance becomes comprehensible input to the NNS

and there occurs meaning negotiation. As a result, the

communication breakdown is resolved.

y~ Meaning negotiation strategies

1. Comprehension checks (네가 잘 이해했는가?)

- The moves by which one interlocutor seeks to make
sure that the other has understood correctly
 “You know what I mean?” “Do you understand?”

2. Clarification requests

- The moves by which one interlocutor requests
assistance in understanding the other’s preceding
utterance
 “Could you repeat what you said?” “’m sorry?”

3. Confirmation checks (내가 잘 이해했는가)

- The moves used by one interlocutor to confirm whether
he or she correctly has understood what the other has
said
 “Oh, do you mean that ~?” “You mean ~, right?”

글: Meaning negotiation

- Long’s Interaction Hypothesis: stress the importance of
corrective feedback during interaction — interlocutors
must negotiate for meaning — language development
- Uptake: an interlocutor’s immediate response to his or

her partner’s signal of noncomprehension
+ Interlocutor uses a variety of communication
strategies (message abandonment, topic change,
circumlocution, word coinage ...). (2015학년도 ASA

서술형 2번)

Read the passage and follow the directions.

“Palette” is a homophone for the term ‘fpalate|”
signifying both the roof of the mouth and the sense of

taste. While the Galett® is both the board on which the

paint is placed and the paint itself, thé alat©is only
juxtaposed to the canvas for gustatory compositions,

unless one considers that the palate~ as in the sense of

taste—is the tongue which generates flavor by crushing

the food mass and the taste buds on to the hard roof of

the mouth, a rich analogue to the application of paint to

canvas. Receptor célls on the tongue are responsible for

sensing particular flavors, the primary ones being bitter,

sweet, salt, and sour. The stimuli from these cells in

combination with saliva and the olfactory sense generate

taste. Just as the primary hues generate all others in

the color spectrum, the four taste cells blend to create

all flavors. Thus the ‘pala

idéavors. Crushing the tongue against the roof

of the mouth drives the food mass into the taste buds,

and this clamping or sucking motion intensifies and
prolongs the flavor.

However, taste also requires the                    [에

which must recognize, evaluate, and
categorize the stimuli from the mouth and nasal

passages. The evaluative quality of taste or the

recognition and judgment of flavor can be processed only

in the brain, and, as with the visual imagery of painting,

HIS >                                       .               .
the quality of taste is assessed in relationship to a history

of tastes, and the pleasure or revulsion generated relates

in large part to the familiarity of the flavor. The tasting

subject frequently judges the outcome of the experience

in opposition to or alliance with previous experiences of

the same or similar tastes. This process might be defined

as the “semiotics” of taste.

A @orpiis is a collection of texts of written or spoken
language from various sources presented in electronic
form. It provides evidence of how language is used in
real situations, from which lexicographers can analyze
millions of examples of each word to see how real
language behaves. Many contemporary dictionaries,
therefore, incorporate the features derived from the
analyses of corpus data, some of which are shown below.

statistical data on how often words are

used in the language

information on what other words
commonly occur with the word in focus

iteXt: information on which particular field (e.g.,

users of the language actually write or say in books,
newspapers, speeches, or recorded conversations,

- Collection of texts of written or spoken language

a Strategies for Teaching Vocabulary
1. Allocate specific class time to vocabulary learning.
2. Help students to learn vocabulary in context.
- Data from linguistic corpora can show real-world
language.
3. Engage in “unplanned” vocabulary teaching.
4. Encourage students to develop word-learning strategies.

find scoring students’ writing quite challenging.

T1: What makes you say that?

T2: 1 rated my students’ writing assignments last night.

But when I look at them today, I feel I would give

different scores.

it. I just put them away until the

and check the last few essays you’ve marked to see
that yow’re still following the rating criteria?

: Exactly.

: Thanks for your advice.

## p.97

![p.97](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0097.jpg)

a Principles of Language Assessment

Principles

Explanation

Practicality

- costs & time to construct and to administer
the test must be appropriate

- clear administrative details

- ease of scoring and interpreting the results

Reliability

- consistent and dependable

- given to the same student, the test should
have the same results

- clear direction for scoring/evaluation

- clear rubrics

- unambiguous test items

Validity

- the extent to which the test measures what it
intends to measure

- appropriate, meaningful, and useful in terms
of the purpose of the assessment

- has appropriate theoretical background

Authenticity

- correspondence of the features of a language
test task to the features of a target language
task

- “real-world” connection

- natural language

- contextualized

meaningful, relevant topics

- thematic organization

Washback

the effect of the test on learning
promotion and inhibition of learning
- formative test

1

ax Reliability

Category

Explanation

Student-related
reliability

- physical or psychological factors
- test-wiseness of students (test-taking strategies)

Rater reliability

- inter-rater reliability: when two or more
scorers yield consistent scores

- intra-rater reliability: when one scorer yields
consistent scores / fatigue, bias X

Test administration

- street noise, audio player ...
administration environment of test must be

reliabilit                   .
ty             consistent
~ Test itself can cause errors.
.         - Multiple-choice item must be carefull

b. low               000]

a Morphemes: The Minimal Units of Meaning
- The linguistic term for the most elemental unit of
grammatical form is morpheme.
- A single word may be composed of one or more

morphemes:

boy

one                                     desire
meditate
boy+ish

two                               desire+able
meditate+tion
boytish+ness

three                                      y                 .
desiret+abletity
gentle+man+li+ness

four                                                 .                 ,
un+desire+abletity

untgentletman+li+ness

more than four                on            ;                  ;        .
anti+dis+establish+menttari+an+ism

Bound Morpheme                           Free Morpheme
- must be attached to a base           - can stand alone
morpheme = affixes                       (e.g.
                                            boy, finger, girl, buy, stand, cup,

To account for some syntactic phenomena in English,

we can resort to phrasal categories such as VP, TP, CP, and
so on. First, let us assume that only constituents which

belong to the same phrasal category can be coordinated.

For example, NP can conjoin with another NP, but not
with AP.

(1) a. The student or the teacher NP, NP (0)
b. *The student or very pretty NP, AP (x)

The same restriction also holds true with clausal
structures. The bracketed structures in (2a) and (2b) are
clearly different, since they cannot be coordinated by the
conjunction or, as shown in (2c).

(2) a. We didn’t intend [you to hurt him].

b. We didn’t intend [for him to hurt youl.

의 *We didn’t intend [you to hurt him] or [for him to
hurt you].                         7                          cP
So, based on the phrasal category and the assumption
about coordination, we can explain the ungrammaticality
of the sentences such as (2c).

Second, the restriction on wh-cleft sentences can also
be attributed to phrasal categories.

(3) What I'll do is [ppostpone the meeting].

VP can be in the focus position (the position after be

verb) of wh-cleft sentences like (3). Consider further the
following examples.

(4) a. Bill promised [,p@ PRO to behave himself].
b. What Bill promised was [200 PRO to behave
himself].

(5) a. They believe [phim to be innocent].
b. *What they believe is [,phim to be innocent].

The verb promise in (4a) is known to take a CP
(Complementizer. Phrase) complement which is headed
by a null complementizer @, and we find that CP can
be in the focus position, as shown in (4b). Meanwhile,

in (58), the verb believe requires a TP (Tense Phrase)
complement, and from the ungrammaticality of (5b), it is
clear that TP cannot be in the focus position of a wh-cleft

100    메가썸 기출 공략서 ㅣ 전공 영어

sentence. Thus, based on the types of phrasal categorics,

we can explain the restriction on wh-cleft sentences.

Note 1: indicates the ungrammaticality of the sentence.
Note 2: ‘PRO’ is a null pronoun which represents the

understood subject of some infinitive clauses.

She argued persuasively or that their offer should
be rejected.

They offered us a choice of red wine, white wine, or
beer.

What he claims is that he was insulted.

What we hadn’t intended was you to get hurt.

Teacher’s Reflection

This semester I have been using a @H@€KIlst in
my English writing class to help my students revise
their drafts by themselves. The checklist I provide

for my students covers the following areas: content,
organization, grammar, vocabulary, and mechanics.

Below is a part of the checklist.

Areas                        Indicators

[= > ㅋㅋ

I use correct subject and

(4) grammar    verb agreement.

I use verb tense correctly.

I put a period at the end of
every sentence.

(2) mechanics

10896 capital letters correctly.
I spell the words correctly.

ae   J

At first, the checklist didn’t seem feasible because

there was little improvement,  especially in organization

in writing. To find the reason, I held{g
with the students and discovered that the indicators

cause
for organization were too complicated for them to

understand. Some of them included more fhan an. 7

aspect to check simultaneously. So, I jdivided those
indicators] into two or three separate sentences so that

one indicator assesses only one aspect. Since the revision

of the indicators, the students’ organization has gotten

much better.

However, some students still had problems oping, the

checklist appropriately. So, I ran a couple ofjtrainin
u ution 2-2    y                                P       fraining
sessions] to teach the students what the indicators meant

and how they should be utilized. First, we read the

indicators and I asked if they made sense. Then, I had
them practice checking particular errors with a sample
paragraph I had prepared. Since the training sessions,
the students have been making significantly fewer
errors. Overall, the use of the checklist has worked well
in the revision process.

Some studies claim that there is a predictable language
development. For instance, the following is one way
of understanding &

i, which posits six stages, each with some

a)

prominent features.

Stage                         Key Feature                                     Example

1     * Rising intonation on word or Airplane?
phrase

2       * Rising intonation with a                You like this?

declarative word order

3       * Fronting (e.g., do-fronting,
wh-fronting, other fronting)

Where the train is going?
Is the boy has a dog?

4     * Inversion in wh-questions with Where is the book?
a copula be
*No inversion in wh-questions Where Ican draw them?
with auxiliaries
*Yes/no questions with auxiliaries Can he catch the ball?
such as can and will

5     «Inversion in wh-questions with How can she solve it?
both an auxiliary and a copula
be

6       * Complex questions (e.g., tag
questions, embedded questions)

She’s pretty, isn’t she?
Can you tell me where
he is?

The information about the sequences in English
language acquisition like the above is mostly from child

native speakers. Familiarity with them can help EFL

teachers estimate their students’ level of development,

which in turn can help determine realistic goals for

language instruction.

qi< Interlanguage

- The separateness of an L2 learner’s system, intermediate
status between native and target languages

- As comprehension is difficult to objectively measure,
interlanguage research relies heavily on production of

learners.

# Stages of learner language development

1. Presystematic stage
- Anumber of random errors

- Frequently incomprehensible

PART 01 기출편

2.Emergent stage

- More consistent

- Internalized some rules, words, phrases

- The hearer can understand the intended meanings.

- Still unable to correct errors when they are pointed out
by another

- Backsliding: A learner seems to internalize a rule
or principle, and then regress to a previous stage.
(U-shaped learning)

3. Systematic stage
- More consistency
- Ability to correct the errors

4. Postsystematic stage

~ Stabilization

- Few errors, great fluency

- Ability to self-correct

- Fossilization: relatively permanent incorporation of
incorrect linguistic forms into one’s second language

competence

2011학년도 28번

## p.106

![p.106](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0106.jpg)

16

Read the teacher’s note in <A> and the lesson plan

in <B>, and follow the directions.

Teacher’s Note

Last week, I attended a teacher training workshop on
intercultural education. In the workshop, the trainer
defined @ulttiré as the beliefs, way of life, art, and
customs that are shared and accepted by people in a
particular society. She also explained that understanding
another culture involves constructing a new frame of
reference in terms of the people who created it. I totally
agree with her. I believe that in order to help my students
develop iteretilttiralcompeterice, | need to have them
understand [their own frame of reference as well as the
target culture’s. i also think that it is necessary to utilize

various materials to arouse students’ interests. Below is

the list of instructional techniques that the trainer taught
us in the workshop.                                          ㅣ

를 Artifact study It is designed to help students discern

the cultural significance of certain unfamiliar objects

from the target culture. The activity involves students
in giving descriptions and forming hypotheses about

the function of the unknown object.

Cuiturecapsile! [t is a brief description, usually
one or two paragraphs, of some aspect of the target
culture, followed by or incorporated with contrasting

information about the students’ native culture. Culture

capsules can be written by teachers or students.

classroom where posters, maps, objects, and pictures

ff A culture island is an area in the

of people, lifestyles, or customs of other cultures
are displayed to attract learners’ attention, evoke

comments, and help students develop a mental

image.

Nativeinforiviant? Native informants can be valuable
resources to the classroom teacher, both as sources of
current information about the target culture and as
linguistic models for students. Students can develop
a set of questions they would like to ask before native
speakers come to the class.

106      메가썸 기출 공략서 | 전공 영어

Lesson Plan

Unit 7 Hello From Around The World

10th out of 12 sessions
Greeting customs in the UK

To teach about the ways in which people greet

each other in the UK and how they are different
from those in Korea

Preparation

Decorate |the culture board|in the English classroom

with pictures and posters which illustrate the greeting
= culture island

customs of the UK.

Lesson Steps
1. Have the students check out the culture board and tell
what they think about the pictures and posters.
. Read aloud about greeting customs in

the UK, which is prepared in advance, and have|the
brief description

students take notes.
. Divide the students into small groups to compare
their notes. Then, have them discuss and write the
similarities and differences between Korea and the

UK regarding the greeting customs. = culture capsule

. Have the students imagine situations in which people
from the two cultures meet. Ask them to write a
conversation script based on the situation and to
perform role-plays.

Based on <A>, identify TWO instructional
techniques that the teacher implements in the
lesson plan, with corresponding evidence from
<B>.

Read the passage in <A> and the sample items in

Ms. Kang, a new high school English teacher, was
assigned to create questions for the listening section of
the semester’s final exam. In order to make the most
effective test items, she goes over her notes from her
college assessment class and finds the following:

<Item Techniques>

세 transferring aural information
to a visual representation
nh writing down parts of what you

hear while listening to a passage
i choosing the correct paraphrase
from 3-5 distractors

language with oral repetition
answering a question with a word ora
short phrase without given choices

Looking at her notes, she remembers that each of
these techniques has its own strengths. For example,
the sentence paraphrase technique has high practicality
because it is easy to grade. Other techniques, such as_
information transfer, partial dictation, and sentence

repetition, work well for assessing students’ listening

ability in a more integrative way. Ms. Kang thinks that'l

she will utilize some of these techniques because she
wants to test her students’ listening and other language-

skills simultaneously. Ms. Kang also thinks her students —

should be able to understand specific details, which

is one of her main goals for the class this semester.

So, she wants to test this particular ability in the final
2
exam. While all the techniques in her notes are good }

for assessing the ability to find specific information,

Ms. Kang thinks the sentence repetition technique may

not be appropriate since it may only require students to-
simply repeat what they hear.

Below are two sample items made by Ms. Kang.

@ Listen to the information about Minsu’s daily schedule
and fi        i

The information will be given twice.

ith the correct information.

We can find many geographic regions in Korea. The

and                      parts of the country

part of the country.

country.

PART 01 기출편

qo Assessing Listening

1. Four types of listening performance

- Intensive: listening for perception of the components
(phonemes, words, intonations, etc.)

- Responsive: listening to a relatively short stretch of
language (a greeting, question, command, comprehension
check, etc.) in order to make an equally short response

- Selective: scanning for certain information / listening
for comprehending designated information in a context
of longer stretches of spoken language

- Extensive: listening to develop a top-down, global
understanding of spoken language

2. Intensive listening
- Paraphrase Recognition: asking the test-taker to choose
the correct paraphrase from a number of choices

ESI “sentence paraphrase”)

<Hello, my name’s Juan. I come from Spain.>

A. Juan feels good in Spain.
B. Juan wants to come to Spain.
C. Juan is Spanish.
D. Juan likes es Spain.

3. Responsive listening

(제시문의 “short answer”)

Q. How long did it take to do your homework?
A.
4. Selective listening
- Listening cloze (제시문의 “partial dictation”)
~ Listening cloze(cloze dictations, partial dictations)
requires the test-taker to listen to a story, monologue,
or conversation and simultaneously read the written
text in which selected words or phrases have been
deleted.
- Information transfer
> Aurally processed information must be transferred to
a visual representation, such as labeling a diagram,
identifying an element in a picture, completing a

form, or showing routes on a map.

2021 학년도             109

19

Read the passage and follow the directions.

In the classroom today, there are a growing number
of children who are being raised outside their parents’

home cultures after moving across geographic boundaries
because of their parents’ employment. Children in this

situation are called {Phi
categorized as a TCK, a child must spend more than
three years outside their passport country during the

developmental years, from birth to eighteen years

네 문단으로 나누어진 긴 지문을 하나의 문단으로 요약하는 문제로, 각

요약하여 네 문장의 단락을 완성하면 된다. 하고

old. Living as a TCK can provide |bénefits| as well as
undeniable |challé:
Growing up internationally, TCKs are able to become

speakers of multiple languages. Learning a language can

be easier if you have daily exposure to it. TCKs attend
schools taught in the target language and encounter the
language in everyday life. This can help them learn the
language more quickly, especially during their formative

years. Depending on how much time is spent in a
country, the level of fluency can vary, but it would not
be unlikely for a TCK to be capable of speaking three or
more languages by the end of high school. <benefit>
Despite this benefit, there is an undeniable challenge
that comes along with being a TCK. The multiple shifts
in location can lead to a sense of isolation for these
children. Having to repeat the process of moving and
adjusting to new life circumstances can take a serious

toll. Such changes can prevent children from maintaining

relationships with those outside their immediate family.
Hence, jisolation| becomes the most common challenge
for TCKs. <challenge>

Given the modern context in which the migration and
transnational flow is increasing, understanding TCKs’
lives will be a valuable resource for international and

global education. It is important that educators support
this growing community by maximizing the potential

benefits of cross-cultural experiences while helping them
navigate the challenges that arise.

Write a summary following the guidelines below.

* Provide a topic sentence, two supporting ideas, and a

concluding sentence based on the passage.

## p.113

![p.113](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0113.jpg)

모범답안

Third Culture Kids(TCKs), the children who have lived
abroad for more than three years, have both benefits
and challenges. As for the benefit, TCKs are likely to
speak multiple languages as they are exposed to the
target language in their daily lives. However, a lot of
TCKs feel a sense of isolation, as the change of
circumstances makes it hard to keep the relationships
outside the family. It is important to understand both
sides of TCKs so that we can help them to maximize

the benefits and to overcome the challenges.

Read the passage in <A> and the teacher’s journal
in <B>, and follow the directions.

Vocabulary is a core component of language
knowledge and provides much of the basis for how
well learners listen, speak, read, and write. Without
extensive knowledge of vocabulary or diverse strategies
for acquiring new words, learners are often unable to
produce as much language as they would like.

Knowing a word does not simply mean knowing its

surface meaning. Rather, it involves knowing diverse
aspects of lexical knowledge in @éptW including:

phonological and morphological forms and syntactic and
semantic structures. Therefore, activities that integrate
lexical knowledge of form, meaning, and use should be
included in class.

Teacher’s Journal

= knowing surface meaning

and discussed how we can improve the way we
teach vocabulary. We |realized that we have been

heavily focused on [expanding the size of our students’

vocabulary. As a result, they seem to know a lot of words
but do not understand or use them properly in context}

believe help our students develop asf
vocabulary knowledge across form, meaning,jand use.

Vocabulary activities to be implemented:

Trying to pronounce the target words by listening to a
recorded text

Analyzing parts of the target words (e.g., prefixes and
suffixes)

Guessing the meanings of the target words using
contextual cues

Studying concordance examples to see various
contexts and collocation patterns

Writing a short story using the target words

a Principles of Teaching Vocabulary
1. Allocate specific class time to vocabulary teaching.
2. Help students to learn vocabulary in context.
3. Engage in “unplanned” vocabulary teaching.

4, Encourage students to develop word-learning strategies.

![p.118](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0118.jpg)

2 POINTS

02

Read the passage in <A> and a teacher’s note in
<B>, and follow the directions.

Curriculum design is a series of systematic efforts to
develop a curriculum that satisfies the target learners as
well as teachers. Researchers suggest that there are five
main stages in the process of designing a curriculum.

u
Goal Specifications
u
Materials Development
u
Language Teaching & Learning
u

Curriculum Evaluation

Teacher’s Note

I am planning to develop a new English course for
winter session, so I wanted to establish the basis for
developing the curriculum. The first step of this process

requires me to systematically collect and analyze areas

of necessity for my students in order to satisfy their
language learning requirements. So, I created a survey
which asked students questions about their English
deficiencies and the difficulties they face in performing

certain language tasks in their current classes. It also
asked them about the methods they enjoy learning

through as well as the types of English skills that they

want to improve. For the second step of this process,
I wanted to get more information about the students’

preferred learning styles and interests, so I referred to

my classroom observation notes to learn about them. I

then asked my school’s principal to show me the results
of their placement tests to gain an understanding of

their levels of linguistic proficiency and background

experience, Furthermore, I interviewed students
both in groups and individually to get more detailed
information. In short, I conducted              by
collecting all these data.

## p.120

![p.120](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0120.jpg)

03

Read the passage in <A> and the conversation
between two teachers in <B>, and follow the
directions.

The way you speak is affected in many ways. For
example, how much attention you are paying to your
speech may be one factor. When you are not paying
much attention to the way you are speaking, your
speech may be more casual. By contrast, if you are
conscious about the way you are speaking, your output
will be less casual. The social position of the person
with whom you are engaging in conversation may also
affect your language output. It is natural to use more
formal language when you speak to someone whose
social position is above yours. The sociolinguistic
concept of solidarity should also be considered. If your
interlocutor comes from the same speech community
or shares a similar social or cultural identity with you,
you will feel connected to him or her, and this will affect

the way you deliver your message. In addition, where

you are affects the formality of your output. When you
are in a formal situation, such as a business meeting,
you naturally use more formal language, and the
opposite is true as well. Lastly, the channel or medium
of language, that is, whether you deliver your message
through speech or writing, can be another critical factor
that affects your speech. All of these things need to be
considered carefully, because they constitute what is
called pragmatic competence which relies very heavily
on conventional, culturally appropriate, and socially

acceptable ways of interacting.

T1: What are you writing?

T2: Oh, this is a recommendation letter for Miri.

T1: I see. She is very active in school activities, so you
must have a lot to write about her.

T2: Yes, she is a good student, but she doesn’t know how
to adapt her conversational style when making a

request.
T1: Hmm... what do you mean by that?
T2: When Miri approached me, she said, “Hi, teacher,

can you write me a recommendation letter?”

T1: Haha... I understand what you mean. Some of my
students also seem to have trouble making their
speech style appropriate to the situation. Miri is just

one example.

120            OPE 기출 SAA | 전공 영어

T2: Exactly! Still, I feel it’s my responsibility to show
them how speech styles differ across various

situations. Hey, why don’t we offer a special lecture

on this topic?

: Definitely! We can invite a guest speaker who can
show the importance of selecting the appropriate
conversational style to match the                 of

the situation.

Note: T= teacher

classifications of speech styles using the criterion of

formality, which subsumes subject matter, audience,

and occasion:

Oratorical

、

used in public speaking before a large audience
carefully planned wording

exaggerated intonation

rhetorical devices

Deliberative

employed in more impromptu contexts in front of
audiences

Consultative

a dialogue formal enough that words are chosen

with some care

 Business transactions, doctor-patient
conversations, teacher-student conferences.

Casual

used in conversation between friends, colleagues,
or sometimes members of a family

Words need not be guarded.

low social barriers

Intimate

、

## p.122

![p.122](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0122.jpg)

04

Read the passage and fill in the blank with the ONE
most appropriate word.

Diphithongs such as [ar] and [av] are vowels that exhibit
a change in quality within a single syllable. This is due
to tongue movement from the initial vowel articulation
toward another. In English, this combinatory sound is

considered one vowel, as it behaves as a single unit. That
is, the words hide [at] and loud [au] are MOHOSYIAbIC, as
are heed [i] and hid [1]. Diphthong vowels are different

from two consecutive monophthongs as in seeing [sim]
and ruin [rum], which are counted as two syllables.

A similar phenomenon is also observed among
consonant sequences. Consider the following examples
where two different consonants occur together at the

end of a word:

(1) a. ninth [ne], warmth [me] b. laughs [fs], twelfth [fe]

c. maps [ps], width [de]      d. match [tf], badge [d3]
When the words in (1) are followed by a word beginning
with a vowel, such as is/are as in (2), the second member
of the consonant sequences in (2a)-(2c) can move to the
next syllable:

(2) @)Leaving on the ninth is fine with me. ({ne] or [n.e])
©) His laughs are heard from down the hall. ([fs] or [f.s])
€)Maps are useful when you travel abroad. ([ps]} or [p.s])
XK A match is found in the box. ([tf] but not {t,J})

In (2a), for example, the second consonant of the
underlined part [ne] forms a new syllable in fast speech.
That is, [6] in ninth is a coda of the syllable, but it can
move to the next syllable and in turn, it becomes the
onset of [612]. However, this resyllabification does not
happen in (2d). That is, (2d) is pronounced [me.tf1z]
and not [met.fiz]. This is because English treats them
differently: the consonant sequences in (2a)-(2c) are

two consonant clusters while the one in (2d) is a single

sound. This class of sounds is indeed inseparable just
like diphthongs, and a member of this class is called a(n)

Note: ‘’ represents a syllable boundary.

Read the passage in <A> and the interaction in <B>,

Different words and phrases can be used to organize
the structure and manage the flow of ongoing
conversations. Language elements of this function
88 such as CONjtiN                  10

88. Conjunctions join

include different ty

words, hedges, and bi
words, phrases, or clauses together. Cataphoric words
refer forward to other words which will be used later in

the conversation. Hedges are words or phrases employed
not to express the truth of a statement categorically, and

back channel cues indicate that one is paying attention

to his or her interlocutor’s speech. As using these types
of language is associated with discourse and strategic
competence, the ability to use them in an effective way
constitutes part of communicative competence.

(Two students are doing a task on finding differences
between each other’s pictures without showing them to
each other.)

$1: Do you see any people in your picture?

$2: [have a man. He is tall.

There is a class of words, such as yet and any, called

does not c-command any. Hence, the sentence is

ungrammatical.

ac Negation

1. Negative Polarity Items

- Words such as any, which normally occur only in negative
statements but are themselves not negative, are called
negative polarity items.

- anybody, anything, anymore, anyone, anywhere...

gc C-command

- C-command (informal):.A node c-commands its sisters
and all the daughters (and granddaughters and great-
granddaughters, etc.) of its sisters.

- C-command (formal): Node A c-commands node B if
every node dominating A also dominates B, and neither
Anor B dominates the other.

ax Ambiguity

- Asentence is ambiguous if it has two or more meanings.
- Sometimes multiple meanings arise because a sentence

has more than one tree structure associated with it.

Read the passage in <A> and the dialogue in <B>,

and follow the directions.

One of the most effective ways of testing a learner’s
pronunciation is to observe and record repeated
errors in a variety of situations. Speech contexts often
change the way a given word is pronounced. Careful
or emphasized speech is usually employed to show
how to pronounce words clearly. In a connected or
conversational speech, words are often contracted and
the pronunciation of a word can change through the
phenomena in (4) and sound rules in (2):

(1) a. Thatis nice >That’s (C6
b. missed *[d], Ms. *[s] (Spiél

Careful Connected
speech speech

a. can’t you      [tj]          [tf
b. because        [a]            -

c. greater            [t]              [r]

d. advantage [nt]                     leletion

It is worth mentioning that spelling often

influences learners’ pronunciation of words. When

fw errors are found in learners’
pronunciation, they can sometimes be critical. For
instance, if the plural form of sea, seas, is pronounced
[sis] rather than [siz], it can be misunderstood as the
word cease [sis] by listeners.

Note: * indicates incorrect pronunciation.

(Two students are talking about what they wrote during a

dictation task without looking at each other’s notes.)

$1: Can you tell me what you got for the second
sentence? I wrote down, ‘Last [wma] (winner) was
colder,’ and it does not make sense to me.

was colder.’ How|@) did youlunderstand it? /d+j/~ [ds]

winner. That is why the sentence did not make sense
to me.

$2: [winta] (winter), [wine] (winter), ... yes, when you

say it quickly, it does sound the same.

First, “palatalization” changes the pronunciation of
identify
(i). Here, the alveolar stop /d/ changes into the palato-
해당 현상에 개입하는 음소와 결과를 모두 언급

alveolar [d3] when immediately followed by palatal /j/.

글: English Consonants

1. Palatalization
- /t, d/ may turn into palato-alveolar affricates when they
are followed by the palatal glide in the following word.
- Thus, we get did you...[did3ju]
ate your dinner [etfja- dina]

2. Tapping (= Flapping)

- Words such as letter, atom, header, and ladder are
pronounced as [lera], [88020], [hero], and [lere],
respectively as a result of flapping(tapping in some
books).

- Tapping converts an alveolar stop to a voiced tap.

~ Intervocalic, when the second syllable is not stressed

Tapping                                             No tapping

atomic [atémik]
metallic [mateélik]
Italian [rteeljan]
autumnal [stamnal]
rhetorical [ratérakel]

atom [#ram]
metal [méral]
Italy [0911]
autumn [20910]
rhetoric [récartk]

- The principle is also valid across word boundaries.

3. /t/ deletion

planner - planter [plene]
canner - canter [keene]
winner - winter [wine]

tenor - tenter [tena]

- /t/is deleted in the second member of these pairs.

PART 이 7/344

+ The voiceless alveolar stop /t/ in an unstressed

syllable is deleted when preceded by /n/.

08

Read the passage and follow the directions.

According to W. Ury and R. Fisher’s best-selling book,

. The authors

purport that by having a good walk-away option (BATNA:
Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement), you protect
yourself from difficult opponents. They advocate walking

away from more powerful opponents so you don’t give

away the store, or at the very least, make a sale that

ot                » > Ury & Fisher! SHOIM Sia BS SPAR
doesn’t “make sense.     나가는 것의 wea of  되었음
this might, make sense,

In 나 바이두 바

맥락       iggle = negotia ate
Telling” a oP on to walk away from the table
and kissing off a sale is a bit cavalier for an expert who’s

never had to make a living by making a quota. [Fe fl

of the characteristics that makes salespeople successful:

irrational optimism in the face of certain defeat.
EB 4l SE=That=2 LHS [ ]
Technically, you might have the ability to abort settling

on a big sale, but you still have a quota to make and a job
to keep.

With that said, however, salespeople are always better
dealers when they’ve already reached their quota. Why?
Because they don’t need the business. Still, they may
indeed walk away from a potential sale, but they always
come back as the situation changes.

As a sales manager, I urged my team to have as many
potential prospects as possible. | encouraged them to
be working on many potential deals, not just because it
would help them make quota, but also because it made
them tougher negotiators. They were all much better at
holding their ground when they didn’t feel desperate to
have a customer’s business.

Here’s an important side note:

At times, I get the opportunity to ask about the skills
taught in the Getting to Yes seminar with people who
attended the workshop in the past. I always ask them,
“What is the thing you most remember about the Ury and
Fisher tenet?” to which I receive the almost universal
response: “The thing I remember most is to make sure
you know your BATNA.”

Teachers can employ a variety of techniques when
teaching reading that will help enhance students’
reading comprehension.  For instance, at the preparation

Schnigqué can be used: Pictures
or photos and titles can be viewed quickly to give the
students an idea of the overall content of the text. While
reading, if students find some words difficult, the teacher
may help them to guess their meanings by looking at
the surrounding words. Also, as for the reading content,
the teacher can employ ¢                        )

the text by reconstructing the ideas or events. After
reading, diverse techniques can be used in order to
check the students’ level of comprehension: sérainbled

134           7 Pl 기출 공략서 | 전공 영어

Many contemporary tourists avoid encountering

reality directly but thrive on pseudo-events in

their tourism experiences thus affecting t6tfisiii
68061     Urs and iMdigendiis pi
one, many tourists prefer to stay in comfortable

accomodations, thereby separating themselves from
the local people and environment. For instance,
sleeping in a hotel filled with the comforts of home
may insulate them from the fact that they are in
a foreign land. In addition, much of the tourism
industry is bolstered by the use of tourist-focused
institutions such as museums and shopping centers.
The needs of the contemporary tourists have

induced entrepreneurs to build tourist attractions
for the sole purpose of entertaining visitors.
This detracts from the colorful local culture and
presents a false view of the indigenous cultures.
The other group affected by modern tourism is
the local population. These people find themselves
learning languages in a contrived way based on the
changing tides of tourist groups solely for marketing
purposes. Furthermore, when curious visitors do
venture outside their cultural bubbles, they enjoy,
albeit intrusively, watching locals doing their daily
tasks, thereby making them the subject of the tourist
gaze. In sum, while tourism is on the rise, the trend

is to maintain a distance from the real environment

rather than to see the locations for their own values,

Handout
Topic sentence: Modern tourists’ demands

A. Effects on tetirism entrepii
* Provide comfortable accommodations

* Create tourist-focused entertainment attractions
B. Effects on local populations
¢ Learn tourists’ languages

* Become the objects of the tourist gaze

글 Strategies for Reading Comprehension

1. Skimming
- Quickly running one’s eyes across the whole text to get
the gist of it
- Predict the purpose of the passage, the main idea, and
some of supporting ideas
[689 You can have students skim the text for 30 seconds
and close their textbook. Ask what they learned.

Killer robots: point toa fear of indestructible, clever,
artificial intelligence-powered computers. Angela

Becerra Vidergar, a scholar at Stanford, suggests that the
fascination with zombies and the undead in media is one
of the creative legacies of the Second World War, when
the Holocaust and the use of the atomic bomb changed
perceptions about humanity’s propensity for mass
destruction. Modern art and society are not yet rid of the
instincts found in the Morgan’s medieval collection.

We’ve come to assume that just about any bug we’re
saddled with — from strep to staph — can be wiped out
with a quick round of antibiotics. But in the U.S. alone,
roughly 2 million people every year get infections that
can’t be treated with antibiGti¢s, and 23,000 of them
die as a result. The bacteria to blame are now present

in every corner of the planet, according to a landmark
report from the World Health Organization (WHO). In
some countries, about 50% of people infected with K.

pneumoniae or E. coli bacteria won’t respond to our

most powerful 해     ties, say global health experts.

That suggests doctors are increasingly running out of
wunlition they rely on to fight these harmful
microbes.

Bacteria have been evolving to resist the drugs

designed to kill them 81140 the first antibiotic was         다소 간략하고 비유적으로 표현된 (])과 (2)가 구체적으로 FAS 의미

였다

farming, prescription drugs and antibacterial soaps has

France used to have the highest rate of antibiotic
prescription in Europe, but a government mandate
helped lower the number of doctor-issued Rxs by 26% in

In Sweden, regulations to phase out preventive use of

antibiotics in agriculture — in which low doses are given

to keep animals healthy and plump — cut sales of the
drugs for farming by 67% since 1986. The Netherlands
and Denmark have also restricted antibiotic use on
livestock.

Progress in the U.S. has been slower. The Food and
Drug Administration recently tried to encourage farmers

to voluntarily reduce antibiotics in farming. The agency

is also taking steps to curb use of antibacterials in some

consumer goods, requiring manufacturers to prove that
antibacterials are better than simple soap and water in
keeping germs at hay,
Before antibiotics, something as minor as a cut or
a sore throat could be a death sentence. While some
in the right
direction, some might say that much more needs to be
done.

two semantically related constituents are separated, as

글 Cross-Categorical Structural Symmetry

- There is broad structural symmetry across categories
insofar as all major categories (e.g. N, V, P, A, Adv)

permit two types of phrasal expansion.

- More particularly, any head lexical category X can
be expanded into an X-bar by the addition of a set of
Complements; and X-bar can be expanded into X-double-

bar by the addition of a Specifier/Determiner.

- Thus, the ‘basic’ structure of Phrases is of the schematic

form below:

Specifier

XP

>

x

14

Read the conversation between two teachers and
follow the directions.

(Two teachers are evaluating two textbooks, Textbook
A and Textbook B, in order to select the one that their
students are going to use next year. This is part of their
conversation.)

T1: So, why don’t we start with the first criterion? I went
with Textbook A.

T2: May I ask you why?

T1:I think that the illustrations and graphics in
Textbook A portray people in the target culture more
realistically.

: Yeah! Textbook A contains very realistic visuals that
can provide our students with cultural information
more accurately.

: Good! Then, what about the second criterion?

: Well, I think Textbook B is the better of the two.
I couldn’t give Textbook A a good score, because
it appears to aim at explicit learning with many
contrived examples of the language.

T1: Hmm... could you clarify your point a bit more?

T2: Well, I mean the texts and dialogues in Textbook A
are 6VErsiniplified.

T1:I had the same impression, but don’t you think

that they may help our students by focusing their
attention on the target features?
: You may be right, but I think that such texts might

deprive them of the opportunities for acquisition

provided by fichtexts.
T1: Oh, I see. That’s a pretty good point.
T2: So, in my opinion, Textbook B can provide more

exposure to language as it is actually used in the 068

world outside the classroom.

Ti: Yeah! From that point of view, Textbook B will be
intrinsically more interesting and motivating to our
students.

T2: I agree. Okay, then, I think we are ready to move on

to the next evaluation criterion.

Note: T=teacher

evaluation

Inappropriate) mss

Adopt/

1. External evaluation
- Examining the organization of the materials as stated
explicitly by the author/publisher

2. Internal evaluation

- Analyzing the extent to which the factors in the
external evaluation stage match up with the internal
consistency and organization of the materials as stated
by the author/publisher

글: Authenticity of Language

1.In teaching listening
- Authentic language and real-world tasks enable
students to see the relevance of classroom activity to

their long-term communicative goals.

2. In teaching speaking

- Encourage the use of authentic language in meaningful
contexts. Even drills can be structured to provide a
sense of authenticity.

3. In teaching reading

- Balance authenticity and readability in choosing texts.
Authentic simple texts can either be devised or located
in the real world.

4.In teaching writing

- Provide as much authentic writing as possible.

- Sharing writing with other students in the class is
one way to add authenticity.

## p.144

![p.144](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0144.jpg)

15

Read the passage and follow the directions.

Some morphemes in English are pronounced
differently depending on their phonetic environments.
These variants of the same morpheme are called
allomorphs. An important question is how we know
which allomorph appears for a given word.

Consider the following examples in (1), where the
negative morphemes il- and ir- are added to a base:

(1) a. il-: egal, illogical, illiterate, illegible

b. ir-: irregular, irrational, irreducible, irrecoverable

Comparing the examples in (1a) with the ones in (1b), a
simple distribution is observed for the two allomorphs
[11] and [11]. That is, [11] and [11] are selectively combined
with their bases conditioned by the initial sound of
the base: when the base begins with /]1/, the prefix il- is

chosen, and when the base begins with /1/, the prefix ir-

is chosen.

There is another case where [1] and [1] alternate
between allomorphs. The adjectival suffix has two
allomorphs: -ar [a1] and -al [al], as shown in (2):

(2) a. -ar: singular, popular, solar, velar

b, -al: rural, plural, viral, moral

These suffixes -ar [ax] and -al [al] are attached to the

base depending on the final consonant of the base. (1)

and (2) are different in where the morpheme is attached:
(1) precedes the base, which is a prefix, and (2) follows
the base, which is a suffix. On the other hand, these
two morphemes are similar in that the allomorphs
for different morphemes show the same alternation
between [1] and [1]. It is interesting to find the two
apparently different phonemes /1/ and /2/ are involved in
the alternation of the allomorphs il-/ir- and -al/-ar.

The two sounds /1/ and /1/ share many phonetic
properties such as voicing, the place of articulation, and
the manner of articulation. They only differ in terms of
the way air passes through the mouth. This characteristic
difference can be made using the distinctive properties
known as the distinctive feature [lateral].

144   메가썸 기출 공략서 | 전공 영어

in (1) and (2) in the correct order. Then, using
the distinctive feature [lateral] (i.e., [+lateral]
or [-lateral]), generalize the distribution of the
allomorphs -al and -ar.

a The Pronunciation of Morphemes

1. Allomorphs of plurals

Allomorph             Environment

[2]                          after voiced nonsibilant segments

[82]                         after sibilant segments2~

- Here, the variant of a morpheme is called allomorph.

gc The Rules of Phonology

1. Assimilation Rules

- The vowel nasalization rule in English is an example
of the assimilation rules that makes neighboring
segments more similar by adding the feature [+nasal]
to the vowel.

2. Dissimilation Rules

- Languages also have feature-changing rules called
dissimilation rules, in which certain segments become
less similar to other segments.
