## p.146

![p.146](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0146.jpg)

16

Read the passage in <A> and the interaction in <B>,
and follow the directions.

When problems in conveying meaning occur in

conversational interactions, interlocutors need to
interrupt the flow and negotiate meaning in order
to overcome communication breakdowns and to
understand what the conversation is about. A negotiation
routine may have a sequence of four components:

A trigger is an utterance that causes communication
difficulty.

An indicator alerts the speaker of the trigger that a
problem exists.

of the trigger attempts to resolve the communication

sis the component through which the speaker

aifficulty:
00088 can tell the speaker of the trigger

whether or not the problem has been resolved.

(The following is a student-student talk occurring in the

morning.)

$1: You didn’t come to the baseball practice yesterday.
What happened?

1. Interaction Hypothesis

- Interactive communication is not merely a component
of language learning, but rather the very basis for L2
development.

- Interaction is the basis of L2 learning. > Learners are
engaged both in enhancing their own communicative
abilities and in socially constructing their identities
through collaboration and negotiation.

- The primary role of the teacher is to optimally scaffold
the learner’s development within a community of

practice.

글: Multiple-choice items

. Multiple-choice items

Receptive or selective response items

The test-taker chooses from a set of responses rather
than creating a response.

Every multiple-choice item has a stem and several
options or alternatives to choose from.

One of those options, the key, is the correct response,
whereas the others serve as distractors(distracters).

- Item Facility(IF)

Item facility (or item difficulty) is the extent to which
an item is easy or difficult for the proposed group of
test-takers.

_ # of students answering the item correctly
Total # of students responding to the item

1                                  ,
2 * total of your two comparison groups                        Sentences containing [be + past participle] can fall into

two subtypes, based on whether the past participle form
ear  tN       is a verb or an adjective, as shown in (1)
2011학년도 2181                                                                                       verb
011학년도                                                        (1) a. The cat was|bitter] by the mouse.
b. He had always been|interested]in Korean history.

adjective

In (1a), bitten is a verb, and in (1b), interested is an
adjective.

There are two syntactic properties that distinguish
adjectives from verbs. First, adjectives can be modified

by                   $s, whereas verbs cannot, as shown in
>
(2).        so, very

으
(2) a. Tcouldn’t stand his veri) offensive behavior.
b. She was    embarrassed by|his demeaning
attitude toward her.           adj.
c.*He (erp sdlved the difficult problem.

d*The ball Ws Gery bounced against the wall
verb

several times.

The fact that offensive and embarrassed in (2a) and (2b)
can be modified by degree modifiers whereas solved and
bounced in (2c) and (2d) cannot suggests that the former
are adjectives and the latter are verbs. For an adjective to
be modified by a degree modifier, it should be gradable.

Such modification is not possible with non-gradable

adjectives. This is illustrated in (3).

(3) a. The meeting was rather serious. gradable adj.
b.*The situations were too impossible. non-gradable adj.

Second, other than be, verbs such as remain can be

@
followed by adjectives, but not by verbs, as given in (4).

(4) a. The visitors remained assembled outside the
museum for over an hour.

b.*The safe remained broken by the burglars.

forms only with more and most and with less and least.

Mr. Lee:

Yuna:

Mr. Lee:

Mr. Lee:

Your presentation was pretty good.

Thank you, Mr. Lee.

Yeah, you were really prepared. And so you got

I tried my best to make my PPT slides as

informative as possible.

I know! They were really impressive. And your
topic was really good.

Thank you! How was my pronunciation?
Overall, I think your language was easy for the
other students to follow. But you may want to
try to use your language more appropriately.

For example, some expressions you used like
you guys and you know, may not be appropriate

in this kind of presentation.

I see. Thank you for your feedback.

cards too frequently without looking at the
audience. ㆍ

I did?

been much better if you had shown more
confidence in your presentation task.

lagree.

1. Advantages
* Commenting on drafts of essays and reports, or an
oral performance
+ Reviewing portfolios
* Responding to journals
* Advising on a student’s plan for a paper or presentation
* Giving feedback on the results of performance on a
test
* Setting learning goals for the near future
- Aconference is a dialogue that is not to be graded.

~ Conferences are by nature formative, not summative.

gic Rubrics

1. Rubric

- Arubric is a device used to evaluate open-ended oral
and written responses of learners.

- Usually composed of a set of criteria or competencies,
each with descriptions of levels of expectation

Advantages                                        Disadvantages
- Ss better focus their effortsand - less exact

produce work of higher quality. - offer Ss a false sense of where
- Ss feel less anxious about                they stand

points for Ss to focus on and

This week, 1 am going to teach names of wild animals,
like ‘ostrich’ and ‘rhinoceros,’ by trying different
activities to address these students’ different learning
styles. Youngmi scored the highest in category A, showing

that she is an ali         learner. So I will let her listen to a

recording and say the names of animals out loud. Minsu’s

high score in category 8 shows that he is a Wi8wal learner.

I will let him look at images of animals and read the
corresponding names. The person who had the highest
score in C was Taeho, who is a taétilé learner. I am going

to use origami so he can use his hands to fold papers
into animal shapes. This will help him learn their names
better. Lastly, Suji’s score in category D shows that she is

a(n) |                    learner. For her, 1 am planning to do

an animal charade activity where she acts like different
animals and others guess the names of them. I think she
will enjoy moving around the classroom. In these ways,

I want to maximize students’ learning outcomes in my

class.

blank in <B> with the ONE most appropriate word.

제라고 할지라도, 임용시험 영어교육학 영역에서 이러한 Learning

다. 긴장된 상황에서 1068801600을 적는 도중, 스펠링을 틀린 응시자

도 꽤 있어서 높은 OHSS 기록했다. 이렇게 AB 1번 문항부터 어렵
게 출제되는 것노 임용시험에서는 전혀 이상한 일이 아니기 때문에 담
이 쉽게 떠오르지 않아도 흔들리지 않고 다음 문제로 넘어가는 것이

필요하다.

02

Read the conversation between two teachers and
follow the directions.

: My students are having trouble with plural nouns. Pm
thinking of trying a new task.

: What’s your idea?

: Pm planning to give short text where every seventh

word is blanked out. Students have to guess the
correct word for each blank to make a complete
sentence.

: Well, that might be a bit difficult for beginning level
students. I did a similar activity last semester. I gave
a text where I blanked out only plural nouns so that

students could focus on them.
: Oh, I see.
: You can also give students only parts of words in the

blanks and ask them to restore each word in the text.

:Hmm, that seems interesting. Well, then, for my
students, I’ll try to use only plural nouns in the
written text and ask my students to fill in the blanks.
Thanks for the suggestion.

Note: T1=teacher 1, T2=teacher 2

2. Deletion

- Fixed-ratio deletion: Every n' word is deleted.

A small group of         will be at the          one

hour before our        start time to put

signs and set up            and chairs.

~ Every 5" word is deleted.
- Rational deletion: choosing deletions according to the
grammatical or discourse functions (> more washback)

A small group          us will be          the venue
one hour            our official start time to put
signs and set            tables            chairs.

~ Conjunctions and prepositions are deleted.

3. Scoring

- Exact word scoring credits Ss only if they insert the
exact word that was originally deleted.
(+) quick scoring, reliability
(-) low face validity

- Appropriate word scoring credits Ss for supplying any
word that is grammatically correct and makes good
sense.
(+) high face validity
(-) less reliable, takes more time to score

4. C-test
- The second half of every other word is deleted and the

test-taker must restore each word.

## p.163

![p.163](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0163.jpg)

- repeats the ill-formed part of the student’s utterance

Repetition                         .                 a          F
- usually with a change in intonation

gi Responses to Feedback

1. Uptake

- Aresponse that (1) immediately follows the teacher’s
feedback and that (2) constitutes a reaction in some
way to the teacher’s intention to draw attention to some
aspect of the student’s initial utterance.
~» Even when the learner’s uptake confirms comprehension,

the production can still not be repaired.

$1: Yesterday, I go to a movie theater.
$2: Uh, sorry, you mean you “went” to see a movie?

577 Ah, yes[laughing], I go to see a movie!

2. Repair

- As a result of teacher feedback, a learner corrects an
ill-formed utterance, either through self-repair or as a
result of peer-repair.
$1: | was in the airport waited for someone.
$2: You mean you were waiting for someone?

S1: Oh yes, Iwas waiting for someone to pick me up.

3. Repetition

- The learner repeats the correct form as a result of
teacher feedback, and sometimes incorporates it into a
longer utterance.
S: I will be studied in school for four years to get degree.
T: Really? Someone will study you?
S: Oh! No, I must study for four years, in the department

of English language and literature.

we Susceptibility of Nasals to Assimilation

1. Labio-dentals
- /m/ and /n/ become labio-dental [m] when they are
followed by a labio-dental sound.
(8) emphasis [ergfasis]
invite [injvart]

2. Total change in place of articulation
 ten pairs [tem perz]
you can go [jukengo]

3. Pro gressive assimilations
- The alveolar nasal /n/ is retroflexed when it occurs
after /r/. (burn, barn)

4. Partial devoicing

- /m/ and /n/ are subject to progressive assimilation in
cases of partial devoicing after the voiceless obstruent
/8/.

Read the passage and fill in the blank with the most

Two different definitions are employed for the tense-
lax distinction. One is the phonetic definition given in (1).

(1) honetic definition

a. A tense vowel has a higher tongue position than its

lax counterpart.
b. A tense vowel has greater duration than its lax
counterpart.

c, A tense vowel requires a greater muscular effort in

production than a lax vowel.

The other is a phonologically defined tense-lax
separation in terms of the different kinds of syllables in
which the vowels can occur.

(2) Ghonological definition

Tense vowels can appear in open syllables with stress

while lax vowels cannot.

The distributionally based phonological classification
of tense-lax comes into conflict with the phonetically
based classification in several respects. First of all, both
/ov/ and /o/ are tense in the phonological classification
while they are separated as tense and lax, respectively,
in the phonetic classification. Second, there is a problem

with regard to aii     fi, which the phonetically based

criterion focuses on. While it is true that several of the

we Tense-lax

1. Phonetic definition

Tense vowels:

- Ahigher tongue position

- Greater duration than its “lax” counterpart

- Require a greater muscular effort in production (hence
the term “tense”)

2. Phonological definition
- Tense vowels can occur in stressed open syllables.
- Tense: /i, 6, 0, 9, 0, U, 81, av, 21/

- Lax: /I, 8, &, 0, A/

Clausal modifiers of GPs which function as the Subject
or the Object can move to the end of the sentence,
which is called ‘extraposition, as shown in (1) and (2),
respectively. The extraposed CP can be adjoined to VP or
TP.

subject
(1) a. [who has red hair] just came in.

b. Aman just came in [who has red hair].
object
(2) a. John won't turn @ friend {who needs help] away.
b. John won’t turn a friend away [who needs help].

Let’s take a closer examination of the extraposition of
the CP from the Object position in (2b). As confirmed in
(3), VP°preposing can be further applied to (2b) and the
resulting sentence is grammatical. This suggests that the
extraposed CP from the Object position is adjoined to VP,

since only phrasal constituents can move.

(3) John said that he wouldn’t turn a friend away who

needs help, and [turn a friend away who needs help]

he wort.

front of the sentence in (4c).

overgeneralization

negative [ interference

880 refers to the effects of the learner’s

previous language knowledge or performance on
subsequent language learning. Transfer can be

categorized into p6                    transfer.

Negative transfer can be further divided into two types-

(Following is a teacher’s reflection on a task for her
Korean students.)

(L1) Korean .안종아해?
A.S, 안좀아해. / 아니, 종아해.
Teacher’s log    (L2) English Q. Don't youlike..?
A. No, | don't like... / Yes, like...

I conducted a task that required students in pairs
to ask and answer questions in class yesterday. At the
beginning of the task, I heard a student asking, “Hon?
you like bananas?” His partner answered, “    I eat
them everyday. They are good for my health.” And
another student said, “Kes, I never eat them. But I like
mangos,” when responding to “Don’t you like oranges?”
I noticed many other students make such errors later in
the course of the task. So I decided to tap into the errors
and explained them to students after the task. I gave
them further question-and-answer exercises to provide
opportunities to practice what I explained before the

class was over.

영어교육학

Second Language Acquisition - Interlanguage - Error Analysis -
Source of Errors

we Transfer and Interference

¢ Positive (+)

Transfer                                       , Overgeneralization
‘          (intralingual)
Negative (-) %

Interference
(interlingual)

1. Transfer

- Transfer usually refers to the carryover of previous
performance or knowledge to subsequent learning.

- Positive Transfer: The prior knowledge benefits the
learning task.

- Negative Transfer: The previous performance disrupts
or inhibits the performance of a second task.

2. Negative transfer

- Interference: Previously learned material conflicts
with subsequent material.

- Overgeneralization: The L2 learner acts within the
target language, generalizing a particular rule or item
in the L2.

I understand that some students have potential
,                ，                                      aptitude test
strengths in learning languages, and in order to

check my students’ aptitude in English, I conducted

a speaking test with picture-cued tasks. For each
task, students looked at pictures and prepared for
_ 1 minute and then described them for 1 minute.

I found that 1 minute was not enough for my
students to prepare their answers, so I felt that I
needed to change the time allocation for the task.
In addition, although my rating and the other
examiner’s rating seemed consistent, I realized
that my approach, providing a global rating with

holistic scoring     ,       ,       .
overall impressions using a single general scale,

was not very effective because the scores didn’t
give much helpful information to students. …
There was one student’s test yielding very different

scores, 80 we (primary and secondary examiners)

had a discussion about the recorded test and found

that I gave the wrong score by mistake. It was good
that we recorded the test even though both of us

were present during the test.

글 Types and Purposes of Tests

1. Achievement Tests

- Related directly to classroom lessons, units, or a total
curriculum

- Determine acquisition of course objectives at the end of
a period of instruction

2. Diagnostic Tests

- Designed to diagnose a particular aspect of a language
that a student needs to develop or that a course should
include

- Pinpoint difficulties of learners

PART O1 기출편

\<

- Specifically tailored to offer information on student
need

3. Placement Tests

- To place a student into an appropriate level or section
of a language curriculum or school

- To find appropriately challenging level for students

4. Proficiency Tests

- Tap global competence ina language

- Not limited to any one course or curriculum

- TOEFL, IELTS

- Key issue: How the constructs of language ability are
specified

5. Aptitude Tests

- Predict a person’s future success

- Measure a person’s capacity or general ability to learn
a foreign language

Scoring methods

1. Holistic Scoring

- The evaluator matches an overall impression with the
descriptors to arrive at a score.
(+) fast evaluation, high inter-rater reliability
() no diagnostic information, raters need to be

extensively trained.

2. Analytic Scoring

- Major elements are scored, enabling learners to hone in
on weaknesses and capitalize on strengths.
(+) more washback
C) low practicality (more time is required)

- Example of scoring writing

Organization                        20
Vocabulary                               20
Syntax                                  25
Mechanics                            5
Total                                     100

## p.179

![p.179](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0179.jpg)

ax Principles for Teaching Writing Skills

1. Balance process and product

- Make sure that Ss are carefully led through appropriate
stages in the process of composing.

- At the same time, don’t get so caught up in the stages
leading up to the final product. — a clear, articulate,
well-organized, effective piece of writing

2. Process approaches to teaching L2 writing

Activities provide background info, stimulate

1. Prewriting           .
interest.

2. First draft           Ss sketch out ideas without much preplanning.

3. Commenting      Peer or T reads first draft and comments.

Ss look at whole essay, use peer/instructor

4, Second draft                           .          .
e                     feedback, rethink, revise.

Learner edits and attends to writing conventions,

5. Third draft               .
*                 rhetoric, grammar, and vocabulary.

6. Postwriting          Ss share finished products.

self, peer, and teacher assessment of the final

7. Evaluation            .
written product

qi Journals

1. Definition
- Alog of one’s thoughts, feelings, reactions, assessments,
ideas, or progress toward goals, usually written with

little attention to structure, form, or correctness.

2. As self-assessment reflections
- Journals can be a stimulus for self-assessment in a
more open-ended way than through checklists and

questionnaires.

영어학
Grammar - Adjectives and Adverbs - Positions of Adjectives

The vast majority of adjectives in English can appear

in both Stt#ibitive and pYedieative positions. Attributive           문제 유형

adjectives modify the head noun in an NP and occur ^의 분류 중 8의 사례에 해당하는 것을 찾고 설명하기

before that head noun (e.g., That elephant has a really

Gig trunk). In contrast, predicative adjectives appear        Teacher’s GrammarS 원전으로 하는 형용사 문제가 출제되었
after a verb, not in an NP, and function as a predicate (e.g.,            다. 형용사의 쓰임에는 NPS] 앞에서 modifyst= 한정적/제한적
That elephant’s trunk is really Gig). However, a number         (attributive) 용법과, 동사 뒤에 등장하여 서술어로 쓰이는 서술적

(predicative) 용법이 있는데, 일부 4-2} attributive-only, S 한정
적 용법으로만 쓰일 수 있다. <8>의 제시문에서 그것을 가려내면 된다.

of adjectives function only as attributive. Listed below

## p.181

![p.181](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0181.jpg)

In <B>, ‘absolute’ and ‘urban’ are attributive-only
identify
adjectives. ‘Absolute’ belongs to (1) Adjectives of
type DHA!
Degree, whereas ‘urban’ belongs to (4) Associative
type DHAI

Adjectives.

w< Positions of Adjectives

1. Attributive position and Predicative position
- Attributive: occuring in an NP
 They chose a young employee for the job.
He gave an interesting presentation.
- Predicative: occuring after a verb
[68 The employee they chose was very young.
His speech was very interesting.
- Many adjectives can occur in both positions.

2. Attributive-only adjectives

- Adjectives of degree (absolute, sheer, total, utter 등)

- Quantifying adjectives (entire, occasional, only 등)

- Adjectives of time and location (future, previous, left,
northern 등)

- Associative adjectives (economic, moral, public 등)

- Certain adjective compounds (big-name, best-selling 등)

3. Predicative-only adjectives

- Adjective with the prefix a- (asleep, awake 등)

- Adjectives taking infinitive or prepositional phrase
complements (devoid, liable 등)

- Adjectives describing medical conditions or health
(faint, ill 등)

The alveolar lateral approximant /1/ presents
appreciable differences among different varieties. In
British English, we find the clear ‘1,’ which is articulated
with the tongue tip in contact with the alveolar ridge,
in words such as like, law, leaf, light, etc. On the other
hand, /l/ is realized as the velarized dark ‘1,’ which has
a quality similar to /u/ with raising of the back of the
tongue toward the velum, in words such as fall, file, belt,
milk, etc. In Welsh English, /1/ is always pronounced as
the clear 1”

In some varieties of American English (AE), however,
the clear ‘’ may hardly be found; most commonly, the
realizations differ in terms of shades of the dark ‘1.’
Thus, a dark ‘Y is found in words given in (1a), a more
velarized darker ‘’ variety in words in (1b), and the
darkest ‘? in words in (1c).

(1)  Realizations of /l/ in some AE varieties

lip, léft, Wash, af 1, ¢, 2, i

may vocalize to [이 as in (2a) and may be deleted as in
(2b).

dark ‘I’: /I/ is followed by front vowels.

darker ‘T: /I/ is followed by back vowels.

vocalization of /1/: /1/ follows a vowel.

= /1/ is in the postvocalic position.

= /\/ is in the coda position.

deletion of /1/: /I/ is followed by labial consonants.

dark‘!
vowels, while darker ‘]’ occurs when /1/ is followed by
darker ‘T
back vowels. Second, /1/ is vocalized when it follows
vocalization
a vowel(it is in the postvocalic/coda position), and

deleted when followed by labial consonants.

¢ American English Vowels

1. Frontness

- Front: /1, i, 6, 86, /
- Central: /a/

- Back: /u, u, 0, 9, a/

2. Height

- High: /t, i, u, of
- Mid: /e, 6, 0, 9/
- Low: /@, A, a/

3. Roundness

- Rounded: /u, 4, 0, 9/
- Unrounded: 나머지

## p.184

![p.184](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0184.jpg)

14

Read the passages and follow the directions.

(Below is a student’s writing and a conversation with his

teacher about the writing.)

Student writing
SUESUEGUGUGEUUSUSEUGE

Someone first showed the bicycle to the public

in the late 18th century. People first thought it was
not safe or comfortable. But many creative people
improved it. So, many people use the bicycle widely as
a form of transportation or for exercise today. Bicycle
makers manufacture lighter, faster and stronger
bicycles now than before. Because of that, more
people ride the bicycle around the world these days
than any time in the past. But they used some unique

types of cycles in the old days like the four-cycle:

Teacher-student one-on-one conference

T: What is this writing about?

S: It’s about the bicycle. Do you ride a bicycle?

T: Yes, I sometimes do. So your writing is not about
people who produce or use the bicycle.

S: That’s right.

T: OK, the main theme is the bicycle. But none of the
sentences has the bicycle as its subject.

S: I know. But if the bicycle becomes the subject, then I
have to use many 80880880088. They are complicated and
difficult. So I tried not to use them. (1)

T: But it would be better to use the bicycle as the subject
in most sentences. That way, it will become clear that
the main focus of your writing is the bicycle.

S: Well, okay. I'll try.

T: You used the word “manufacture.” Did you know this
word?

S: No, I didn’t. At first, I wanted to use “make” but
then the sentence looked a bit awkward because the
subject is “makers.” It would go like “Bicycle makers
make.”

T: 1866,

S: So I looked up a different word in a dictionary that

has the same meaning as “make.” (2)
T: That works. What about this word “four-cycle?” What

do you mean? Are you trying to describe a bicycle but
with four wheels?

184        O7 Hd 기출 공략서 | 전공 영어

S: Yes, I am. 1 added “four” to “cycle” just like “bi” is put

before “cycle” in bicycle. (3)

T: Oh, it is called “quadricycle.” “Quadri” means four

just as “bi” means two.

Note: T= teacher, S=student

When writing as well as speaking in a second language,
learners who have limited command of the second
language may have to use a variety of strategies that
can compensate for their lack of knowledge of the
target language grammar and vocabulary in order
to effectively get their intended meaning or message
across to a reader or listener. Strategies employed for
this purpose include aveidante, code cwitthing, word
|                       ity, and Usingprefabricate
patterns. As these strategies constitute a significant

part of strategic competence, advances in the learners’
ability to effectively use them play a considerable role in
promoting their communicative competence.

Based upon the student’s writing and his dialogue
with the teacher in <A>, identify THREE strategies
the student used from those mentioned in <B>.
Then, provide corresponding evidence for each
identified strategy from <A>.

영어교육학
Second Language Acquisition - Individual Differences -
Strategies - Compensatory Strategies

분석하기

1. Cognitive Strategies

- Help learners construct, transform, and apply L2
knowledge

- Planning, organizing, monitoring, evaluating, using
senses, activating knowledge, contextualization, going
beyond the data

ae

2. Affective Strategies

- Activating supportive emotions
- Minimizing negative emotions
- Generating motivation

- Building positive attitudes

3. Sociocultural-Interactive Strategies
- Interacting to learn

- Overcoming knowledge gaps

- Guessing intelligently

- Generating conversation

- Activating sociocultural schemata

4. Compensatory Strategies

- Avoidance: 본인이 어렵다고 생각하는 주제나 개념, 문법구조,
발음을 회피하는 전략

한 의미 전달하기 (68100281{ 대신 ship)

## p.186

![p.186](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0186.jpg)

15
Read the passage and follow the directions.
<주제> 인간을 화나게 하는 SUE 세 TALS

Inanimate objects are classified scientifically into
three major categories. The goal of all inanimate objects
is to resist man and ultimately to defeat him, and the
three major classifications are based on the method each
object uses to achieve its purpose.

As a general rule, any object capable of breaking down.
유형 © 7|막힌 타이밍에 고장나는 물건
at the moment when it is almost needed will do so. The

automobile is typical of the category. With the cunning
typical of its breed, {He automobil> never breaks down

SS
while entering a filling station. It waits until it reaches a

downtown intersection in the middle of the rush hour.
Thus it creates maximum inconvenience, frustration and
irritability among its human cargo, thereby reducing its
owner’s life span.

Many inanimate objects, of course, find it extremely
difficult to break down. for example, are almost

totally incapable of breaking down. Therefore, they have
had to evolve a different technique for resisting man.
They get lost. Science has still not solved the mystery of
3 @ 사라지는 불건(=잘 고장 나지 않는 유형)

how they do it, and no man has ever caught one of them
in the act of getting lost. The most plausible theory is
that they have developed a secret method of locomotion
which they are able to conceal the instant a human eye
falls upon them.

Scientists have been struck by the fact that things that
break down virtually never get lost, while things that get
lost hardly ever break down. A furnace, for example, will

~ 180 Halx = el
invariably break down at the depth of the first winter

cold wave, but it will never get lost. A woman’s purse,

~ Dx BAO = SO
which after all does have some inherent capacity for
breaking down, hardly ever does. Some persons believe
this constitutes evidence that inanimate objects are not
entirely hostile to man, and that a negotiated peace is
possible.

The third class of objects is the most curious of all. These

include such objects as cigarette lighters and flashlights
.                        예) 라이터, 손전등
It is inaccurate, of course, to say that they never work.

They work once, usually for the first few hours after
being brought home, and then quit. Thereafter, they
never work again. In fact, it is widely assumed that they
are built for the purpose of not working.

오 건

Melatonin, which is famous for inducing sleep, has

turned out to keep our brain in order as well. First, it
works as an antidepressant, alleviating the symptoms
of seasonal affective disorder by realigning the sleep-
wake cycle when used with light therapy. Secondly,
melatonin contributes to slowing down the cognitive
impairment process related to aging by countering the
harmful proteins’ toxic effects. To sum up, melatonin,
with its new effects being studied and found, is
expected to make our lives happier and healthier.

## p.192

![p.192](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0192.jpg)

18

Read the passage in <A> and the two teachers’
reflections in <B>, and follow the directions.

Mr. Kim and Ms. Jo, English teachers, attended a
workshop for language teachers where they both gained
a lot of useful information to promote student learning.
Below is part of the information from the workshop.

Teachers need to...

(1) keep in mind that their course goals and/or

procedures can be modified.
(2) offer students a variety of learning strategies to
develop learner autonomy.

(3) involve students in self-/peer-evaluation instead

of evaluating them alone.
(4) assess students frequently throughout the

semester.

(Below are the two teachers’ reflections after the
workshop.)

Mr. Kim’s reflection

To develop English writing abilities, my students
engaged in writing activities. I simply assumed that
paragraph writing would be enough for my students.
However, J realized that I should change the initial

course goal after assessing my students’ first classroom

writings. Their writing abilities were well above my
expectations so I changed the goal set earlier and
included essays. Since I believe that one-shot assessment
at the end of the course is not effective for enhancing
student learning, I carried out assessment periodically

over the whole course period. I also believe assessment

should be objective and that students’ self-assessments
are rather subjective in some ways. So, I did all the
periodic assessments by myself, not asking students to

evaluate their own work.

192           메가썸 기출 공략서 | 전공 영어

Ms. Jo’s reflection

In my class, students were expected to develop
debating skills in English. I organized my lesson in this

way: brief mini-lectures, short video presentations to

provide content for debating practice, followed by small

group debating practice. | taught a range of learning

strategies so that my students could become independent
language learners utilizing those strategies whenever
needed. For improving students’ oral skills, I thought

that arranging assessments multiple times, not just
once, would be better. So I carried out assessments

every two weeks during my instructional period. Based

on the results of the assessments, I noticed that strictly
following the lesson procedure was rather challenging to
my students. However, I kept the same procedure over

the course period since I believe maintaining consistency

is crucial in order not to confuse students.

Write TWO paragraphs based on <A> and <B>. In
the first paragraph, identify TWO elements from
<A> that Mr. Kim employed in his course and
ONE element that he did not employ, and provide
evidence from <B> for each identified one. In the
second paragraph, identify TWO elements from
<A> that Ms. Jo employed in her course and ONE
element that she did not employ, and provide
evidence from <B> for each identified one.

+ Self and Peer Assessment

1. Theoretical justification

- the principle of autonomy for successful
learning

Self Assessment               .    0      og
- developing intrinsic motivation that comes
from a self-propelled desire to excel
Peer Assessment       - cooperative learning (collaboration in learning)
2. Benefits

- Direct involvement of Ss in their own destiny
- Autonomy
- Increased motivation

3. Drawbacks
- Subjectivity (too harsh / too self-flattering)
- Ss may not be able to discern their own errors.

4. Guidelines

- Tell Ss the purpose of the assessment.

- Define the task(s) clearly.

- Encourage impartial evaluation of performance or
ability.

- Ensure beneficial washback through follow-up tasks.

## p.194

![p.194](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0194.jpg)

a Formative and Summative Assessment

1. Formative Assessment

- Evaluating students in the process of “forming” their
competencies and skills with the goal of helping them
to continue that growth process

2. Summative Assessment

- Aiming to measure or summarize what a student has
grasped

- Typically occurs at the end of a course or unit of
instruction

- Final exams in a course

- General proficiency exams

Read Mr. Lee’s teaching log below and fill in the
blank with the ONE most appropriate word from
the passage above.

SESS ISSUES SUS Uy

Through my teaching experience, I’ve learned
that different students learn in different ways.
Considering the current trend in teaching and
learning, I believe that students should be provided
with more opportunities to be exposed to the

learning condition. Minsu’s case
may illustrate that point. At the beginning of the
semester, Minsu introduced himself as a book
lover. He wanted to read novels in English but was
not sure if he could. I suggested that he didn’t have
to try to comprehend all the details. deed, Minsu

has benefitted a lot from reading novels. He said he

learned many words and expressions even though
he did not make attempts to memorize them. I

will continue observing his progress as his way of

learning is of great interest.

Read the dialogue and follow the directions.

T1: There’s no doubt that young children beginning
school need the basics of reading, writing, and math.

T2: 1 agree, but the big problem is determining the best
way for them to get it. 1 think the classic mode of a
teacher at the chalkboard, and books and homework
is outdated.

: True. That’s why I have been looking at some
teaching literature based on the ideas Jonathan
Bergman and Aaron Sams came up with.

+ What do they suggest?

: Well, they have reconsidered the role of the
traditional classroom and home. So Hémie becomes

: 18800088)
Students view lecture materials, usually in the form

It is well known in English that we get antepenultimate
stress in nouns of at least three syllables when the
penultimate syllable is light: <rule1>

) Site penultimate syllable stressed:

cinema, asterisk, América, Canada, dnimal

When the penultimate syllable ends with atoda, or has a

long vowel or atiphthong!/ stress, however, falls on that

heavy penultimate syllable: <rule 2>

(2) p

a. uténsil, agénda, syndpsis (coda)

b. aréma, horizon, aréna (@,@long vowel, diphthong)

In the above examples in (2a), it is clear that a syllable
boundary seats itself between word-internal consonantal
sequences such as fs] (in utensil), [01 fndj Gin | agenda) and
Eps (in synopsis), since English phoriotael

does not permit such consonantal sequences to occur

as an onset cluster. However, the word-internal
consonantal sequence [st] poses an interesting challenge
for syilabification. Unlike the -ns-, -nd-, or -ps-, the -st-
sequence could be an onset cluster (as in student, stupid)

or a coda cluster (as in list, mist).

- Maximal onset principle: The principle simply assigns
any series of intervocalic consonants to the syllable on
the right as long as it does not violate language specific
onset patterns.

글 Stress and Syllables

1. Stressable syllables

- Heavy syllables attract stress.

- Weight of a syllable is determined by its rhyme structure.

- If the rhyme is non-branching (a short vowel, no coda)
> light syllable (unstressable)

- Ifthe rhyme is branching (a short vowel [except schwa]
+ coda / long vowel or diphthong)
~ heavy syllable (stressable)

2. Ult, Penult, and Ante-penult

Ante-penult                     Penult                              Ult

the syllable before the the syllable before the __ the last syllable
penult                          ult

Zone of Proximal Development

aw  Sociocultural Viewpoints of SLA

1. Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD)

- The distance between learners’ existing developmental
state and their potential development

- Tasks (knowledge or skill) that a learner has not yet
learned but can learn with appropriate stimuli

- Tasks (knowledge or skill) that a child cannot yet do
alone but could do if he is helped by more competent
peers or adults

Cognitive Constructivism                Social Constructivism

\

2. Long’s Interaction Hypothesis

- Redefined comprehensible input & scaffolding as
modified interaction

- Modified interaction: various modifications that are
created to make one’s input comprehensible to learners
e.g.) slowing down, comprehension checks, clarification

request, giving paraphrases

- Optimal environments and tasks for collaboration and
negotiation are needed.

- Acommunity of practice in a socially constructed
process

- Input modification & interaction modification

ge Constructivism

1. Cognitive constructivism

- Learners construct their own representation of
reality.

- Piaget: learning = change, self-generation, construction,
each building on prior learning experiences

- Learners individually discover and transform complex
information when they make their own. ~ Teachers
need to suggest more active role for students.

2. Social constructivism

- Emphasizes social interaction and cooperative learning

- Vygotsky: Children’s thinking and meaning making is
socially constructed and emerges out of their social
interactions.

- Zone of Proximal Development

Euphemism

1. Definition

- Aword or phrase that replaces a taboo word or is used
to avoid frightening or unpleasant subjects

- Polite and endearing names of something

2. Examples

- die — pass on, pass away

- toilet -» powder room

- crippled — handicapped — disabled — challenged —
person with a disability

- brainstorming — thought showers (a local council in
Britain)

## p.209

![p.209](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0209.jpg)

3. Meanings of words

Denotative meaning                         Connotative meaning

- literal meaning of a word             - attitudes, emotions, value
judgements reflected in a word
— Euphemism is created.

wv Language and Society

1. Social Dialects

- Different dialects of a language evolve within social
groups.

- Socioeconomic status, religious, ethnic, and racial
differences, country of origin, gender...

- Standard American English (SAE): prestige dialects

- African American English (AAE)
 r-deletion, multiple negatives, diphthong reduction

2. Lingua Franca

- A language that is commonly agreed to be used for
communication among people who speak different
languages

3. Bilingualism

- The ability to speak two or more languages

- Individual bilingualism (by an individual speaker)

- Societal bilingualism (within a society)

- Code-switching (shifting between languages within a
single sentence or discourse) shows that the grammars

of both languages work simultaneously.

4. Styles and registers

- Situation dialects

- Formal / informal

- Syntactic, phonological, lexical differences in a certain
context

- With family, gang, church, team...

5. Language and sexism

- Marked and unmarked forms

Male (unmarked)                          Female (marked)
heir                                             heiress

hero                                            heroine

major                                          majorette

cop                                                    lady cop

actor                                           actress

interaction.

Mr. Kim designed and implemented a TBLT program
based on the six steps described in <A>.

<I>
Step 1. He did some questionnaire surveys with his

students and interviewed fellow teachers to identify

what his students would really want to do in everyday
life.

Step 2. He grouped the identified real-world tasks (e.g.,
purchasing a train ticket, booking a room, renting a

car) into more general categories (e.g., planning a trip).
<2>
Step 3. He developed tasks that his students would

<3>
perform in the classroom. Those tasks were expected

to elicit communicative language use in the classroom.

Step 4. He designed a syllabus with a central aim of

presenting different grammatical items one at a time

and teaching them separately. + <4> syllabus 32a} OHAl

Step 5. He drew student attention to linguistic forms
when needed, while the primary focus of the lessons

<5>
was still on communication during task performance.

Step 6. He assessed the student outcomes, focusing on

whether’ ghd how much they accomplished each given
task.

글 Task-Based Language Teaching

1. Task

- Meaning is primary.

- Itincludes a problem to solve.

- Itis linked to real-world activities.
- Task completion has some priority.

- An objective can be assessed in terms of an outcome.

2. Target Tasks vs. Pedagogical Tasks

Target Tasks                                      Pedagogical Tasks

- real-world tasks                   - in-class tasks             ,

- uses of language in the world       - simulation of the target tasks
beyond the classroom                ~ include both formal and

- communicative function for          functional techniques
language                          - involve learners in doing

something to achieve a non-
linguistic outcome

exercices in comprehension of
wh-questions with do-insertion

drills in use of frequency adverbs

modeling a typical interview
protocol

 giving personal information
in a job interview

role playing a simulated
interview

3. Characteristics of TBLT

- It points learners beyond the forms of language alone
to real-world contexts.

- It contributes to the communicative goals of learners.

- Elements are carefully designed.

- Specific objectives — You can accurately determine
the success of one task.

- Learners are engaged in genuine problem-solving activity.

4. Principles
- Starts with the development of an inventory of learner

needs (~ an inventory of grammatical, lexical items...)

PART 01 기출편

- Learners develop the ability to communicate through

using the language. (~ memorizing, studying bits of

the linguistic system)

- Learners’ personal experiences are central to the

process.

- Focuses on learning processes and strategies as well

as on language content (not a ‘necessary’ component)

- Linked to learning outside the classroom

- Exposed to authentic listening and reading texts

(authenticity)

w< Needs Analysis

1. Needs

- Learners’ wants, desires, demands, expectations,

motivations, lacks, constraints, and requirements

2. Objective and Subjective Needs

Objective Needs

Subjective Needs

- relatively easily measured and
quantified

- analyzed through test data,
questionnaire results, teacher
reports, interviews...

- proficiency levels, language
skills, what they need to do in
English

- needs seen through the eyes of
the learners themselves

- attitudes toward the language
and culture, expectations of
oneself, preferences, purpose
of learning English

of the schematic form [C TP], and thus contain a C

Read the dialogue and follow the directions.

: Come here, Sumin. How was your vacation?

: Pretty good. Thank you, Ms. Kim. Actually, I’m so
happy to be taking English classes from you this year.

: Good! You’re really welcome in my class. Okay, then,
let’s talk about thetest you had.

: You mean the reading test you gave us in the first
lass? Actually, I was wondering why you gave us a
test instead of going directly into the textbook.

: Right, your class hasn’t had a lesson yet. It was mainly

to see how much you are ready for this semester and

give you individual attention for any strong and weak

points you have.
: Isee. So, how were the results?

: Hmm .... Overall, you did quite well. Especially, you
did well on the grammar questions. But it appears you
had a bit of trouble with some words in the reading
texts.

: Yow’re right. Some words are really hard to memorize
although I keep trying.

: understand. Well, why don’t you try to learn them
through a context particularly relevant to you? That
will be helpful, I believe.

: Thank you for your advice, Ms. Kim.

Note: T=teacher, S= student

Fill in the blank with the ONE most appropriate
word. Then, support your answer with evidence
from the dialogue.

Tests can be categorized according to the purposes for
which they are carried out. In this respect, the test that
Ms. Kim and Sumin are talking about is an example of
a(n)                  test.

and reflect them to the class.

a Types and Purposes of Tests

1. Achievement Tests

- Related directly to classroom lessons, units, or a total
curriculum

- Determine acquisition of course objectives at the end of

a period of instruction

2. Diagnostic Tests

- Designed to diagnose a particular aspect of a language
that a student needs to develop or that a course should
include

PART O1 기출편

(

- Pinpoint difficulties of learners
- Specifically tailored to offer information on student

need

3. Placement Tests
- To place a student into an appropriate level or section
of a language curriculum or school

- To find appropriately challenging level for students

4. Proficiency Tests

- Tap global competence in a language

- Not limited to any one course or curriculum

- TOEFL, IELTS

- Key issue: How the constructs of language ability are
specified

5. Aptitude Tests

- Predict a person’s future success

- Measure a person’s capacity or general ability to learn
a foreign language

6. Comparison

Achievement Tests                            Diagnostic Tests

- elicit information on what
students need to work on in the
future

- more detailed, subcategorized
information about the learner

- how much students have
acquired language features that
have already heen taught

Formative and Summative Assessment

1. Formative Assessment

- Helps students to continue the growth process

- To improve the learner’s language ability

- Delivery by the teacher and internalization by the
students of appropriate feedback on performance is
important

- Toward the future continuation and formation of

learning

2. Summative Assessment

- Aims to measure or summarize what a student has
grasped

- At the end of a course or unit of instruction

- How well that student has accomplished objectives

- Not necessarily related to future progress

- Final exams in a course

The participants in the drinking groups have usually

had one drink daily, while some of the people in the
nondrinking groups have abstained from drinking
due to their possible medical problems. Those
experiments are flawed in design, as they didn’t apply
‘random assignment’ which can lead to true comparison
without being affected by other variables such as the
medical history of the subjects. As they didn’t control

those variables, the experiments are problematic.

글: American English Vowels
- Vowels can be characterized by the position of the
tongue and the lips.

ㅣ            front                  central                back

|
|              1                                                                       uj.           |
ㅣ                                                                                   high

ㅣ
|                e                      a                    0| |

a                                                       a                       |
1

ale
a Tense-lax
1. Phonetic definition

Tense vowels

- Ahigher tongue position

- Greater duration than its “lax” counterpart

- Require a greater muscular effort in production (hence
the term “tense”)

2. Phonological definition
- Tense vowels can occur in stressed open syllables.
- Tense: /i, 6, 0, 9, 0, U, al, av, 217

- Lax: /I, 8, 2, 0, A/

3. Confliction

- Both /o/ and /o/ are tense phonologically, but different in
phonetic definition. (/o/=tense, /9/=lax)

~ Several of the lax vowels /1, 8, 0, A/ are short, but /z/ is

not.

PART O1 기출편

a Glides

- jl, tw]

- Produced with little obstruction of the airstream

- Followed directly by a vowel

- Do not occur at the ends of words

- [j] is a palatal sound.

- [w] is a labiovelar sound. (rounding the lips + raising
the back of the tongue toward velum)

The act of searching for and finding underground
supplies of water using mothing more than a rod] is

commonly known as “dowsing.” Many dowsers in

Germany claim that they respond to “earthrays” that

emanate from water. These saythrays, say the dowsers,
are a subtle form of radiation potentially hazardous to
human health. As a result of these claims, the German
government in the mid-1980s conducted a 2-year

experiment to investigate the possibility that dowsing is

a genuine skill.

A group of university physicists in Munich, Germany,
were provided a grant of 400,000 marks to conduct
the study. Approximately 500 candidate dowsers were

Fill in the blank with the TWO most appropriate
consecutive words from the passage. (Use the SAME
consecutive words for both blanks.) Then, write
the two factors used to determine the underlined

words, “correct guesses.”

라고 했다는 것과 동일한 어구가 들어가는 빈칸 두 개가

recruited to participate in preliminary tests of their
skill. To avoid fraudulent claims, the 43 individuals who
seemed to be the most successful in the preliminary

tests were selected for the final, carefully controlled,
experiment.

The researchers set up a 10-meter-long line on the
ground floor of a vacant barn, along which a small
wagon could be moved. Attached to the wagon was
a short length of pipe, perpendicular to the test line,

that was connected by hoses to a pump with water.

[The                 along the line for each trial of the
experiment] was assigned using a computer-generated
random number. On the upper floor of the barn, directly
above the experimental line, a 10-meter test line was
painted. In each trial, a dowser was admitted to this

upper level and required, with his or her rod, stick, or

other tool of choice, to ascertain where the pipe with

water on the ground floor was located.

Over the 2-year experimental period, the 43 dowsers
participated in a total of 843 tests. The experiment was
{double blind? in that neither the researcher on the top
floor nor the dowser knew the                    , even after
a guess was made.

For each trial, an examination of|the actual pipe’s

location (in decimeters from the beginning of the line)
and the [dowser’s guess] were recorded. The German
physicists from these data concluded in their final report
that although most dowsers did not do particularly well
in the experiments, “some few dowsers, in particular
tests, showed an extraordinarily high rate of &

BulessEs, which can scarcely (if at all)be explained as due
to chance ...

a real core of dowser-phenomena can be
regarded as empirically proven ... .”

어떻게 맞아떨어지는지 설명

ww  Focus on Form

1. Definition

- Leads students to engage in incidental learning by
drawing their attention to target forms while they are
engaged in communicative activities

- Emphasizes the learners’ noticing their grammatical
knowledge, which is necessary for successful target
language use

- Important in Task-based approach

- Focus on Form occurs reactively when it is needed
during or after doing the task

2. Noticing and Consciousness-raising

- Noticing: “The process of the learner picking out
specific features of the target language input which
she or he hears or reads, and paying conscious
attention to them so that they can be fed into the
learning process”(Cullen, 2012)

- Consciousness-raising: helps student notice and pay
attention to the gaps between their utterances and the
correct forms

- Gives corrective feedback with further explanation,
exemplification, and follow up practice as requires

3. Advantages

- More similar to natural language acquisition or
interlanguage development

- Students are not overwhelmed by the grammatical
explanations

- Intrigues more intrinsic motivation

2018 SHAE         227

![p.228](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0228.jpg)

gq Grammar Techniques

1. Input Enhancement
- Highlighting, boldfacing

- Stressing, slowing down, using gestures

2. Input Flood
- Presents texts with a target structure repeatedly

3. Input Processing

- Text used for input remains reasonably natural.

- Learners make the necessary connections between
form and function in authentic context of L2 Use.

As children, many of us were taken to[museumg, In
most cases this was probably with a group of fellow
students from our school on a field trip. We were there to
learn. The displays were static and the importance of the
so-called great works escaped many in attendance. As a
result, many adults rarely revisited museums. Museums

were only seen as cultural repositories. In the last few
decades, however, they Have char              poseai
Throughout human history, museums collected the

extraordinary as evidence of the past. More recently,
they have reevaluated the purpose of their collections

material which best represents the lives of most people.
Such a change in their collections enables museums to
show their relevance to people who previously were

underrepresented, and thus uninterested in museums.
Museums have started to play anew ro ain Qociety
through their Partnerships, as well. It is no longer an
option for a museum to remain isolated and aloof.
Museums are social constructs and have assumed their
place in mainstream contemporary life. They are now
king their value to all sectors of society, not just
with traditional allies like the education sector.

associations and usinesS and Communitp sectors are

now included. @ partner ships/networking

In these ways, the institutions that once were just
hallowed halls of important objects are now quickly
adapting with new attitudes towards what they collect.
They also have evolved to interact and work with a
variety of members within their communities. 68608

모범탑안

Beyond their reputations as mere culture repositories,
museums have been changing their purpose and role
recently. First, they started to collect and display the
ordinary collections of common people, sheding new
lights on the ordinary lives of people. Second, they try
to permeate wider aspects of our lives by building
partnerships with educational, political, and business

associations. With these changes, museums are

becoming an essential part of contemporary cultures.

* Objective: Get class Gen          {tiguage:

impeding communication of meaning.

(Below is Ms. Shin’s talk at the beginning and closure of

her single-activity class.)

© functions
Today, you are going to practice how to make requests

using the question forms you learned from the last class.
To do this, you will be doing an activity in pairs where
you need to fill in a book order form by asking your
partner for the necessary information. While doing this,
you will get a chance to use the question forms to make

@ communication strategies
requests. If you can’t come up with the exact words to

express the meaning you intend during the activity, you

can try using similar words you know or even gestures,

instead. Now, I will hand out the copies of the order
form. Then, you can begin the activity with the student
next to you. You’ll work in pairs. OK, here are your
copies.

w« Categories of Feedback

Types                     Definition & Examples
(implicit) reformulates or expands an ill-formed or

Recast “I break my window yesterday.”

g< Responses to Feedback

1. Uptake

- A response that (1) immediately follows the teacher’s
feedback and that (2) constitutes a reaction in some
way to the teacher’s intention to draw attention to some
aspect of the student’s initial utterance.
~ Even when the learner’s uptake confirms comprehension,

the production can still not be repaired.

$1: Yesterday, I go to a movie theater.
$2: Uh, sorry, you mean you “went” to see a movie?
S1: Ah, yes[laughing], I go to see a movie!

2. Repair

- As a result of teacher feedback, a learner corrects an
ill-formed utterance, either through self-repair or as a
result of peer-repair.
$1: I was in the airport waited for snmeone.
$2: You mean you were waiting for someone?
S1: Oh yes, I was waiting for someone to pick me up.

234            7H 기출 공략서 ㅣ 전공 영어

3. Repetition

- The learner repeats the correct form as a result of
teacher feedback, and sometimes incorporates it into a
longer utterance.
S: I will be studied in school for four years to get degree.
T: Really? Someone will study you?
S: Oh! No, I must study for four years, in the department

of English language and literature.

* essays and compositions in draft & final form
* reports, projects, and outlines for presentations
* audio/video recordings
* notes on lectures
* self and peer assessment
2. Nature & Purpose
- CRADLE
* Collecting
* Reflecting
* Assessing
* Documenting

* Linking

* Evaluating

3. Advantages

- Fosters intrinsic motivation, responsibility
- Promotes student-teacher interaction

- Individualizes learning

- Facilitates critical thinking, self-assessment

4. Things to remember

- Clear purpose & Specific guidelines
- Designated time & location

- Scheduled review & Conferencing

- Positive final assessment

PS Other alternative assessment

1. Journals

- Language learning logs
- Grammar discussions

- Response on readings

- Self-assessment

- Reflections on oneself’s attitudes or feelings

2. Conferences

- Process approach to teaching writing

- Commenting on drafts, reviewing portfolios, advising
on a student’s plan for presentation, setting learning
goals of students

3. Self and Peer Assessment
- Encourages learner’s autonomy
- Direct involvement of students

- Increased motivation

M. Ringelmann, a French agricultural engineer, was          realized when they participated in groups.

one of the first researchers to study the relationship

between process loss and group productivity.

crank faster than four? Instead of speculating about the

The following is part of a lesson procedure that aims to
facilitate students’ comprehension of a text concerning

global warming.

Steps:                                                activate schematic knowledge

1. Before reading the text, T activates Ss’ background

knowledge concerning global warming and provides
other relevant information to help Ss to have a better
comprehension of the text.

T instructs Ss to Peadhetextgquickly in order to
grasp the: mai ideas. In doing so, T tells them not to
read every word.

T asks Ss to Fereaidat quickly for spetifieinfornmation,
such as the type of disasters caused by global

warming.

T instructs Ss to read the text again at their own pace.
T checks Ss’ overall comprehension by having them
write a brief summary of the text.

T then checks Ss’ understanding of the details by

using a cloze activity.

Note: T=teacher, S=student

order that they appear.

글 Strategies for Reading Comprehension

1. Skimming
- Quickly running one’s eyes across the whole text to get
the gist of it
- Predict the purpose of the passage, the main idea, and
some of supporting ideas
(8) You can have students skim the text for 30 seconds
and close their textbook. Ask what they learned.

## p.245

![p.245](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0245.jpg)

2. Scanning
- Quickly searching for some particular information ina
text

t

Extract specific information without reading through

the whole text

Names of dates / definition of a key concept / list of
supporting details

3. Semantic mapping or Clustering

Grouping ideas into meaningful clusters

Provide some order to the chaos when reading
- Can be done individually and in a group

4. Guessing

- Students can guess the meaning of a word, a grammatical
relationship, or a cultural reference, and infer implied
meaning.

- Compensation strategies in which they fill gaps in
competence by intelligent attempts to use whatever

clues are available to them

Po Types of classroom reading performances

1. Intensive and Extensive Reading

Intensive Reading

Extensive Reading

- usually in classroom

- Ss focus on the linguistic or
semantic details of a passage.

- calls Ss’ attention to grammatical
forms, discourse markers, and
other surface structure details

~ literal meaning, implications,
rhetorical relationship

to achieve a general understanding
of a usually longer text

outside class time

pleasure reading

striving for global or general
meaning from longer passages

- stimulating reading for enjoyment
of reading

2. Oral and Silent Reading

Oral Reading

Silent Reading

- Advantages: serves as an
evaluative check on bottom-up
processing skills / pronunciation
check

- Disadvantages: not authentic
language activity / mere
repetition

- essential for learners to gain
speed in process

- for extensive reading, the speed
is important

minimal pairs

글 Minimal Pairs
- Pairs of words or phrases that differ in only one
phonological element and have distinct meanings
~> phoneme, stress, intonation ....
 Contextualized minimal pairs
- This [pen/pan] leaks.
Put the words according to the meaning of the

sentence.

q< Intelligibility

- The extent to which a speaker’s message is actually
understood by a listener (Munro and Derwing, 1995)

- Suprasegmental factors (stress, intonation) affect the
intelligibility of one’s pronunciation a lot. However,
segmental factors also have influence, so teacher should
apply both top-down and bottom-up approach.

## p.247

![p.247](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0247.jpg)

07

Read the passages and follow the directions.

Nonverbal Communication is an important aspect of
intercultural communication. It includes the following
categories, which also apply to cultural norms in public
space. First, there is Kii@8i¢s, which is the use of gestures
or body language. A second category is %         1,

which refers to eye contact and eye movement. Eyes

can provide signals as to one’s mood, such as being
interested, bored, empathetic, or annoyed. Third,
there is PFOXEMIES, which relates to physical distance
between interlocutors (and other people in public
§§ (also called
haptics), meaning touching or making physical contact

spaces). A fourth category is 벼

with someone. Across cultures, norms relating to these
categories can vary significantly, which can lead to
misunderstandings or inappropriate behavior in cross-
cultural situations.

A group of students in Ms. Lee’s school won a regional

English contest and they received an all-expense-
paid trip to Seattle as a reward. In preparation, Ms.
Lee tutored them on how to be polite, which included
lessons comparing cultural norms and non-verbal
communication in Korea and America. However, the
following event occurred.

After arriving in Seattle, they were hungry, so they
asked their shuttle bus driver to stop at the nearest fast
food restaurant, but it was busy and the line was long.
A student, Gyumin, led the group through the line. As
the line moved, so did Gyumin, inching ever so closer

to the front. He was excited-this was his first time in a
restaurant abroad-and he was eager to order his meal.
However, Ms. Lee noticed something recurring. Gyumin
was closely following a middle-aged American man in

line, and as the line moved forward, Ms. Lee saw that
the man frequently turned his head to the side and,
with a scrunched forehead, gazed down at Gyumin for
a moment as if to tell him something. Ms. Lee quietly
pulled Gyumin aside and the following exchange
occurred:

PART O1 기출편

: Gyumin, do you remember what I taught you about

lining up?

: You mean not to bump into anyone? I didn’t!
: No, no, not that. Rather, do you remember |the arm’s

length rule?

: Oh, that!

T: It’s okay. Just remember it for next time. We want to

be polite while we are here.

: Okay. I got it.

Note: T=teacher, S=student

we Validity

- The extent to which inferences made from assessment

results are appropriate, meaningful, and useful in
terms of the purpose of the assessment

- how much the test actually samples the subject

matter about which conclusions are to be drawn

requires the test-taker to perform the behavior

measured

- direct testing: involves the test-taker in actually
performing the target task (« indirect testing)

Content-related
validity

- how much ‘criterion’ of the test has actually been
reached
(1) concurrent validity: how the test results
correspond to that of other existing authorized
tests
(2) predictive validity: how accurately the test
predicts the test-takers’ future performance

Criterion-
related validity

- construct: any theory, hypothesis, or model that
attempts to explain observed phenomena

- how much the test actually taps into the
theoretical construct as it has been defined

- how much a test is based on theoretically proven
sub-skills of the target ability

- issue when validating large-scale standardized
tests of proficiency

Construct
validity

Consequential       - the impact of a test
validity                     = micro-level: washback

- how much a test looks right, appears to measure
the knowledge or abilities it claims to measure

- subject judgement of the examinees

expected formats and well-constructed familiar

tasks, clear directions, tasks related to their

courseworks ...

Face validity

## p.252

![p.252](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0252.jpg)

1 0                                                                                        4 POINTS|

Read the passage and follow the directions.

The early 20" century was not a great time for grocery
shoppers. Sure, industrialization meant that more food
products were available than ever before, and at lower
prices, too. But in the days before the FDA, who knew
what those products were really made of? A bottle of
ketchup might contain dyed pumpkin, ground ginger
might be mixed with bits of tarred rope, and cans labeled
“potted chicken” might include no chicken at all.

All those things happened, and worse. Once the public
became aware of the extent of the problem, the Pure
Food and Drug Act of 1906 was passed. The legislation
laid out standards for food safety: You could no longer
mix poisonous, dirty, or rotten ingredients into a product.

It also stopped outright mislabelling} You could not call

something a particular food if it was not that food.
실제로 함유되지 않은 AES 제품네이빙메쓰는 것 금지                    ;
This was going to hurt the bottom line for

manufacturers of cheap 1        mi food, so they came up
with a way around the new rules. What if you technically
did not say a product was something it was not? What

if you are calling a product that is mostly cornstarch

Puddine, expecting that people will think it is real

pudding? $+ Sa: "pudding" Cid! "puddine"

That strategy worked for a while, A “dist             me”
proviso was inserted into the law that allowed clever
names. In addition to Puddine (mostly cornstarch),
consumers could buy Grape Smack (imitation grape
juice) and Bred Spred (a nearly fruit-free sugar-pectin
mixture in a jam jar). If buyers thought they were getting
pudding, grape juice, and strawberry jam — well, it was
not the companies’ fault. They did not say their products
were those other things.

The courts agreed. In the marvelously titled cases
United States v. 150 Cases of Fruit Puddine, United States
v. 24 %Gallons of Smack, and United States v. 15 Cases of
Bred Spred, the distinctive name proviso let the imitators
off the hook. But the rules got stricter with the 1938 Food,
Drug, and Cosmetic Act, which mandated that products

could still bear fanciful names, but if?

manufacturers started to use so-called ‘distinctive

[ Mr. Song ]

My students often tell me that they feel
overwhelmed by the number of grammatical

structures they have to learn. While thinking
about ways to help students develop grammatical
competence, I decided to teach grammar explicitly

in class. Today I spent most of the class time on

explaining grammatical rules using meta-linguistic

terms. Although some of the students initially
showed some interest in learning about the rules,
many of them got bored, with some dozing off after

ten minutes or so.

a Form-focused Instruction
- Any planned or incidental instruction activity that
is intended to induce learners to pay attention to
linguistic forms
- Includes explicit treatment of rules, noticing and input
enhancement, implicit techniques for structuring input

to learners

1. Focus on FormS

- Teach forms based on structural syllabi

- Teach grammar explicitly by explaining grammar
rules using meta-linguistic forms

- Decontextualized presentation of grammar structure

2. Focus on Form

- Attempt to induce learners’ incidental learning
by drawing their attention to target forms during
communicative activities

- Noticing: the process of the learner picking out specific
features of the target language input / paying conscious
attention to them — learning process

글 Maximal onset principle

- When decoding where the syllable boundaries lie, any
series of intervocalic consonants go to the syllable
on the right as long as it does not violate language-

specific onset patterns.

wv Schwa deletion

- Schwa is deleted when it is preceded by a stressed
syllable and followed by another schwa.

글: Meaning negotiation strategies

1. Comprehension checks (네가 잘 이해했는가?)

- The moves by which one interlocutor seeks to make
sure that the other has understood correctly
(¢.8.] “You know what I mean?” “Do you understand?”

2. Clarification requests

- The moves by which one interlocutor requests
assistance in understanding the other’s preceding
utterance
 “Could you repeat what you said?” “I’m sorry?”

3. Confirmation checks (내가 잘 이해했는가)

- The moves used by one interlocutor to confirm whether
he or she correctly has understood what the other has
said
[6689 “Oh, do you mean that ~?” “You mean ~, right?”

글: Meaning negotiation
- Long’s Interaction Hypothesis: stress the importance of
corrective feedback during interaction — interlocutors
must negotiate for meaning — language development
- Uptake: an interlocutor’s immediate response to his or
her partner’s signal of noncomprehension
> Interlocutor uses a variety of communication
strategies (message abandonment, topic change,
circumlocution, word coinage ...). (2015학년도 ASA Aj
#4 24)

2015학년도 AGA 서술형 2H
2014학년도 ASA 서술형 2번

Read the passages and follow the directions.

The English 했
because there are only two articles: the indefinite article
100닉 and the definite article 19 There are, however,
many situations in which a noun phrase is considered

18 system seems deceptively simple

definite and thus requires the definite article. Described

below are five common uses for the definite article:

(1) [second mention|— when the speaker wants to refer

to something that has already been mentioned,

e.g., She has a brother and a sister. The brother is a
university student. The sister is still in high school.

(2) [situational use|— when the speaker wants to refer to

something specific that is present in the environment

and visible to the listener, e.g., Can you pass me the
salt?

(3) [associative use|— when the speaker can assume that

the listener knows about the relationships that exist

between certain objects and things usually associated

with them, e.g., J bought a book yesterday, and I spoke
to the author today.

(4) [post-modification|— when relative clauses are used

to define or specify something, making it specifically

identifiable and hence definite, e.g., She’s never met
2292
the girl I dated in college.
(5) [generic reference|— when a noun refers generally to
members of a species or class, e.g., The penguin is a
flightless bird.

A taxi was involved in a terrible accident last night.

Luckily, neither nor |the passengerg were

injured.

Identify which of the five uses of the definite
article described in <A> best accounts for the use
of the two definite articles in <B>. Then explain the
reason.

that the listener knows that a taxi must have a driver

a

a

Types of Articles
1. The definite article (the)
2. The indefinite article (a/an)

3. Zero article

- Abstract nouns: beauty, intelligence, education

- Names and professional titles: Harvard University, Tokyo

- Nouns that refer to a custom or an institution: He enjoys
breakfast. / She talks a lot in class.

Definite Articles in Discourse
1. Second mention

2. Immediate presence
 Pass the sugar, please.
Can you answer the phone?

3. Part of a larger context
- Familiar to both the speaker and the hearer
(e.g) I saw you at the library yesterday.
I'll meet you in the lobby.

4. Associative anaphoric use

- The hearer is aware of a basic relationship between
a given object and some other object that has already
mentioned.

5. Generic reference
- A species or class can be made with a definite article,
indefinite article, and zero article.

6. Post-modification

- Relative clause

- Prepositional clause

- Clauses beginning with that that specify anoun
@.g.) The fact that I liked you doesn’t matter.

- Superlative adjectives

- Modified by ordinals
8) 1 want the first flight to LA.

sister. It does not c-command any other nodes.

그래서 (4)만 설명할 수 USS 언급

g< Binding Theory

1. Binding
- A binds B if and only if A c-commands B and A and B
are coindexed.

i)

. Locality constraints

*Julie,; said that herself, was the best performer.

(cf. Julie; said that she; was the best performer.)

~ Although the anaphor ‘herself’ is c-commanded by its
coindexed antecedent ‘Julie,’ it is ungrammatical.

The anaphor needs to find its antecedent in the same
clause. This is called a locality constraint.

The anaphor’s antecedent must be bound in its binding

domain.

ww

. Binding domain
- The clause containing the NP

4. The Binding Principles

Principle A: An anaphor must be bound in its binding

domain.

- Principle B: A pronoun must be free in its binding
domain.

- Principle C: A proper noun must be free and cannot be

bound at all.

## p.265

![p.265](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0265.jpg)

The assumption that multitasking is an efficient
strategy for improving productivity is now being
challenged. Recently, it is found that multitasking
while driving, such as using cell phones, can be fatally
dangerous. Also, multitasking decreases productivity
in the workplace as well as has bad influence on
learning by making it fragmented and less flexible.
Therefore, we should no longer try to do many things

at the same time.

agitation                    불안, 동요
shorthand                속기, 약칭
fragmented                      파편화된

Hu

1. Controller
- Structuring the lesson and carrying out techniques

2. Director
- Keep the process flowing smoothly and efficiently
- Like a conductor of an orchestra / director of a drama

3. Manager
- Plans lesson and allows each individual to be creative
within the lesson

4. Facilitator

- Help students to overcome difficulties and move on

- Allow students to find their own pathways to success,
with your guidance

5. Resource

- The least directive role

- Advice and counsel when the student seeks

Classroom Management

1. Atmosphere / Psychological environment

- Clear teacher and student roles

- Articulate unambiguous objective and goals
- Be flexible / Be fair

- Allow some choices for students

- Positive attitudes & Enthusiasm

2. Physical Environment
- Sights, Sound, Comfort

- Seating arrangement

- Equipment

Providing extra-class assistance for learners

gq Learning Styles

1. Field Sensitivity

- Field: a set of thoughts, ideas, or feelings within which
learners must distinguish specific relevant subsets

- Field independence: the perception of a particular,
relevant item or factor in a “field” of distracting items

- Field dependence: the tendency to be dependent on the
total field so that the parts embedded within the field
are not distracting

2. Impulsive vs. Reflective

- Impulsivity: the tendency to be comfortable making
quick or gambling decisions

- Reflectivity: the tendency to make slower, more
calculated decisions

gic Group Work Techniques

1. Jigsaw

- Aspecial form of information gap

- Each member of a group is given some specific
information.

- The goal is to share all information that each member
has to achieve some objectives.

2. Role-play
- Gives a role to one or more members

- Assigns an objective or purposes

3. Simulations
- Involve a more complex structure               \
- Entire group is working through an imaginary

situation.

4. Projects
- Hands-on approaches
- Purposeful projects
 environmental bulletin board, making videos...

5. Information Gap

- Primary attention to information and not to language
forms

- The necessity of communicative interaction arises to

achieve the objective.

6. Problem solving and Decision making

- Problem solving: centers students’ attention on
meaningful cognitive challenges

- Decision making: one kind of problem solving / ultimate

goal is for students to make a decision

7. Opinion Exchange
- Exchange of various opinions which are related to the
learner’s personal thoughts and experiences

gic Prabw’s Task types
1. Information-gap activity

2. Opinion-gap activity

- Debate, discussion

3. Reasoning-gap activity

- Inference, deductive thinking
- The most interactive activity

dependencies between peaks and codas provide evidence
for the existence of rhyme as a constituent of syllable.
For example, we can see the relationship between /av/
peak and its coda as follows:

(1) town [taufi]
house [808]
rouse [0800]

(2) *[tavt¥i]/*[tavty] bilabial / velar

[01808] labiodental

couch [kauff] palato~alveolar *[kaug] velar

alveolar
sprout [spraut]

loud [lava]

mouth [11800] interdental

The examples in (1) show that the coda following /au/
has to be 225225252) while those in (2) show that it
cannot be [labial] or {dersal] to form a rhyme.

## p.277

![p.277](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0277.jpg)

글: Major Phonetic Classes

1. Noncontinuants and Continuants
- Noncontinuants: stops, affricates

- Continuants: all other consonants, vowels

2. Obstruents and Sonorants
- Obstruents: non-nasal stops, fricatives, affricates
- Sonorants: vowels, nasal stops, liquids, glides

3. Consonantal Sounds
- Consonantal: Consonants, but glides are excluded;
obstruents, nasal stops, liquids
- Subclasses of consonantal sounds
> Labials
[p], Ub], fm], [f], [v], Iw]
~ Coronals
[6], (61, [t, (d], 0], [9], [2], (1, [3], (fl, [43], G1, [가
> Anteriors
(pl, [b], fm], [f], fv], fw, (81, [6], [¢), (al, fn, [9], [2]
> Sibilants
[s], [2], [[], [3], [tf], [03]

4. Syllabic Sounds
- Vowels, liquids, nasals
- Obstruents and glides are never syllabic.

#: + Syllables

1. SyHable Structure
- A monosyllabic word ‘dog{dog]’ has the following

structure.
Syllable
Onset                      Rhyme
         >
Nucleus                      Coda

2. Dependencies between nuclei and codas
(e.g. /av/ nucleus and its relationship with its coda

ie)                                                                             x
