trout [traut]                               *[traup] *[travk]
rouse [ravuz]                              *[ravv]

crowd [kraud]                           *{kraug] *[kraub]

> The coda of /au/ has to be alveolar or interdental; /av/

cannot be followed by labial or velar consonants.

04

Read the passage and fill in the blanks. Write your
answers in the correct order.

English suffixes can be grouped into three different
types when they are added to a root: stfesssbearing,
SEROSS*SHIEING, and &      veuitral. Stress-bearing
suffixes attract the primary stress to themselves as in

(1a). Stress-shifting suffixes move the stress to some
other syllables as in (1b). Stress-neutral suffixes do not
make any difference to the stress of the root as in (1c).
Meanwhile, the suffix -y is classified into two classes.
Noun-forming suffix -y in (2) belongs to        @
suffixes, while adjective-forming suffix -y in (2) belongs
to        @        suffixes.
(1) a. engine-engineer, attest-attestation,
statue-statuesque
b. public-publicity, commerce-commercial,
library-librarian
c, clever-cleverness, consult-consultant,

parent-parenthood

N           A         _ ON           N
(2) simmer-stiimmery, télephone-teléphony,
synonym-synénymy, friimp-frumpy,
N
advisor-advisory, phétograph-photégraphy,

aN         A
vélvet-vélvety

  영어학적 용어를 활용하여 언어 현상에 대한 설명을 완성하기

음운론 기입형이지만 les 일반화하는 형태는 아니었다. 명사에 붙어
서 또 다른 명사를 만드는 접미사 “??와, 명사에 붙어서 형용사를 만드
는 접미사 “??가 해당 단어의 stress patterns 어떻게 바꾸는지와 관
련되었는데, 수많은 접사마다 88688 pattern} 어떤 연관이 있는지 암

knowledge of lexical items

competence            semantics, phonology
- linguistic competence
- ability to connect sentences
Discourse            - form a meaningful whole
competence        - inter-sentential relationships
- spoken conversation & lengthy written texts
Sociolinguistic - follow sociocultural rules of language
competence         - understanding of social context

- ability to use verbal and nonverbal communicative
techniques to compensate for breakdowns in
communication or insufficient competence

guessing

Bachman(1990)

Language competence

A. Organizational competence
1. Grammatical
2. Textual (=discourse)

B. Pragmatic competence
3. Iocutionary
4, Sociolinguistic

Littlewood(2011)

1. Linguistic (=grammatical)
2. Discourse (=textual)
3. Pragmatic (=strategic)

4. Sociolinguistic
5. Sociocultural

PART 01 기출편

Sv
글: Speech acts
- Communication is a series of communicative acts or
speech acts.
- Minimal units of analysis in conversational interaction

- Three different meanings / forces of speech acts

Locutionary            .                        we               .

.                - literal and propositional meaning of an utterance
meaning
Mocutionary      - intended effect that an utterance has on the hearer
meaning                  or reader

.             - actual effect the utterance has on the hearer

Perlocutionary             .

:             - received message
meaning

- consequences of the message

## p.284

![p.284](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0284.jpg)

“4 POINTS.

07

Read the passage in <A> and the sentences in <B>,
and follow the directions.

There are two-kinds of events or situations that
predicates describe in sentences: One is telic and the
other atelic. A télié event is the kind of event that has a

natural finishing point and once it is completed, it cannot

go on any further as shown in (1). In contrast, an atélié
event does not have a natural finishing point and it can

go on and on as shown in (2).

(1) a. They built the barn.

b. They reached the summit.
(2) a. The room was sunny.

b. The choir sang.

One of the tests for telicity is modification of the event
duration by an adverbial led by in or for. Telic predicates
take in adverbials; atelic predicates take for adverbials,
as shown in (3-5) below. In the sentences describing a
telic event in (3-4), in adverbials have either the event

duration interpretation as in (3a) or the event delay

interpretation as in (4a). In the latter interpretation, the
 time which elapses prior to the event is specified by in
adverbials, and the event occurs at the end of the stated
interval. Meanwhile, in the sentences describing an atelic

event as in (5), for adverbials have the event duration

interpretation only.

(3) a. They built the barn in two days. event duration

telic b, #They built the barn for two days.
event
delay

b. #They reached the summit for half an hour.

\w  a. They reached the summit in half an hour.

(5) a. #The room was sunny in an hour.

atelic                                                                       .
b. The room was sunny for an hour. event duration

It is essential to use simple past tense sentences when
we do the above adverbial test; if in adverbials occur

in future tense sentences, they can modify any type of

predicate, including atelic predicates, and produce the

event delay interpretation, as shown in (6a). This in turn
leads to the following; certain unambiguous sentences
with in adverbials may become ambiguous in the future

tense as in (6b).

{         ;         in + atelic:
(6) a. The room will be sunny in an hour.  event delay

b. They will build the barn in two days. ambiguous

Note: # indicates that the sentence is anomalous.

284            OY 기출 공략서 | MS 영어

0) a. John walked to the park.
b. John walked in the park.
00 a. John will arrive at the station in five minutes.

b. John will eat the pizza in five minutes.

c. John will play football in five minutes.

PART O1 기출편

q< Lexical Aspect
- Lexical aspect refers to semantic properties of verbs.
- 기준: duration, end point, change
- Four categories: stative verbs + three dynamic verbs

1. Stative verbs
- Stative verbs describe states or situations (rather than
actions).
- Atelic verbs (no endpoint)
- Continuous, emotional, physical, cognitive
 She owns a large red car. ©
He hates his boss.

2. Dynamic verbs

(1) Activity verbs

- Actions that go on for a potentially indefinite period
of time

- Atelic verbs (no endpoint)

- Constant action (run, swim, walk) / inherent change
(decline, develop, grow)
 He is walking around the park.

This plant is really growing fast.

(2) Achievement verbs
- Actions that occur instantaneously (punctually or
as a change of state)
- Punctual: bounce, faint, hit, kick
- Change of state: find (a solution), cross (the finish
line)
- Telic verbs

(3) Accomplishment verbs
- Actions that are logical in terms of their action
(@-g.)) build a house, paint a picture

- Telic verbs

3. Expressing more than one type
 He ran in the hall. -> activity verb
He ran to the post office. > accomplishment verb

years they’ve revised that figure downward, to 450 ppm.

qe Categories of Feedback

- implicit

Recast               - reformulates an ill-formed or incomplete utterance
- inan unobtrusive way

Clarification       - indicates the hearer has not understood it

request            - attention is drawn to an utterance

Metalinguistic - privides comments, information, or questions
feedback             related to the correctness of students’ utterances

prompts the learner to self-correct

Elicitation                            f        .
more overt in their request for a response

Explicit             - clear indication that the form is incorrect
correction         - provides a corrected form
~             - repeats the ill-formed part of the student’s utterance
Repetition                         -                 aan          {
- usually with a change in intonation

move to a higher level class. But I got 76 and I was so

washback 1            —_

A paragraph in the papers of last week recorded the
unusual action of a gentleman called Smith (or some

such name) who had 6       for reasons of conscience

to be made a justice of the peace. Smith’s case was that

the commission was offered to him as a reward for
political services, and that this was a method of selecting
magistrates of which he did not approve. So he showed
his contempt for the system by refusing an honour which

most people covet, and earned by this such notoriety as

the papers can give. “Portrait of a gentleman who has

wefased something!” He takes his place in (1) the gallery

The subject for essay has frequently been given, “If a

million pounds were left to you, how could you do most
good with it?” Some say they would endow hospitals,
some that they would establish almshouses; there may
even be some who would go as far as to build half a
Dreadnought. But there would be a more decisive way
of doing good than any of these. You might refuse the

million pounds. That would be a shock to the systems

of the comfortable — a blow struck at the great Money
God which would make it totter; a thrust in defence of
pride and freedom such as had not been seen before.
That would be a moral tonic more needed than all the
draughts of your newly endowed hospitals. (2) Willeit

wv Schema theory
- Atext does not by itself carry meaning.
- The reader brings the schemata: information,
knowledge, emotion, experience, and culture.
- Readers bring more information from the brain to the
page than from the page to the brain.

1. Content schemata
- What we know about people, the world, culture, and

the universe

2. Formal schemata
- Our knowledge about language and discourse structure

## p.300

![p.300](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0300.jpg)

15

Read the passage and follow the directions.

“The average Yaleman, Class of ’24*, makes $25,111 a
year.” Time magazine reported.

Well, good for him!

But, come to think of it, what does this improbably
precise and salubrious figure mean? Is it, as it appears to
be, evidence that if you send your boy to Yale you won’t
have to work in your old age and neither will he? Is this
average a mean or is it a median? What kind of sample

is it based on? You could lump one Texas oilman with
two hundred hungry free-lance writers and report their
average income as $25,000-odd a year. The arithmetic is
impeccable, the figure is convincingly precise.

In just such ways is the secret language of statistics,

so appealing in a fact-minded culture, being“used to
sensationalize, inflate, confuse, and over-simplify.

Statistical terms are necessary in reporting the mass
data of social and economic trends, business conditions,
“opinion” polls, this year’s census. But without writers
who use the words with honesty and understanding and
readers who know what they mean, the result can only

be semantic nonsense.

In popular writing on scientific research, the abitised

Statistic is almost (crowding out)the picture of the white-
abused staticO] 2a} 23 수당도 없이 과로하는 노동자의 SSS 보지 못하게 St
jacketed hero laboring overtime without time-and-a-half

in an ill-lit laboratory. Like’

중요한 사실들

Wel) 가려서 실제와 다르게 보이게 Bt
imp                he ain't”

abused statistic / The average Yaleman’s annual

likely that the average cannot represent the overall

tendency.

## p.302

![p.302](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0302.jpg)

16

Read the passage in <A> and examine the teaching

procedures in <B>, and follow the directions.

Language learning can be classified into different
types in various ways in terms of how learners process
linguistic form to acquire rules that govern its use. One
way is to distinguish inductive learning from deductive
learning. This distinction is made by taking into account

how a rule is learned in relation with its specific

instances.

(Below are parts of two teachers’ instruction procedures
for teaching past tense verb forms in hypothetical
conditionals.)

Teacher A’s Class

* T @xplains to Ss that past tense verb forms should
be used in sentences with if clauses to describe
hypothetical situations. T's explicit explanation

* Tasks Ss to complete sentences with appropriate verb

forms to show hypothetical situations. Ss apply rules

11         (can) fly to you, ifI         (be) a superhero.

2. If he          (have) a time machine, he          (will) go back

in time.

Nl tN hl NN Nal ll Nga alot
* Tasks Ss to read a short text with sentences describing
hypothetical situations.

If I had a spaceship, I would fly to Mars. I would also
build my own house there and live forever, if there were
both oxygen and water. Unfortunately, I don’t have lots of
money to buy a spaceship. ...

* T asks Ss to write a paragraph starting with the given

expression.

If I lived on Mars,...

302           OP 기출 공략서 | MS 영어

Teacher B’s Class
* T gives back the written texts about hypothetical
situations Ss produced in the previous class and
provides their reformulated texts T has produced at the
same time. Only incorrect verb forms in Ss’ writings
are changed in T’s reformulation as in the examples

below.

<A student’s original writing>
rf 1060 last year to live over again, T @il) exercise
more and eat less junk food because 1600 be healthier.
T @il) spend more time with my friends and have better
grades, if 169 more active and Gate  less TV....

NN.

<The teacher’s reformulated text>
if t ad) last year to live over again, | Would exercise
wore and eat less junk food because i@ould) be healthier.
| @oul  spend more time with my friends and have
better grades, if t @erd more active and   atche  less

NN el Nl

* T asks Ss to compare T’s reformulated sample with
their writings and to underline all the words in the
sample that are different from those in their writings.

* T asks Ss to find what the underlined words have in

common and in what way they differ from the ones

form.

* T asks Ss to work out the rule that applies to all their
underlined words based on their findings in the

previous step. Ss discover/work out the rule from the data

Note: T=teacher, $=student

Identify the type of learning applied to each class
in <B> based on <A>. Then explain how each class
orients students toward its identified type of
learning with supporting evidence.

- Complements expand N into N-bar.

## p.307

![p.307](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0307.jpg)

Today, in the globalized world, Korea is expanding its

role both in terms of economy and culture. That is, its

electronic and automotive products as well as cultural

products are getting popular among the global

community. In fact, this success is not a coincidence,

given that Korea has been planning for globalization

for a long time, waiting for the right place and time. To

maintain the momentum of its global success, Korea

should keep managing its role in globalization

carefully.

The technique Ms. Song used before consultation is a

장학 이전에 사용한 technique identify
substitution drill. She suggests the sentence “She will

구체적인 문장몰 가지고 SIA substitution drill 과정를 보여줌
go swimming.” first, and then shows a picture of John
riding a bike’ and suggests a verb phrase ‘ride a bike’.
Then, students are asked to make a sentence using
provided verb phrase, which is a simple substitution
of the verb phrase replacing ‘go swimming’ with ‘ride
a bike’.

After the consultation, she made two changes.
First, she changed the types of questions. Before
첫 번째 EASt identify
the consultation, she asked display questions such
as “What will they do?”, which allows only a limited

question FSS 간단한 설명과 함께 비교
set of answers. However, after the consultation, she
used referential questions like “what will you do this
구체적인 BAOS support

vacation?”, which is a real asking of the students’

personal plans. Second, the teacher changed the types
두 번째 Ht identify

of feedback. Before Mr. Cho’s advice, she gave explicit

corrections such as ‘No, you should say, “Tom will

visit a museum”’, which directly gives the alternative

feedback $8 = 1H] Hl

correct form. After the advice, she used repetition as a

corrective feedback in the utterance “I go to Grandma’s
구체적인 예시 덧붙임

house?”, which simply repeats the ill-formed output to

indicate the error. It pushes the student to self-correct

their errors.

## p.317

![p.317](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0317.jpg)

gv Multiple-choice items

1. Multiple-choice items

- Receptive or selective response items

- The test-taker chooses from a set of responses rather
than creating a response.

- Every multiple-choice item has a stem and several
options or alternatives to choose from.

- One of those options, the key, is the correct response,
whereas the others serve as distractors(distracters).

2. Item Facility(IF)

- Item facility (or item difficulty) is the extent to which
an item is easy or difficult for the proposed group of
test-takers.

” Total # of students responding to the item

3. Item Discrimination
- Item discrimination is the extent to which an item

differentiates between high- and low-ability test-takers.

ID= 3 2

er

2009학년도 18번

Read the conversation and follow the directions.

The other day we were talking about the Battle of
Waterloo. And we’ve already talked about the two
main generals in that war. Does anybody remember
who they are? Historical events

: Napoleon and Wellington.
Correct, but don’t forget that Wellington is a title
which he received for his military successes. Born
Arthur Wesley, he became the Duke of Wellington in
1814. He received that title for ending the Peninsular
War by storming what city?

: Toulouse.
That’s right. Shortly after, Napoleon abdicated and
was imprisoned on Elba. And when did the Battle of
Waterloo take place?

: 1815,
Very good. Napoleon escaped Elba and was
attempting to restore his rule. It wasn’t until his
defeat at Waterloo by Wellington that Napoleon’s
reign finally came to an end. Now we’re going to see ...

글 Types of Spoken Language

1. Monologue

- One speaker uses spoken language for any length of
time.
 speeches, lectures, readings, news broadcasts

- Planned / rehearsed / spoken from a written text or
notes
> Little redundancy

- Spontaneous / impromptu / unplanned
> More redundancy

2. Dialogue
- Interpersonal / social / conversational
> Promote social relationships
- Transactional / informational / factual
~ For which the purpose is to convey propositional or

factual information

qx Glottal stop

- In American and British English, glottal stops or the
preglottalized /t/ are commonly found as allophones of
/t/in words such as

Batman / Hitler / atlas / Atlanta
he hit me / eat well / hot water

- The target /t/ should be in a syllable-final position.

- Also observable before syllabic nasals (beaten, kitten)

- The words that do not allow the replacement have their
/t/in the onset position.

PART 01 기출편

a

- Phonemes and Allophones

1. Definition

name /snail/ panther / invite

- The /n/ sounds that are identified as the same are not
phonetically identical.

- name: a voiced alveolar nasal [n]

snail: partially devoiced [n]

panther: dentalized [n]

invite: labiodental [1]

+ 이때 /0/을 phoneme} 하고, 서로 다른 음운환경에서 나
타나는 [0], fn, (nl, [0] 등을 /07의 allophones#}3t 부른다,

2. Overlapping distribution

|      (a) lake-rake (b) mole-more (0) elect-erect      |

The sounds [1] and [r] are capable of occurring in the
same environment.

~ They are in an overlapping distribution.

- When two sounds are found in an overlapping
distribution, and the substitution of one sound for the
other changes the meaning of the word, we say that
they are in contrast, and they are the manifestations of

different phonemes.

3. Complementary distribution

- Two sounds are in complementary distribution if /X/
never appears in any of the phonetic environments in
which /Y/ occurs.

- Two or more sounds are allophones(positional variants) .
of the same phoneme if (a) they are in complementary
distribution, and (b) they are phonetically similar.

## p.323

![p.323](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0323.jpg)

글: Binding Theory

1. Binding
- A binds B if and only if A c-commands B and A and B

are coindexed.

2. Locality Constraints

- *Judy; said that herself, is the best performer.
(cf. Judy; said that she; is the best performer.)
~ Although the anaphor ‘herself’ is c-commanded by its

coindexed antecedent ‘Judy,’ it is ungrammatical.

- The anaphor needs to find its antecedent in the same
clause. This is called a locality constraint.

- The anaphor’s antecedent must be bound in its binding

domain.

3. Binding Domain

- The clause containing the NP

4. The Binding Principles

- Principle A: An anaphor must be bound in its binding
domain.

- Principle B: A pronoun must be free in its binding
domain.

- Principle C: A proper noun must be free and cannot be
bound at all.

integrated language learning for 2nd year middle school

students.

which is summarized below.

wice as many as Japanese mothers

wice as many €@¢ial routiné>of teaching politeness
norms (empathy and greetings). An American mother’s
attern might go like this: “That’s a car. See the car? You
ike it? It’s got nice wheels.” A Japanese mother might
say: “Here! It’s a vroom vroom. I give it to you. Now
give this to me. Yes! Thank you.” American children are
earning that the world is mostly a place with [objects]
apanese children that the world is mostly about
célationships. Relationships usually involve a verb.

English. Asians tend to use an expression like “Drink

more?” rather than “More tea?” when they perceive

here is a need. Americans are noun-oriented| pointing

objects out to their children, naming them, and telling

hem about their attributes. Nouns denote categories.

(2) Students share their own experiences about {noun-

oriented] expressions as opposed to

ones, and discuss different ways of thinking for those

expressions.

(3) Students do Activity 1 in order to learn a variety of
noun-oriented English expressions.

09

Read the passage in <A> and the conversation in
<B>, and follow the directions.

In negotiation of meaning, 5        refers to an

interlocutor’s immediate response to his or her partner’s

signal of noncomprehension. In uptake, the interlocutor

often uses a variety of communication strategies such

as message abandonment, topic change, circumlocution,

word coinage, foreignizing, and code switching.

The following is part of a teacher-student interaction
that contains negotiation of meaning.

Hi, Sangjee. How was your weekend?

Hello. Well, I had a busy weekend.

Did you go anywhere?

No, I stayed home all weekend.

Why were you busy, then?

Thad to fly ten chickens.

Uh, what? What did you do? signal of noncomprehension

Uh, you know, put chickens in oil, very hot oil, kind of

bake them. uptake: immediate response!
Oh, you FRIED them!
Yeah, I fried them with my mother.

‘fry'B SON AS

Why did you have to fry that many chickens?

We had a big party on Sunday. My grandfather’s
birthday. Many people came.

T: Oh, so that’s why you fried so many. The party must
have been a lot of fun.

Note: T=teacher, S=student

Identify where the uptake takes place by writing
the specific utterance from <B>, and select the
strategy used in the uptake from those in <A>. Then
explain how the utterance in the uptake shows the
selected strategy.

## p.327

![p.327](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0327.jpg)

< Responses to Feedback

1. Uptake

- A response that (1) immediately follows the teacher’s
feedback and that (2) constitutes a reaction in some
way to the teacher’s intention to draw attention to some
aspect of the student’s initial utterance
~ Even when the learner’s uptake confirms comprehension,

the production can still not be repaired.

eg)
S1: Yesterday, I go to a movie theater.
$2: Uh, sorry, you mean you “went” to see a movie?

S1: Ah, yes[laughing], I go to see a movie!

2. Repair

- As a result of teacher feedback, a learner corrects an
ill-formed utterance, either through self-repair or as a
result of peer-repair.
8)
$1: I was in the airport waited for someone.
$2: You mean you were waiting for someone?
$1: Oh yes, I was waiting for someone to pick me up..

3. Repetition

- The learner repeats the correct form as a result of
teacher feedback, and sometimes incorporates it into a
longer utterance.
S: will be studied in school for four years to get degree.
T: Really? Someone will study you?
S: Oh! No, I must study for four years, in the department

of English language and literature.

Learner variables - Strategies

1. Cognitive Strategies

- Help learners construct, transform, and apply L2
knowledge

- Planning, organizing, monitoring, evaluating, using
senses, activating knowledge, contextualization, going
beyond the data

2. Affective Strategies

Activating supportive emotions

Minimizing negative emotions

Generating motivation

Building positive attitudes

PART 01 기출편

3. Sociocultural-Interactive Strategies
- Interacting to learn

Overcoming knowledge gaps
- Guessing intelligently
- Generating conversation

- Activating sociocultural schemata

4. Compensatory Strategies

Types                               Definition & Examples

Avoidance                      avoiding a topic, concept, or syntactic/

phonological elements that cause difficulty

Circumlocution              describing an object or idea with a definition

Approximation             using an alternative term that is as similar as

possible to the target item

creating a new word based on a supposed
rule

Word coinage

Nonverbal signs            mime, gestures, facial expressions...

Prefabricated patterns using memorized patterns

Code switching              using L1 and L2
Appeal to authority        asking for help
Keeping the floor           using fillers or hesitation devices to fill

pauses and to gain time to think

Linguistic expressions are often ambiguous, and
Homonymy is one source of ambiguity. Homonyms are
words that have different meanings but are pronounced
the same, and may or may not be spelled the same.
Another source of ambiguity is 888808

€. Sometimes,
homonymy creates even more ambiguity in combination
with different structures.
structure
(1) John admires intelligent professors and students.
(2) They are pitchers from America. homonymy
(3) Mary observed the man at the bank. both

The ambiguity in (1) is created by different structures.
The source of the ambiguity in (2) is homonymy,
whereas (3) is ambiguous due to different structures and
homonymy.

## p.329

![p.329](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0329.jpg)

yw« Ambiguity

- Asentence is ambiguous if it has two or more meanings.

1. Lexical ambiguity
- Sometimes an ambiguity arises because a word has
more than one meaning, as in the following sentence:
 This will make you smart.
The two interpretations of this sentence are due to
the two meanings of smart (‘clever’ and ‘burning

sensation’).

2. Structural ambiguity

- Multiple meanings often arise because a sentence
has more than one tree structure associated with it,
resulting in a structural ambiguity.

- Each tree will correspond to one of the possible
meanings of the sentences.
[6880 She saw the man with the telescope.

- Meaning 1: The seeing is done with the telescope.

Sue

saw    the man _ with the telescope

Meaning 2: The man is holding the telescope.

the

Read the passage and follow the directions.

Scientists made a splash last week when they presented

fa radical new view of DNA, solving a puzzle that has long

gnawed at investigators and shedding light on diseases

such as cancer, heart disease, and Alzheimer’s. Ever
since decoding the human genome, scientists have been
perplexed by the long strands of our DNA that appear
to do nothing. They called the idle double helixes “junk
DNA,” thinking they were nothing but leftovers from ill-
fitting assembly parts, useless bits of this and that, last
season’s models.

The days of junk status are now officially over.
Working for almost 10 years on a collaborative project,
440 scientists from 32 labs across the globe announced
that they have finally figured out Just what the silent
majority of our DNA does: fi

It seems these large branches of the DNA family tree —
formerly called “junk” but now renamed as “dark matter”
— run the factory but don’t actually make anything.

s, the guys with administrative

approval to greenlight a project or stop it cold — in this

case to determine which genes step forward to produce

a protein and which ones remain stalled, waiting for that

second chance. And with a million supervisors for every
23,000 genes, a ratio of about 50 to 1, it appears [middle
Management is well staffed.

Though perhaps a bit humbling to discover that our
DNA is so bureaucratic, the insight is likely to résult in

substantial 1           68, Up to now, therapies have

focused on influencing the behavior of the gene itself—
sometimes successfully but often not.

that genetic mutations, though somewhat understood
for many diseases, have prover difficult to fix. The
realization that genes are surrounded by an entourage of
[promoters and suppressorg expands the list of possible
targets for intervention considerably. In|cystic fibrosig,
for example, we’ve discovered the genetic mutation that

causes disease, but we’ve been unable to repair it. Using
the new approach, researchers might defuse not the
mutant gene itself but one of the bits of DNA responsible

for greenlighting the bad gene’s expression.

above context, and explain why DNA’s “middle

management” could be the key to future cures.

## p.333

![p.333](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0333.jpg)

The underlined words mean that the DNA called ‘dark
matter’ functions as a decider which promotes and
suppresses the activity of certain genes. DNA’s middle
management could be the key to future cures, as it
increases the number of possible target genes to be
fixed to cure a disease.

and British English. However, words such as tuition,
endure, and annuity vary, as shown in (1a) and (1b).

shown in (1b). The same difference is observed after the
underlined consonants /s/ and /z/ for the words in (2a)

Dialectal variation of English Consonants

4
Me

- Words such music, museum, pure, cure, cute have to
have the same two-sounds sequence /mj, pj, kj/ in their
onsets in both American and British English.

- However, the two dialects vary when we examine

words such as tune, nude, dune, news, and lute.

British English                                  American English
without a /j/
tune [tun]
nude [nud]
dune [dun]
with /j/                                           news [nuz]
tune [tjun]                              lute [hut]
nude [njud]
dune [djun]                              with /j/

onion [anjan]
tenure [tenje]
annual [zenjual]
value [veelju]
failure [felja-]
million {muljan]

news [njuz]
lute [Hut]

- In American English, /j/ cannot follow an alveolar
obstruent; it can follow an alveolar sonorant when in
an unstressed syllable.

PART 01 7/244

Major Phonetic Classes

1. Noncontinuants and Continuants
- Noncontinuants: stops, affricates

- Continuants: all other consonants, vowels

2. Obstruents and Sonorants
- Obstruents: non-nasal stops, fricatives, affricates
- Sonorants: vowels, nasal stops, liquids, glides

3. Consonantal Sounds
- Consonantal: Consonants, but glides are excluded;
obstruents, nasal stops, liquids
- Subclasses of consonantal sounds
> Labials
(p], Lb], [ra], [f], Iv], fw]
> Coronals
(61, [6], 띠, [0], 0], [8], (21, Cf, 13], (tf, (31, 1, 미
~ Anteriors
[p], [b], fmf, If], [v], Ew], [6], [6], [t], [a], In), [5], [2]
> Sibilants
[s], [2], Ef], [3], (tf), [43]

4, Syllabic Sounds
- Vowels, liquids, nasals
- Obstruents and glides are never syllabic.

is a carefully type. (i) can be paraphrased as ‘It is rare

that John talks with philosophers.’ and ‘It is rarely
the case that John talks with philosophers.’ However,

swims.’ or “It is slowly the case that the fish swims.’

Adverbs

1.

Adverbs can be divided into the following categories.

Manner adverbs: Manner adverbs describe how the

action expressed by the verb is carried out.

8) erratically, hesitatingly, loudly, methodically,
quickly, quietly, slowly

. Degree adverbs: Degree adverbs describe how much or

the degree to which the verbal action is carried out.
 awfully, completely, extremely, greatly, infinitely,
slightly, thoroughly

. Duration adverbs: Duration adverbs answer the

question How long does the action go on?
(e.g. briefly, momentarily, permanently, temporarily

. Frequency adverbs: Frequency adverbs answer the

uestion How often does the action occur?

as

[-)
vo

always, never, often, sometimes, usually

. Time adverbs: Time adverbs answer the question When

a

id the action occur?

©
oa,

already, earlier, later, now, then

"

i

. Instrumental adverbs: Instrumental adverbs answer

+
>
oO

question By what means was this done?

©
teh

mechanically, mathematically

ta)

lace adverbs: Place adverbs answer the question
Where did the action occur?

[이
oo,

locally, inside, here, outside, there

. Additive adverbs: Additive adverbs show that

something additional was done or something was added
to something else.

(8) exclusively, merely, only, just

. Act-related adverbs: Act-related adverbs provide

background or motive for the action expressed by the
verb.
 deliberately, expressly, knowingly, voluntarily

10. Stance adverbs: Stance adverbs constitute a special

category of act-related adverbs. They express the

speaker or writer’s attitude or judgment about the

content of a clause.

 foolishly, regrettably, conceivably, imaginably,
maybe, possibly

11. Connective adverbs: Adverbs that link sentences

to other sentences (=linking adverbs, discourse
connectors)

 additionally, alternatively, moreover, therefore

## p.339

![p.339](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0339.jpg)

The two major types of products, namely disposable

and durable ones, have the following similarity in
terms of their positive economic effects. First,
disposable products contribute to benefitting the
economic growth continuously. Manufacturing
disposable products requires new designs and ongoing
employment of many workers, as they are used only
for a short term. Likewise, the other type of product,
the durable ones, provide economic advantages. This
is because such products with durability last for a long
time, thereby requiring the secondary industries to
give sipport to repair them or supply parts. Despite
the fact that they would not be sold frequently,
durable products can boost the economy for this
reason.

However, when it comes to their environmental 7
effects, the two different product types behave quite
in different ways. As for the disposable products, they
have negative impact on the environment, as they
often cause a lot of waste in the manufacturing
process. Also, some disposable products such as
plastic bags do not compose easily, harming the
environment. In contrast, durable products can bea
more eco-friendly option. That is, the products are
made for long-term usage, cutting emissions of
pollutants generated during the manufacturing. In
addition, they include less residual waste, thereby

reducing the landfill expansion.

a Vocabulary

decompose                 썩게 하다, 분해시키다

residual                        나머지의, 잔존의

뜨

PART OI 기출편                                                                                                                                                                                          2015 학년도             339

and how I can correct them myself.

![p.341](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0341.jpg)

모범답안                                                                     ( 관련기출                                                               )
First, what the students liked about the lesson is that         20163 UE ASB
학생들이 좀아한점 언급                                                학년도 전공8 논술형
the teacher used the recipes from cooking magazines,        2014학년도 ASB 논술형 2번
2012학년도 15번

which are the real-life and practical material, as a
class material. The reasons for this are as follows:
First of all, the students liked that they could clearly
understand the target expressions and words in the
context of real-life materials. Also, they liked the
feature because the lesson was motivating and fun,
with the use of authentic materials that they can use
beyond the classroom.

However, there is a problem with the lesson,

문제점 언급
regarding the teacher’s correction for the students’
errors. That is, all of the corrections in the lesson
were made by the teacher, preventing the students
from correcting by themselves and making them
embarrassed. Two solutions for this problems can
be suggested. First, the teacher can let the students
correct the errors by themselves. This will enable

rationale 1

the students to think about their errors and become
reflective learners. Second, the teacher can engage the

해결책 2
students in peer-correction, where they can exchange

corrective feedback in pairs. This will help students
rationale 2
feel more comfortable when getting correction.

gic Self and Peer Assessment

1. Advantages

- autonomy

Self Assessment     0   0
- intrinsic motivation

- cooperative learning
Peer Assessment         - learn how to offer useful feedback
- increased motivation

@ Drawbacks : subjectivity of learners

2. Guidelines

- Tell Ss the purpose of the assessment.

- Define the task(s) clearly.

- Encourage impartial evaluation of performance or
ability.

- Ensure beneficial washback through follow-up tasks.

Read the passage and follow the directions.

We are born, each of us, with such self-centeredness

that only the fact of being babies, and therefore cute,
saves 45.       Wingip is largely a matter of growing out
of that condition: we soak in impressions, and as we do
so we dethrone ourselves — or at least most of us do —

from our original position at the center of the universe.

It is like taking off in an airplane: the establishment
of identity requires recognizing how relatively small

we are in the larger scheme of things. Remember how

it felt to have your parents unexpectedly produce a
younger sibling, or abandon you to the tender mercies .
of kindergarten? Or what it was like to enter your first
public or private school? Or as a teacher, to confront
your first classroom filled with sullen, squirmy,
slumbering, solipsistic students? Just as you have
cleared one hurdle, another is set before you. Each event
diminishes your authority at just the moment at which

you think you have become an authority.
If that is what fiattiity means in human relationships
— the arrival at identity by way of relative insignificance

— then I would define Histor      WSCIOUSITESS as the
projection of that maturity through time. We understand

how much has preceded us, and how unimportant we are

in relation to it. We learn our place, and we come to realize
that it is not a large one. Even a superficial acquaintance
with the existence, through millennia of time, of
numberless human beings helps to correct the normal
adolescent inclination to r          worl          if
instead of [relating oneself to the world) As historian
Geoffrey Elton pointed out, “Hist6#Y teaches those
adjustments and insights which help the adolescent to

become adult, surely a worthy service in the education

of youth.”

## p.349

![p.349](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0349.jpg)

w< Transfer and Interference

Positive (+)

Overgeneralization
(intralingual)

Transfer

Negative (-)

SN          interference
(interlingual)

1. Transfer

- Transfer usually refers to the carryover of previous
performance or knowledge to subsequent learning.

- Positive Transfer: The prior knowledge benefits the
learning task.

- Negative Transfer: The previous performance disrupts
or inhibits the performance of a second task.

2. Negative transfer

- Interference: Previously learned material conflicts
with subsequent material.

- Overgeneralization: The L2 learner acts within the
target language, generalizing a particular rule or item
in the L2.

(-8) putting regular past tense ending to all past tense
forms (goed, flied)

gw Overgeneralization
- A form of negative transfer
- Within the language (L2 > L2, L1 > L1)
- The process of generalizing a particular rule or item
in the second language, irrespective of the native
language, beyond conventional rules or boundaries

20198 AE ASA 11번
2009학년도 13} 214

## p.350

![p.350](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0350.jpg)

04

Read the lesson procedure and complete the
objectives by, filling in each blank with TWO words.

Write your answers in the correct order.

Students: 2nd year middle school students

Approximate time: 45 minutes

Lesson |objectives|

Students will be able:

* to describe a daily routine using |correct verb forms

and          [0]          from a sample paragraph
* to revise writing through         2)

on first drafts

Lesson Procedure

1. The teacher asks students what they do when they get
home every day.

2. Students take turns asking and answering questions

about their daily routine in pairs. Students take notes

on each other’s answers.

3. The teacher provides [a sample paragraph] and

students choose the correct expressions.

| (As soon as/Since) Taebin finishes school, he goes
to taekwondo. When he arrives, he puts on his

workout clothes, and (first/then) he practices.
(After/Before) he finishes, he rides his bike home.

글: Reading Strategies
- Skimming: main idea
- Scanning: specific data
- Semantic mapping: clustering, grouping, meaningful
clusters
- Predicting: what will come
- Monitoring: whether predictions were correct
- Inferencing: what’s not explicit

글: Adverbial Relative Clauses

- Introduced by where, when, and why
 That’s the hotel where we stayed.
I don’t know why he likes Judy so much.

wv Free relative clauses

1. Definite free relatives
- Introduced by what, where, and when
 He eats what he orders.
They applauded when he stopped singing.

2. Indefinite free relatives
- Introduced by who(m)ever, whichever, whatever,
wherever, and whenever
 He cats whatever she gives him.
Mary dances with whoever asks her to dance.

Every predicate is associated with an |

structure, which specifies the number of arguments it

requires. The predicate assigns its arguments 0680880
00188 including the following:

* Agent: the instigator of the action

* Theme: the entity affected by the action or state

* Experiencer: |the entity| experiencing the psychological
state

+ Instrument: the means by which the action or event is
carried out

Thematic roles do not have a one-to-one relationship

with grammatical functions such as the subject, the

object, and so on. For example, the argument the ball is

the [object] in (1a) and the [subjecf| in (1b), but it retains

the same thematic role, Theme, in both sentences. Other
(1) a. David kicked theall.

theme

(2) a. 08886 smashed the window. instrument
b. They expected th@shijp to sink.
.                                          theme
c. David opened thi

examples can be seen in (2).

d. Bob cut the tree with @ Saw. instrument

the subordinate clause in (2b) and the object in (2c) have

PART O1 기출편

#< Thematic Roles

Agent               the doer or instigator of the action
Patient              the undergoer of the action or event
Theme               the entity that is moved by the action or event
Experiencer       the living entity that experiences the action or event
Goal                   the location or entity in the direction of which
something moves
Benefactive        the entity that benefits from the action or event
Source                the location or entity from which something moves
the medium by which the action or event is carried
Instrument
out
.               the specification of the place where the action or
Locative                        sae
event is situated
Proposition        the specification of a state of affairs

a Grammatical Functions and Thematic Roles

- No one to one relationships
(a) Ismashed the window.
(b) The window was smashed by me.
(c) A bat smashed the window.
(d) I used a bat to smash the window.
- I, The window, A bat
>A 구조가 다 달라도 각자의 thematic role® 그대로 유지함
- 016018001016은 deep structure]A] 받기 때문
- I: Agent (smashé}+= 주체)
- the window: Patient (smash=]+= 대상)

- a bat: Instrument (smash3}= 도구)

Hews stylé, it is the overwhelming tendency to downplay
the big social, economic, or political picture|in favor of

the human trials, tragedies, and triumphs that sit at the
surface of events] For example, instead of focusing on
power and process, the media concentrate on the

surface
engaged in political combat over the issues. The reasons

for this are numerous, from the that

probing analysis will turn off audiences to the relative

ease of telling the of a story as

they can find a wide range of private, emotional

meanings in it. However, the meanings inspired by

8008 may not add up to the shared critical
understandings on which healthy citizen involvement
thrives. The focus on encourages

péctatary attitude among the public. Whether the focus is

와
on sympathetic heroes and victims or hateful scoundrels
and culprits, the media preference for personalized
 news creates a “cant-s6é-the-forest-
for-fhe7trees” information bias that makes it difficult
to see the big picture that lies beyond the many actors
crowding center stage who are caught in the eye of the
news camera.

ce                                      would be
less worrisome if human-interest angles were used to
hook audiences into more serious analysis of issues
and problems. Almost all and theater,
from the Greek dramas to the modern day, use strong

characters to promote audiencejidentifications and

reactions in order to draw people into thinking about
larger moral and social issues. News often stops at the

character development stage, however, and leaves the

larger lessons and social significance, if there is any, to
the imagination of the audience.  ,

is. What often passes for analysis are opaque news

formulas such as “He/She was a reflection of us,” a line

that was used in the media frenzies that followed the
deaths of Britain’s Princess Diana and America’s John
Kennedy, Jr. Even when large portions of the public

reject persOnatized news formulas, the

never stops. This systematic tendency to personalize

situations is one of the defining biases of news.

68) organized around making
requests has a common overarching sequence of
interactional moves:

* A greeting exchange

* Preliminary moves toward a forthcoming request
* Making the request

* Short negotiation about the request

« Acceptance/Rejection of the request

* Closing/Thanking

(A low-proficiency English learner asks her roommate, a

native speaker of English, to go buy some bread for her.)

Jisu: Hi, Kelly.
Kelly: Hi, Jisu.  ) greeting

Jisu: Buy me bread, OK? - making the request

Kelly: Do you want bread?

Jisu: Yeah.

Kelly: So, there’s no bread in the fridge?

Jisu: J

Kelly: You don’t have bread?

Jisu: No.

Kelly: So, do you want me to go to the supermarket and
get some bread for you?

Jisu: [What was that?

Kelly: Do you want me to get bread for you? | Short

negotiation
Jisu: Yeah.

Kelly: Do you want it right now?

Jisu: Tomorrow morning.

Kelly: OK. ’ll get it for you later tonight. - acceptance
Jisu: OK. Thank you. - closing

Note: * Jisu=low-proficiency learner,

w< Meaning negotiation strategies

1. Comprehension checks (네가 잘 이해했는가?)

- The moves by which one interlocutor seeks to make
sure that the other has understood correctly
(e.g. “You know what I mean?” “Do you understand?”

2. Clarification requests

- The moves by which one interlocutor requests assistance
in understanding the other’s preceding utterance
(€-8) “Could you repeat what you said?” “I’m sorry?”

3. Confirmation checks (내가 잘 이해했는가)

- The moves used by one interlocutor to confirm whether
he or she correctly has understood what the other has
said
(€-8) “Oh, do you mean that ~?” “You mean ~, right?”

## p.368

![p.368](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0368.jpg)

14

Read the passage in <A> and examine the teaching

procedure in <B>. Then follow the directions.

Processing instr    OW, a type of focus-on-form
instruction, is based on the assumption that when
processing input, L2 learners have difficulty in attending
to form and meaning at the same time due to working
memory limitations. Not surprisingly, they tend to give
priority to meaning and tend not to notice details of
form. PROCESSING INStHICHON Mises SEVERAL PHINCIples to
explain what learners attend to in the input and why.

Below are some of these principles.

:           reference Principle: In (1), both -es and
boy convey the same information, ‘the third person
singular’. Yet, learners prefer to focus on
 to arrive at meaning, and often ignore the

grammatical item, -es, while processing the sentence.

(1) The boy studies in the library, not at home.

first noun or pronoun they encounter in a sentence as

iiciplé: Learners tend to process the

the agent of action. For example, they may misinterpret
(2) as “Jack collected the data for the project.”

(2)fack|let[foe| collect the data for the project.
G The first noun

THeEVeNt Possibilities Principle? Event possibilities
refer to the likelihood of one noun being the agent of

action as opposed to another. Since it is more likely in
the real world that a dog would bite a man than the
other way around, learners would likely misinterpret
(3) as “The dog bit the farmer.”

(3) The dog was bitten by the farmer.

In processing instruction, teachers provide students
with strlictiredinputactivities, taking into consideration
the principles above. In a structured input activity,

students are forced to jattend to formjin order to

comprehend a sentence.

368             HI7PA 기춤 공략서 ㅣ 전공 영어

Teaching Procedure

1. Explicit Explanation
Explain how a past tense sentence is constructed in
English. Then inform students of why they tend not to
notice the past tense marker -ed and thus misinterpret

past tense sentences.

Have students read six sentences and decide whether

they describe an activity that was done in the past

or usually happens in the present. Then, check the

answers together.                                    judge = focus on form

Sentences                         Present! Past
(1) They watch€d)television at night.          a
(2) They watch television at night.            디

(3) I walk to school on Mondays.
(4) I walk€d)to school on Mondays.
(5) We play€d)soccer on weekends.
(6) We play soccer on weekends.

Identify the principle in <A> that the teaching
procedure in <B> focuses on. Then explain how
the structured input activity in <B> helps students
correctly process the target form for meaning.

- Form-meaning connection = intake
- Input must be noticed and comprehended to become
intake.
- Input processing: input — intake
- (1) An explicit description ofa rule

processing structure that might bother making
form-meaning connections.
(3) Pushed to process the structure with structured

input activities

글 Structured Input Activities

- Designed for the students to process the target feature
correctly

- Learners are forced to attend to forms to understand
the meaning.

- Focus on meaning while attending to form

- Form-meaning connection

- Associate form with meaning

Read the passage and follow the directions.

The population of a certain species tends to remain
relatively stable over long periods of time. After

IAL
domestic 해 became established on the island of
Tasmania in the early nineteenth century, for instance,
their population varied irregularly between 1,230,000

and 2,250,000 — less than a factor of 2 — over nearly
a century. We know this because sheep were, and
still are, very important to the economy of Tasmania,
and their numbers were carefully recorded. [In sharp
‘contrast, populations of small, short-lived organisms

may fluctuate wildly over many orders of magnitude
id
diatoms that make up the phytoplankton may soar and
crash over periods of a few days or weeks. These rapid

fluctuations overlay changes with longer periods that

occur, for example, on a seasonal basis.

Cells per cm*

Millions

악 2345678910112   악820 1840 1860 1880 1900 1920
Month                        Year
Figure 2

Figure 1

 do sheep and algae show such disparate
fluctuations in population? First of all, sheep and algae

differ in their sensitivity to environmental change.

Because sheep are larger, they have a greater capacity
for homeostasis and better resist the physiological effects
of environmental change. Furthermore, the populations
of sheep and algae are differentially affected by birth

rate fluctuations. Because sheep have a relatively long

lifespan, short-term fluctuations in birth rate do not

greatly affect the overall population at a given time.
Thus, sheep populations possess a high intrinsic stability.

On the other hand, the lives of single-celled algal cells
span only a few days, so these intrinsically unstable

populations turn over rapidly.

Note: homeostasis =the maintenance of a stable equilibrium

Identify the figure that represents the fluctuations
in sheep population and explain why the sheep
population fluctuated the way the figure depicts.
Do NOT copy more than FIVE consecutive words
from the passage.

Korea is quickly becoming an laging society.
Consequently, we need to prepare so that we can
effectively deal with the changes this situation will bring
about. Clearly, these changes will have a wide-ranging
impact on different facets of the nation. We should
consider how we must prepare for the future from both

individual and societal perspectives. Below are some of

the points to consider within these two main areas.

+ Possible pre                  ints at the ini               vel

Develop life-long hobbies
amas healthier lifestyles

Seek financial self-sufficiency

+ Possible preparati            § at the societal level

Re-examine mandatory retirement ages
| mprov the healthcare system

Expand the existing pension system

How must we prepare for the future? Choose one
preparation point that you think is most important
at each level, and write a composition following
the guidelines below.

<Guidelines>
* Write TWO paragraphs, one for each preparation point.

* For each paragraph, include a topic sentence.

* Support each preparation point with two specific reasons.

* Use neat handwriting.

## p.373

![p.373](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0373.jpg)

모범답만

To prepare for the coming of an aging society in
Korea, we could seek financial self-sufficiency at the
individual level. Firstly, as the existing healthcare
and pension system will confront the limit in the
future, we need to ensure that we can be financially
independent. The second reason is that we need some
money to engage in our own hobbies. Having hobbies
can be a helpful solution to the sense of isolation
which is often caused by aging.

At the societal level, we should re-examine
mandatory retirement age. The first reason is that it
can decrease the cost spent for the senior welfare in
the future. Secondly, working itself can give the aged
people the sense of connectedness to society. As a
result, working will boost the confidence of senior
citizens, which will enable them to live happier and
healthier lives.

PART O1 기출편

(1)                                                             (Learning Styles                                             |

Jin-soo is a high school student learning English in        Serres
Korea. For him, studying and learning English does
not feel like a burden. He likes to receive and produce         답만작성
English whenever he can. In addition, he feels that
learning English is important because it will allow him to

converse and be with various international speakers of        |
English.
(2)                                                                    |

Hye-ri is a university student who is learning English

in Korea. She is taking a course in English literature
taught by a Canadian professor. Even though she
sometimes does not understand everything that the
professor says in the lectures, it does not bother her.
During her group study meetings with her classmates,
she feels nervous when she speaks in English but tries to
speak whenever she can. She wants to do well in English
because it is important for her to show her ability to her
family and friends.
(3)                                                                                                                                        ㅣ
Mi-jin is an office worker and she is presently studying
English in Australia for a period of six months. She is
taking an English class at a language institute there. For        |
Mi-jin, learning English is important because she thinks
that it will add to her social status back in Korea. The
high level of challenge presented by the tasks makes her
feel nervous, but this tension pushes her to do well in
them. Concerning the reading activities, she doesn’t like
reading something in English because she feels that she
should always look up unfamiliar words in a dictionary.

Based on the passage above, compare (1) and (2)
in terms of intrinsic or extrinsic motivation, and
contrast (2) and (3) in terms of ambiguity tolerance.

more than FOUR consecutive words.

## p.381

![p.381](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0381.jpg)

02

Read the passages and follow the directions.                    영어교육학

Second Language Acquisition - Individual Differences -
Strategies
There are two types of strategies that a language learner

can use. First, a learner can use learning strategies.
Learning strategies include cognitive, metacognitive,       . 답안작성
social, and affective strategies. Second, a learner can
adopt communicative strategies. Literal translation,
circumlocution, code-switching, and appeal for help are
the examples of communicative strategies.

In today’s English class, Minji and her partner were
asked to read a newspaper article and retell the story to
each other. Their performance was video-recorded. Minji
wrote the following in her learning log after watching
the video.

Minji’s Learning Log

Mistakes and difficulties I had during the task
The newspaper article had a lot of new words that I’ve

never seen before. I was worried if I could accurately
retell the story.

Strategies I used to complete the task

Since I didn’t have time to look up words in the
dictionary, I had to guess their meanings based on the
context. I thought I understood the story. When I didn’t
have enough words to describe it, I simply used Korean
words.

Overall assessment of my performance on today’s task

I paused a lot without speaking while I was telling
the story because I didn’t know what to say in English.
When I was listening, I didn’t understand my partner’s
story clearly. But I didn’t ask her to repeat it because I

wasn’t sure if it was okay.

Strategies I will practice

In the past, I wrote down new words at least ten
times to memorize them. It didn’t work very well, but I
don’t know how else I can remember the words. I will
try to read more so I can learn more new words.

Based on <A>, identify the learning strategy
that Minji’s learning process in <B> belongs
to and explain ONE advantage of the strategy
with evidence from <B>. Then, identify the
communicative strategy Minji used and support
your answer with evidence from <B>.

PARTO2 연습편                                                                                  CHAPTER 1 영어교육학      381

Second Language Acquisition - Individual Differences -

Teacher A is talking about his beliefs and teaching         Affective Factor
practices in English classes and his student, Student A, is          sonnei
reflecting on her experience.

Teacher A: I think it’s important to expose students to
as much English as possible. And it’s also important
to create situations where they use English. And so

I design classroom activities to have students work       |
with understandable English both receptively and
productively. I require students to use English in       |
class. I don’t allow them to talk to other students in
Korean or to look words up in a dictionary. I believe
interactive activities maximize student-student talk
time. During these activities, I prefer to let students
sort out their own problems when they don’t
understand each other. I rarely interrupt or stop to
correct their grammar.

Student A: I really enjoy learning English, but I

sometimes feel frustrated in class. I don’t always
understand what the teacher is saying and sometimes
I wish I could get help in Korean, but the teacher is
so strict about using English in his class. He doesn’t
even let us use a dictionary. Because of that, I often
feel nervous and anxious. I do like the activities where
we talk to other students though. But even then, I
get embarrassed especially when he doesn’t give us
time to prepare. I want to recall some expressions
that I know. And he doesn’t really help us when we’re
having problems talking. I don’t even know if what
I’m saying is correct. I just wish he’d give us more help
instead of just letting us work on our own.

First, state TWO problems of the class based on the
reflections, and explain it with evidence.

Note: T= teacher, S=student

Communication Breakdown: Communication
breakdown indicates the moment when the interlocutors
of a conversation cannot understand one another or
each other well.

Pushed Output: Pushed output is the production of
language learners that is pushed to produce again by a
certain feedback from other interlocutor.

Zone of Proximal Development: The gap between what
a learner can do alone and what a learner can do with
help.

Based on <B>, locate the communication breakdowns
in <A>. Then, explain the conversations in [A]
using the concepts ‘pushed output’ and/or ‘Zone
of Proximal Development’, supporting by the
evidence from <A>.

## p.387

![p.387](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0387.jpg)

Teaching Reading

08

Read the passage and follow the directions.

Below is an excerpt of a reading lesson based on a
three-part framework. The reading passage, about 300
words long, is about a boy’s adventure. The ending of

the story is intentionally omitted by the teacher so that

students can learn how to construct a cohesive and
coherent text.

Lesson Procedure

Pre-reading Activity

* Have Ss watch a 2-minute English video clip related
to the reading passage and then answer questions
about the clip.

* Give Ss half a minute to find previously learned
discourse markers (e.g., however, therefore, as a
result, etc.) in the passage while reading quickly

.답안작성 2 [

through it.

Reading Activity

* Have Ss first read the story by themselves, complete |
the story in pairs by predicting the ending based
on the storyline, and then write it down in about
50 words. While they carry out the task on their
own, T circulates, offering feedback, suggestions, or
language help Ss may need to accomplish the task.

Post-reading Activity

* Have each pair make a presentation about their
version of the ending of the story in English.

* Have Ss vote for the most interesting ending.

Note: T=teacher, Ss=students

Identify TWO strategies students are using and
explain how the strategies help students to learn
how to construct a cohesive and coherent text.

09

Read the passage in <A> and the lesson procedure     | esoimees
in <B>, and follow the directions.

Teaching Reading - Principles / Theories ~ Characteristics of
Written Language

Ms. Kang, a middle school English teacher, had a
consulting last week. The consultant teacher gave some        _답만작성 _
suggestions about teaching reading. Below is the notes
Ms. Kang took when she listened to the suggestions.

<SUGGESTIONS>
(a) Teach reading strategies such as scanning or

skimming.
(b) Give information gap task for post-reading activity |
to promote negotiation of meaning.
(c) Provide vocabulary aids that students can refer to.
(d) Involve students in extensive reading instead of
intensive reading.
(e) Help students to be aware of how coherent texts
are constructed.

Ms. Kang’s Lesson Procedure                                                  |

Step 1

* Show Ss several pictures about environmental
pollution, and then have them talk about why it
happens and what they can do to prevent it.

* Have Ss read the text about pollution quickly, for
about 30 seconds, to find out what the text is about,
without focusing on every word.

Step 2

* Have Ss read the text individually, but now more
carefully.

* Explain that the text is a combination of two shorter
ones.             :

* In groups of three, have Ss divide the text into two
separate ones that make sense.

* Have a member of each group give a presentation
about how and why they divided the text the way
they did.

Step 3

* Show Ss the original versions of the two texts, and
have Ss compare them with their own.

* Have Ss vote for the most accurately restored versions.

Note: Ss=students  |

Identify TWO suggestions in <A> that Ms. Kang
reflects in the lesson procedure in <B>. Then,
explain how Ms. Kang reflects the suggestions with
evidence from <B>. Do NOT copy more than FOUR
consecutive words from <A> and <B>.

(6) The teacher asks the students to read the feedback       |

(7) The teacher gives his/her own feedback to each

(8) The teacher asks the students to post their revised
essays on the blog.

approach of teaching writing. In this approach, drafting
and revising is considered important, so appropriate

## p.390

![p.390](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0390.jpg)

11

Read the passage in <A> and the part of an
interview in <B>, and follow the directions.

How to Utilize Blended Learning in Your Writing
Class Effectively
(a) Use corpus data to help students check their language

use,

(b) Give comments using a video-conferencing program.

(c) Engage students in peer-assessment process online.

(d) Utilize both synchronous and asynchronous
communication.

Below is part of an interview of a high school English
teacher in charge of a special program for teaching on-
and off-line English writing.

: What do you think is the most important role of a
teacher?

: Well, I think the main job of a teacher is to get
students to be able to work on their own. I try to step
back and let my students take more responsibility for
their learning.

: But what if students are having difficulties, say, in
writing?

> Well, when they’re unsure of something, they can run
it through a concordancer so that they can look up
authentic examples of language patterns. They can
also use some of the examples in their writing.

: Do students ever complain that you don’t interact
enough with them?

: But I do interact with them in other ways. For
example, I interact online through discussion forums.
Students upload their writings onto the forum on
a regular basis, then I look over their writings and
comment on the forum. | also give feedback via email.
: What if students want immediate feedback from you?
> Normally I use chat rooms. Also, I give feedback
during regular class time.

: Do you ever use technology for assessment?

: Yes, but it’s pretty basic. We’re hoping to get some
computer testing software that adjusts the questions
depending on students’ performance on previous test
items.

: Thank you for the interview.

Note: 1=interviewer, T=teacher

Identify TWO suggestions in <A> that the teacher
reflected in the lesson procedure in <B>. Then,
support your answer with evidence from <B>.

Read the passage in <A> and the lesson procedure

.                                     “                                             영어교육학

Teaching Writing - Process Approach
Teaching Techniques - Activities & Tasks - Activity Types

Suggestions for an Effective Writing Lesson
A. Provide some input to encourage learner output.                  답안작성 _ _
B. Move from controlled to free activities.                                               :

C. Engage students in a problem-solving activity.
D. Have drafting and revising stages in a recursive way.

Below is an excerpt of an English lesson procedure
developed by a high school teacher.

Step 1. Ask questions relating to the topic.
* Do you talk to your parents a lot? Why or why not?
* Who do you talk to when you have a problem?
Step 2. Provide a reading text and have Ss read it
individually.

Dear Dr. Teen,

Recently, our family moved to a new city. My
husband and I knew this would not be easy for our
teenage daughter, but we thought she would adjust
in time. It has now been two months, and we are
really starting to worry about her. She used to be very
outgoing and talkative. But, now she spends a lot of
time in her room and will not talk to us. She seems
uninterested in school, and her grades are slipping.
Why won’t she communicate with us? What can we
do? Please help!

Worried Mother

Step 3. Ask comprehension-checking questions.

Step 4. In groups of four, have Ss discuss and come up
with the best advice for the worried mother.

Step 5. Have each group write a letter to the worried
mother, using the advice they chose. Each group
exchanges their letter with another group and offers
comments.

Step 6. Have each group revise their own letter, taking
into account the feedback from another group.

Step 7. Let students submit their group’s final drafts.

Step 8. Explain the errors that Ss made frequently during
discussion. Have them engage in pattern practice.

Note: Ss=students

Identify TWO suggestions in <A> that the teacher
did NOT reflect in the lesson procedure in <B>.
Then, support your answer with evidence from
<B>.                                      00000   ee

Teaching Listening                                                                                                                       14-15 |

14

Read Ms. Kim’s activity procedure and follow the
directions.

Ms. Kim’s Activity Procedure

(1) Ask students to listen to a five-minute lecture while
jotting down the main points. Then, give students
four minutes to summarize the lecture using their
notes.

(2) Ask students to listen to the lecture again while they
write down as many additional main points of the
lecture as possible. Then, give students three minutes
to revise their summary using all of their notes.

(3) Ask students to listen to the lecture one last time
while they write down as many other main points

of the lecture as possible. Then, give students two

minutes to make a final revision of their summary.
(4) Ask students to check their grammar before
submitting their summary.

First, identify the teaching technique Ms. Kim
used in the activity. Then, identify whether it uses
extensive or intensive listening, supporting it with
evidence from the passage. Lastly, explain how the
activity develops both receptive and productive
skills of students.

Read the passages and follow the directions.

영어교육학                                                                   {
ㅣ  Teaching Listening - Techniques - Top-down Exercises               |
Teaching Listening - Techniques - Strategies                             J
A high school English teacher’s beliefs on how listening         Nenana enn
should be taught are described as follows:
답안작성

When I teach listening, I want my students to focus
more on trying to infer meaning from contextual
clues rather than the recognition of sounds, words, or
sentences. I believe that the process of listening is more
heavily influenced by world knowledge that a listener
brings to a text, called schematic knowledge, as opposed
to the language items that are available within the text
itself. I advise students to rely on content and formal
schemata when unsure about the speaker’s message. For
more effective listening, I often encourage my students

to use strategies such as predicting and inferencing to get

the meaning.

The following is the procedure of a lesson that she has
tried to design based on her beliefs for listening in <A>.

(a) Before listening, the teacher shows students the title
‘Kyle’s Shopping Trip’ of a DVD clip to be viewed, and
asks students to discuss in their mother tongue the
last time they were out shopping.                                                                                                                                   |

(b) The teacher plays the clip once without the sound,

and asks students to guess what the purpose of the        |
conversation is, what the relationship between the        i
speakers is and so on.
(c) The teacher illustrates how certain words may be        |
linked in natural speech. Then, the teacher plays       |
the clip with the sound on and asks students to find
words that are linked in the speech stream.                                                                                                           i
(d) The teacher replays the clip and asks students to
confirm or reject the prediction that they have made.
(e) The teacher plays the clip again and students listen
attentively for some phrasal verbs to identify specific
details of the conversation.
(f) The teacher summarizes what happened in the
conversation.

Read the passages and follow the directions.

Teaching Procedure

Step 1: The teacher asks students where they went last
summer and what they took on their journey. Then she
writes down the following sentences on the board and
explains the difference in form and meaning between
the two sentences using the terms ‘past tense’ and ‘past
participle (pp)’.

I took a light jacket.
I should have taken a warm jacket.

Step 2: The teacher hands out a story from a magazine
that includes the target structure ‘should + have + pp’.
She asks students to read the story carefully and look
for the examples of ‘should + have + pp’ in the given
text.

Kate travelled across the Australian desert. She made no
preparations. She didn’t take a map, and she didn’t take a cell phone,
Soon after she set off, she got lost and got trapped in a flash flood.
Later, looking back on it, she said, “I should have taken a map. | should
have taken a cell phone...”

gee Nt Nagata atl nel gi tl ca gi git

Step 3: The teacher asks students to think about their
own previous journey and complete the worksheet
below.

Step 4: The teacher asks students to write a story using
the sentences they have produced in the worksheet
above, and to share their writings with the partners.

Tips for Teaching Grammar

. Connect target structure to the Ss’ personal experiences.
. Try not to use metalinguistic terms.

. Promote rule discovery to improve implicit knowledge
of the target structure.

. Have Ss proceduralize the target form by doing an
activity that focuses on meaning.

| Syntax - X-Bar Theory - Constituency and Proforms

The pronouns one and it are used to refer to nominal
expressions. However, they are not only semantically but
also syntactically different. Thus, students may misuse

them unless they understand their usage. This means
that teachers need to have a good knowledge of the
principles governing pronominal usage in order to teach
their students how to use the pronouns correctly. In
relation to their syntactic characteristics, one can replace
an N' (not an N), while it can replace an NP.

: [bought a book in this bookstore yesterday.

: What kind of book did you get?

: It’s a book of poems with a pink cover by Wordsworth.

: Is it interesting?

: Yes, very interesting. Why don’t you buy a poetry
book too?

: Yes, I’d like to. But I’d like to buy a modern (a) one by
a different author.

+ Which (b) one?

: 10 like to get a book of poetry by T. 5. Eliot.

+ How about that (c) one over there?

: Do you mean the (d) one of poetry with a yellow
cover? The book on the top shelf?

: Yes, (e) one with a green-colored spine just next to the
poetry book by Frost.

Syntax - Raising vs. Control - Raising Theory / Control Theory

Compare the two sets of sentences in (1) and (2).

(1) a. John was said to have solved all of the questions.
b. John was told to solve all of the questions.
(2) a. The earthquake was said to have hit the city.
b. *The earthquake was told to hit the city.
(The sentence with an asterisk “’ is unacceptable.)

The two sentences in (1) contain an NP-movement;
an NP moves from the position after the main verb to
the subject position. The subject can be interpreted as
identical with that of to-infinitive. We may assume that
the verbs say and tell take the same type of complement.
This assumption, however, cannot be tenable because of
the contrast shown in (2). The contrast indicates that the
subject in (2a) is not selected by the verb say, while that
in (2b) must be selected by the verb tell.

Here are some more examples whose structure is
identical either with (1a)-type construction or with (1b)-
type construction as discussed in <A>.

(a) The gladiators were supposed to survive brutal
fights.

(b) The passengers were reminded to fasten their seat
belts.

(c) The Amish people are reported to live and dress

very simply.
(d) The company’s workers were found to have      |
organized a union.

(e) The flood victims were advised to prepare food in

hygienic conditions.
The listeners were invited to see the world in a
different perspective.

type construction and (1b)-type construction.

## p.408

![p.408](../references/메가쌤_기출분석_images/page_0408.jpg)

03

Read the passages and follow the directions.

A phrase consists of a head and non-head elements.
Some non-head elements complete the meaning of the        . 다아:
head. These elements are referred to as a complement.
It is a general term to denote any element whose
presence is required by the head. Although the presence
of a complement is normally obligatory, that of an N
can be omitted. There are also elements in a phrase
which describe the head rather than complete it. These
elements are modifiers (or adjuncts). They can be
omitted without affecting grammaticality.

An example of error in popular views (a) about the

mind appears in the idea of a faculty of observation. One
often hears it said that we should train the observation
(b) of our students; and it is imagined that by training
them to observe certain things we are training them to

observe anything and everything. Observation, however,

relies on interest and knowledge. We have no reason to

suppose that a botanist, trained in the observation (c) of

flowers, will be more observant than us (d) of the faces

of the people he meets. People are more likely to have

their attention diverted (e) by the objects of their special

interests. So training in the careful observation of the

varied endings (f) of Latin words, or of the changes in

chemical substances in experiments, will have no effect

on the observation of pictures or the movements of the

stars.

complement PPs’ head element based on <A>.

Syntax - Case Theory

Consider the sentences in (1).

. 탑만작성

a

(4) a. John is sure that he will succeed.
b. John is sure to succeed.
c. *John is sure his success.

The example (1c) is ungrammatical, though its underlined
complement is legitimate. The ungrammaticality of (1c)
is attributable to the following constraint on NPs: NPs
in sentences should have a case either inherently or
through being assigned by finite inflection, a transitive
verb, or a preposition. Sentences with a caseless NP are
ruled out. In (1c) the underlined complement lacks its
case assigner.

(a) *The child is difficult to travel alone.
(b) *The city’s recent dumping the garbage made people
upset.

(c) *I was pleased my sister to be pregnant.

(d) *Not surprisingly did they miss the train.
(e) *It turned out the suspect to be innocent.
(* indicates that the sentence is ungrammatical.)

case.
